arXiv:2604.26292v1 [math.DG] 29 Apr 2026

Brane quantization and SYZ mirror symmetry

Kwokwai Chan, Naichung Conan Leung, Qin Li, Yat-Hin Suen and YuTung Yau Department of Mathematics, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shatin, Hong Kong kwchan@math.cuhk.edu.hk The Institute of Mathematical Sciences and Department of Mathematics, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shatin, Hong Kong leung@math.cuhk.edu.hk Shenzhen Institute for Quantum Science and Engineering, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen, China liqin@sustech.edu.cn Department of Mathematics, National Cheng Kung University yhsuen@gs.ncku.edu.tw Kavli Institute for the Physics and Mathematics of the Universe (WPI), The University of Tokyo Institutes for Advanced Study, The University of Tokyo, Kashiwa, Chiba 277-8583, Japan yu-tung.yau@ipmu.jp
Abstract.

Coisotropic A-branes were introduced by Kapustin–Orlov to enlarge the Fukaya category of a symplectic manifold in a way that aligns with predictions from homological mirror symmetry. From a mathematical perspective, however, the categorical framework governing such branes remains largely undeveloped. On the other hand, Gukov–Witten’s brane quantization suggests that a holomorphic deformation quantization of a holomorphic symplectic manifold XX arises from the endomorphism algebra H​o​mA​(ℬcc,ℬcc)Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}) of a canonical coisotropic A-brane ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}, which naturally acts on the morphism space H​o​mA​(ℬ,ℬcc)Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}) with a Lagrangian A-brane ℬ\mathcal{B} that in turn gives precisely the geometric quantization of ℬ\mathcal{B}.

In this paper, we consider a holomorphic symplectic manifold XX which admits an SYZ fibration and apply SYZ mirror symmetry to study its brane quantization. Given any semi-affine, space-filling coisotropic A-brane ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} on XX, we construct the mirror B-brane ℬˇcc\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}} on the mirror manifold Xˇ\check{X} by an SYZ transform. We then present a mathematical definition of the endomorphism algebra H​o​mA​(ℬcc,ℬcc)Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}) by constructing a distinguished non-formal holomorphic deformation quantization of XX. Using a twisted family Toeplitz construction, we transform H​o​mA​(ℬcc,ℬcc)Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}) to the mirror B-side and prove that this induces an isomorphism H​o​mA​(ℬcc,ℬcc)≅H​o​mB​(ℬˇcc,ℬˇcc)Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}})\cong Hom_{B}(\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}},\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}) between the endomorphism algebras. Furthermore, taking any torus fiber of XX as the Lagrangian A-brane ℬ\mathcal{B}, we fully realize Gukov–Witten’s proposal, namely, there is a natural action of H​o​mA​(ℬcc,ℬcc)Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}) on H​o​mA​(ℬ,ℬcc)Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}) which is precisely mirror to the natural action on the mirror B-side. This provides a mathematical framework which is compatible with Gukov–Witten’s brane quantization proposal, SYZ mirror symmetry as well as family Floer theory.

1. Introduction

1.1. Background and the main result

Kontsevich’s homological mirror symmetry [27] asserts that the Fukaya category F​u​k​(X,ω)Fuk(X,\omega) of a symplectic manifold (X,ω)(X,\omega) is quasi-equivalent to the derived category Db​(Xˇ,Jˇ)D^{b}(\check{X},\check{J}) of coherent sheaves of its mirror complex manifold (Xˇ,Jˇ)(\check{X},\check{J}). However, Kapustin–Orlov [25] observed that for some special symplectic structure, the Fukaya category of (X,ω)(X,\omega) could be smaller than Db​(Xˇ,Jˇ)D^{b}(\check{X},\check{J}). An algebraic way to resolve this asymmetry is to add more objects by taking the split closure of F​u​k​(X,ω)Fuk(X,\omega), as was done in e.g., [2, 22, 42]. A more geometric approach was proposed by Kapustin–Orlov [25], who suggested that coisotropic submanifolds, not just Lagrangian submanifolds, should be considered as objects in the Fukaya category. These new objects are called coisotropic A-branes. Nevertheless, a definition of morphism spaces between coisotropic A-branes remains elusive, despite much effort [3, 8, 18, 24, 33, 36, 37, 38, 45].

On the other hand, Gukov–Witten [21] introduced the notion of a canonical coisotropic A-brane of a symplectic manifold (X,ω)(X,\omega). This is a space-filling brane, meaning that it is a coisotropic A-brane ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} supported on the whole XX, and it is equipped with a U​(1)U(1)-line bundle (L,∇L)(L,\nabla^{L}) over XX such that I:=ω−1​F∇LI:=\omega^{-1}F_{\nabla^{L}} defines a complex structure on XX; here −2​π​−1​F∇L-2\pi\sqrt{-1}F_{\nabla^{L}} is the curvature 2-form of the U​(1)U(1)-connection ∇L\nabla^{L}. We thus obtain a structure sheaf 𝒪X\mathcal{O}_{X} and a Dolbeault operator ∂¯I\bar{\partial}_{I} on XX. Also, the 2-form ΩX:=F∇L+−1​ω\Omega_{X}:=F_{\nabla^{L}}+\sqrt{-1}\omega defines a holomorphic symplectic structure on (X,I)(X,I). Gukov and Witten proposed that

  • •

    The endomorphism algebra H​o​mA​(ℬcc,ℬcc)Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}) of the coisotropic A-brane ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} is a holomorphic deformation quantization (DQ) of the sheaf cohomology H∗​(X,𝒪X)H^{*}(X,\mathcal{O}_{X}) of holomorphic functions on XX with respect to ΩX\Omega_{X}.

  • •

    Furthermore, for any Lagrangian brane ℬ=(ℬ¯,ℒ)\mathcal{B}=(\underline{\mathcal{B}},\mathcal{L}) such that F∇L|ℬ¯F_{\nabla^{L}}|_{\underline{\mathcal{B}}} is non-degenerate, the morphism space H​o​mA​(ℬ,ℬcc)Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}) is the geometric quantization (GQ) of ℬ¯\underline{\mathcal{B}} equipped with the pre-quantum line bundle L|ℬ¯⊗ℒ∗L|_{\underline{\mathcal{B}}}\otimes\mathcal{L}^{*}.

In particular, there is a natural action “D​Q↷G​QDQ\curvearrowright GQ” given by the categorical composition

H​o​mA​(ℬcc,ℬcc)⊗H​o​mA​(ℬ,ℬcc)→H​o​mA​(ℬ,ℬcc).Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}})\otimes Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}})\to Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}).

This action is called a brane quantization.

In this paper, we study Gukov–Witten’s brane quantization proposal via SYZ mirror symmetry [43]. Suppose XX admits a Lagrangian torus fibration p:X→Bp:X\to B, or an SYZ fibration, without singular fibers. The SYZ mirror Xˇ\check{X} of XX is then constructed as the total space of the fiberwise dual torus fibration. We obtain a pair of fiberwise dual torus fibrations [12, 29, 31, 43]

Consider a canonical coisotropic A-brane ℬcc=(X,L,∇L)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}=(X,L,\nabla^{L}) on XX which is semi-affine, meaning that the restriction F∇L|FxF_{\nabla^{L}}|_{F_{x}}, where Fx:=p−1​(x)F_{x}:=p^{-1}(x) is the torus fiber over x∈Bx\in B, is a constant 2-form independent of x∈Bx\in B; this notion was introduced in [17]. For any positive integer k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}, we also consider the canonical coisotropic A-brane ℬcc(k):=(X,L⊗k,∇L⊗k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}:=(X,L^{\otimes k},\nabla^{L^{\otimes k}}) with respect to the scaled symplectic structure k​ωk\omega.

By a slight modification of the SYZ transform introduced in [17], we construct a family {ℬˇcc(k)}k∈ℤ>0\{\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}\}_{k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}} of B-branes on Xˇ\check{X} which is SYZ mirror to the family {ℬcc(k)}k∈ℤ>0\{\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}\}_{k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}} of A-branes on XX. We prove that the mirror B-brane ℬˇcc(k)\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)} is a holomorphic vector bundle Eˇ(k)\check{E}^{(k)} on Xˇ\check{X} whose rank is given by r​k​(Eˇ(k))=∫Fxc​h​(L)krk(\check{E}^{(k)})=\int_{F_{x}}ch(L)^{k}, which tends to infinity as k→∞k\to\infty.

Our main focus is on the A-model morphism spaces. Following Gukov–Witten’s proposal [21], we propose a mathematically rigorous definition of the endomorpism algebra of ℬcc(k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}, as a non-formal holomorphic deformation quantization of XX. Here comes the key novelty of the paper. By considering fiberwise geometric quantization with Kähler polarization on XX and introducing a suitable twist of family of Toeplitz operators, we are able to define a mirror transform from the endomorphism algebra of ℬcc(k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)} to that of the mirror B-brane ℬˇcc(k)\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}. Our main result is the following mirror isomorphism.

Theorem 1.1.

For every k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}, the mirror transform, defined as a family twisted Toeplitz operators, induces an isomorphism

H​o​mA​(ℬcc(k),ℬcc(k))≅H​o​mB​(ℬˇcc(k),ℬˇcc(k))Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)})\cong Hom_{B}(\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)})

of graded algebras.

Note that the ring structure on the LHS is given by a star product, while H​o​mB​(ℬˇcc(k),ℬˇcc(k))=H∗​(Xˇ,End⁡(Eˇ(k)))Hom_{B}(\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)})=H^{*}(\check{X},\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)})) so the ring structure on the RHS is the matrix multiplication in End⁡(Eˇ(k))\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)}). Explicit transformations of morphism spaces which induce mirror isomorphisms are very rarely achieved. To the best of our knowledge, our construction is the first such transform for coisotropic A-branes.

As we will see shortly, the fiber of the holomorphic vector bundle Eˇ(k)\check{E}^{(k)} at the point (Fx,ℒ)∈Xˇ(F_{x},\mathcal{L})\in\check{X} is by construction given by

Eˇ(Fx,ℒ)(k)=H0​(Fx,L⊗k|Fx⊗ℒ∗⊗KΩ).\check{E}_{(F_{x},\mathcal{L})}^{(k)}=H^{0}(F_{x},L^{\otimes k}|_{F_{x}}\otimes\mathcal{L}^{*}\otimes\sqrt{K_{\Omega}}).

This is nothing but the geometric quantization of (Fx,ΩX|Fx)(F_{x},\Omega_{X}|_{F_{x}}), which, again according to Gukov–Witten’s proposal [21], should exactly be the A-model morphism space H​o​mA​(ℬ,ℬcc(k))Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}), where ℬ\mathcal{B} denotes the Lagrangian A-brane (Fx,ℒ)(F_{x},\mathcal{L}). In other words, we should set

H​o​mA​(ℬ,ℬcc(k)):=H0​(Fx,L⊗k|Fx⊗ℒ∗⊗KΩ).Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}):=H^{0}(F_{x},L^{\otimes k}|_{F_{x}}\otimes\mathcal{L}^{*}\otimes\sqrt{K_{\Omega}}).

Now the natural action of H​o​mA​(ℬcc(k),ℬcc(k))Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}) on H​o​mA​(ℬ,ℬcc(k))Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}) by Toeplitz operators [13, 45] corresponds, through the above mirror isomorphism, to the simple and natural B-model action of H​o​mB​(ℬˇcc(k),ℬˇcc(k))=H∗​(Xˇ,End⁡(Eˇ(k)))Hom_{B}(\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)})=H^{*}(\check{X},\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)})) on H​o​mB​(ℬˇ,ℬˇcc(k))=Eˇ(Fx,ℒ)(k)Hom_{B}(\check{\mathcal{B}},\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)})=\check{E}_{(F_{x},\mathcal{L})}^{(k)}. This shows that the D​QDQ-module structure on G​QGQ (of the fiber FxF_{x}), which fully and rigorously realizes Gukov–Witten’s brane quantization proposal in the current setting, is compatible with SYZ mirror symmetry as well as family Floer theory.

1.2. The SYZ transform and construction of the mirror B-brane

Let us explain our constructions in more detail. Recall that in SYZ mirror symmetry [12, 29, 31, 43], the SYZ mirror Xˇ\check{X} of XX is constructed as the moduli space of pairs {(Fx,ℒ)}\{(F_{x},\mathcal{L})\} consisting of a Lagrangian torus fiber Fx=p−1​(x)F_{x}=p^{-1}(x) of p:X→Bp:X\to B equipped with a flat U​(1)U(1)-local system ℒ\mathcal{L}. Given a semi-affine canonical coisotropic A-brane ℬcc=(X,L,∇L)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}=(X,L,\nabla^{L}) on XX, we can then construct the mirror B-brane ℬˇcc=Eˇ\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}=\check{E}, as a holomorphic vector bundle on Xˇ\check{X}, using a slight modification of the SYZ transform defined in [17].

More precisely, the fiber of Eˇ\check{E} over (Fx,ℒ)∈Xˇ(F_{x},\mathcal{L})\in\check{X} is given by

Eˇ(Fx,ℒ):=H0​(Fx,L|Fx⊗ℒ∗⊗KΩ),\check{E}_{(F_{x},\mathcal{L})}:=H^{0}(F_{x},L|_{F_{x}}\otimes\mathcal{L}^{*}\otimes\sqrt{K_{\Omega}}),

where KΩ\sqrt{K_{\Omega}} is a holomorphic half-form bundle on FxF_{x} with respect to some auxiliary locally constant (with respect to x∈Bx\in B) flat complex structure Ω\Omega on FxF_{x}. This complex structure turns each fiber into an abelian variety and L|FxL|_{F_{x}} gives a pre-quantum line bundle on (Fx,Ω)(F_{x},\Omega). Thus, Eˇ(Fx,ℒ)\check{E}_{(F_{x},\mathcal{L})} is nothing but the Hilbert space with half-form corrections in the geometric quantization of FxF_{x} with respect to the pre-quantum line bundle L|Fx⊗ℒ∗L|_{F_{x}}\otimes\mathcal{L}^{*} and the Kähler polarization.

To glue these fibers together, we need to consider a change of affine charts in the base BB. Let Ω′\Omega^{\prime} be an auxiliary locally constant flat complex structure on FxF_{x} over another chart. Then the two spaces of Kähler polarized sections are related by the BKS isomorphism

B​K​SΩ′,Ω:H0​(Fx,L⊗ℒ∗⊗KΩ)→∼H0​(Fx,L⊗ℒ∗⊗KΩ′).BKS_{\Omega^{\prime},\Omega}:H^{0}(F_{x},L\otimes\mathcal{L}^{*}\otimes\sqrt{K_{\Omega}})\xrightarrow{\sim}H^{0}(F_{x},L\otimes\mathcal{L}^{*}\otimes\sqrt{K_{\Omega^{\prime}}}).

It is well-known that BKS maps satisfy [4, 6]

B​K​SΩ′′,Ω∘B​K​SΩ′,Ω=B​K​SΩ′′,Ω.BKS_{\Omega^{\prime\prime},\Omega}\circ BKS_{\Omega^{\prime},\Omega}=BKS_{\Omega^{\prime\prime},\Omega}.

This yields the desired cocycle condition and independence of the choice of the fiber Kähler polarization.

This construction can easily be generalized to the level kk canonical coisotropic A-brane

ℬcc(k):=(X,L⊗k,∇L⊗k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}:=(X,L^{\otimes k},\nabla^{L^{\otimes k}})

with respect to the kk-scaled symplectic manifold (X,k​ω)(X,k\omega) for any k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}. The mirror B-brane ℬˇcc(k)\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)} is a holomorphic vector bundle Eˇ(k)\check{E}^{(k)} on Xˇ(k)\check{X}^{(k)} (with complex coordinates zˇi:=xi+−1k​yˇi\check{z}_{i}:=x_{i}+\frac{\sqrt{-1}}{k}\check{y}_{i}, i=1,…,2​ni=1,\dots,2n), whose rank is given by the Riemann-Roch formula:

r​k​(Eˇ(k))=∫Fxc​h​(L)k.rk(\check{E}^{(k)})=\int_{F_{x}}ch(L)^{k}.

The detailed construction of ℬˇcc(k)\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)} will be given in Section 4 (see Theorems 4.1 and 4.13).

1.3. Non-formal holomorphic deformation quantization

The next step is to define the endomorphism algebra H​o​mA​(ℬcc(k),ℬcc(k))Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}) and construct the mirror transform.

First of all, Gukov–Witten [21] proposed that H​o​mA​(ℬcc,ℬcc)Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}) carries a natural noncommutative algebra structure arising from a holomorphic deformation quantization of (X,ΩX)(X,\Omega_{X}). They further suggested that when XX admits a “good A-model” (see [21, pages 2-3]), this deformation quantization should be non-formal and governed by an actual complex parameter rather than a formal one; see [21, page 11].

In Section 3, we construct a distinguished non-formal holomorphic deformation quantization of (X,Ω)(X,\Omega) depending on a real parameter ℏ\hbar. For each level k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}, the specialization ℏ=1/k\hbar=1/k realizes the A-model endomorphism algebra H​o​mA​(ℬcc(k),ℬcc(k))Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}) for the A-model on (X,k​ω)(X,k\omega). Such a non-formal definition is also necessary to make sense of the desired mirror statement H​o​mA​(ℬcc(k),ℬcc(k))≅H​o​mB​(ℬˇcc(k),ℬˇcc(k))Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)})\cong Hom_{B}(\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}).

Let us briefly describe the construction. Using data from ℬcc(k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}, we first write down a non-formal Moyal-type star product ⋆q\star_{q} on each torus fiber of XX. On the other hand, following [17], we construct some distinguished local models for the semi-affine A-brane ℬcc(k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}. These so-called skew-Smith forms of ℬcc(k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)} produce an atlas of complex affine coordinates on XX. Now for each affine chart U⊂BU\subset B, let XU:=p−1​(U)X_{U}:=p^{-1}(U). We expand each smooth function f∈C∞​(XU)f\in C^{\infty}(X_{U}) as a fiberwise Fourier series whose Fourier coefficients f^m​(x)\widehat{f}_{m}(x) are smooth functions on U⊂BU\subset B and then we define ⋆U,ℏ:C∞(XU)×C∞(XU)→C∞(XU)\star_{U,\hbar}:C^{\infty}(X_{U})\times C^{\infty}(X_{U})\to C^{\infty}(X_{U}) by extending the star product ⋆q\star_{q} C∞​(U)C^{\infty}(U)-bilinearly. The Schwartz’s condition (in the lattice variable mm) of the Fourier coefficients ensures (absolute) convergence. We denote this non-formal star product by ⋆U,ℏ\star_{U,\hbar} and extend it to a graded star product

⋆U,ℏ:Ω0,∗(XU)×Ω0,∗(XU)→Ω0,∗(XU)\star_{U,\hbar}:\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U})\times\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U})\to\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U})

in the most natural way.

The transition maps of (X,I)(X,I) being affine allows us to glue these graded algebras to a global graded algebra

⋆ℏ:Ω0,∗(X)×Ω0,∗(X)→Ω0,∗(X),\star_{\hbar}:\Omega^{0,*}(X)\times\Omega^{0,*}(X)\to\Omega^{0,*}(X),

and we can establish the Leibniz rule:

∂¯I​(α⋆ℏβ)=(∂¯I​α)⋆ℏβ+(−1)|α|​|β|​α⋆ℏ∂¯I​β.\bar{\partial}_{I}(\alpha\star_{\hbar}\beta)=(\bar{\partial}_{I}\alpha)\star_{\hbar}\beta+(-1)^{|\alpha||\beta|}\alpha\star_{\hbar}\bar{\partial}_{I}\beta.

As a result, we obtain a differential graded algebra (dga) (see Theorem 3.7)

𝒜X,ℏ:=(Ω0,∗​(X),∂¯I,⋆ℏ).\mathcal{A}_{X,\hbar}:=(\Omega^{0,*}(X),\bar{\partial}_{I},\star_{\hbar}).

A key feature of this Moyal-type star product ⋆ℏ\star_{\hbar} is that, although it is non-local along the fiber direction of the SYZ fibration p:X→Bp:X\to B, it remains local along the base BB. We then define

H​o​mA​(ℬcc(k),ℬcc(k)):=(H∂¯I0,∗​(X),⋆k−1).Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}):=(H_{\bar{\partial}_{I}}^{0,*}(X),\star_{k^{-1}}).

Some justification of this definition is in order. Let FxF_{x} be a torus fiber of XX over a point x∈Bx\in B. Our standing assumption 1.4 on the space-filling A-brane ℬcc(k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)} implies that (X,ΩX)(X,\Omega_{X}) is a complexification of the symplectic torus (Fx,ΩX|FX)(F_{x},\Omega_{X}|_{F_{X}}). According to the Gukov–Witten picture, we shall realize H​o​mA​(ℬ,ℬcc(k))Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}) as the geometric quantization of (Fx,ΩX|Fx)(F_{x},\Omega_{X}|_{F_{x}}) in a torus-invariant Kähler polarization.

As explained in [45], this places us in a setting where the Berezin–Toeplitz star product yields a canonical but formal holomorphic deformation quantization on XUX_{U} for a sufficiently small neighborhood U⊂BU\subset B of xx, uniquely determined (up to equivalence) by Toeplitz operators acting on these morphism spaces [13, 45]. This is also in line with the philosophy of family Floer theory, namely, the noncommutative product on H​o​mA​(ℬcc(k),ℬcc(k))Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}) should be determined by its action on H​o​mA​(ℬ,ℬcc(k))Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}), where ℬ\mathcal{B} ranges over all the Lagrangian A-branes supported on torus fibers of XX (cf. [18]).

Now, as shown in [4] (see also [32]), the Berezin–Toeplitz star product and the Moyal star product on a symplectic torus are actually gauge equivalent (as formal star products). So it seems that we can use the fiberwise Berezin–Toeplitz quantization to define the required holomorphic deformation quantization on XX. Unfortunately, for different open subsets XUX_{U}’s, the choices of torus invariant Kähler polarization could be very different (in fact they could be arbitrary). To glue these Berezin–Toeplitz star products together, we need to choose gauge transformations between different polarizations. The issue is that these gauge transformations need not converge at the non-formal level (i.e., when we evaluate at ℏ=1/k\hbar=1/k). In contrast, our construction gives a holomorphic deformation quantization of XX which is non-formal but locally formally equivalent to the fiberwise Berezin–Toeplitz deformation quantization. This is consistent with the Gukov–Witten picture. More importantly, we are able to construct the mirror transform of the morphism spaces under such a definition.

1.4. The mirror isomorphism

In define the mirror transform, our idea is to apply the family Toeplitz construction. Recall that for a general compact Kähler manifold (M,ω,J)(M,\omega,J) equipped a pre-quantum line bundle LL, the level kk Toeplitz operator Tf(k)∈Endℂ⁡(H0​(M,L⊗k))T_{f}^{(k)}\in\operatorname{End}_{\mathbb{C}}(H^{0}(M,L^{\otimes k})) associated to f∈C∞​(M)f\in C^{\infty}(M) is defined by

Tf:s↦Π(k)​(f⋅s),T_{f}:s\mapsto\Pi^{(k)}(f\cdot s),

where Π(k):L2​(M,L⊗k)→H0​(M,L⊗k)\Pi^{(k)}:L^{2}(M,L^{\otimes k})\to H^{0}(M,L^{\otimes k}) is the orthogonal projection [7].

Applying this construction to each fiber of p:X→Bp:X\to B, we obtain a ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear map

TU(k):C∞​(XU)→C∞​(XˇU(k),End⁡(Eˇ(k))).T_{U}^{(k)}:C^{\infty}(X_{U})\to C^{\infty}(\check{X}_{U}^{(k)},\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)})).

By sending d​z¯i1∧⋯∧d​z¯ild\bar{z}^{i_{1}}\wedge\cdots\wedge d\bar{z}^{i_{l}} to d​zˇ¯i1∧⋯∧d​zˇ¯ild\bar{\check{z}}^{i_{1}}\wedge\cdots\wedge d\bar{\check{z}}^{i_{l}}, TU(k)T_{U}^{(k)} extends to a graded ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear map

TU(k):Ω0,∗​(XU)→Ω0,∗​(XˇU(k),End⁡(EˇU(k))).T_{U}^{(k)}:\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U})\to\Omega^{0,*}(\check{X}_{U}^{(k)},\operatorname{End}(\check{E}_{U}^{(k)})).

The most surprising property of TU(k)T_{U}^{(k)} is that it interchanges the Dolbeault operators ∂¯I\bar{\partial}_{I} and ∂¯End⁡(Eˇ(k))\bar{\partial}_{\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)})}:

Proposition 1.2 (= Lemma 5.10).

The ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear map

TU(k):(Ω0,∗​(XU),∂¯I)→(Ω0,∗​(XˇU(k),End⁡(Eˇ(k))),∂¯End⁡(Eˇ(k)))T_{U}^{(k)}:(\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U}),\bar{\partial}_{I})\to(\Omega^{0,*}(\check{X}_{U}^{(k)},\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)})),\bar{\partial}_{\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)})})

is a cochain homomorphism.

However, we encounter a difficulty when we try to globalize the construction. Namely, the collection of maps {TU(k)}U\{T_{U}^{(k)}\}_{U} does not commute with the transition maps (i.e., the BKS maps) of Eˇ(k)\check{E}^{(k)}. It turns out that this problem can be solved by applying the gauge transformation e1k​ΔΩe^{\frac{1}{k}\Delta_{\Omega}}, where ΔΩ\Delta_{\Omega} is a complexification of the ∂¯Ω\bar{\partial}_{\Omega}-Laplacian of (Fx,Ω)(F_{x},\Omega). Since this gauge transformation involves differential operators, it is not well-defined on C∞​(XU)C^{\infty}(X_{U}). Nevertheless, a careful calculation shows that the composition

ΦU(k):=TU(k)∘e1k​ΔΩ\Phi_{U}^{(k)}:=T_{U}^{(k)}\circ e^{\frac{1}{k}\Delta_{\Omega}}

is actually well-defined. Moreover, the collection of maps {ΦU(k)}U\{\Phi_{U}^{(k)}\}_{U} now commutes with the BKS maps. Extending Φ(k)\Phi^{(k)} as in Proposition 1.2, we obtain a cochain map between the Dolbeault complex of (X,𝒪X)(X,\mathcal{O}_{X}) and that of End⁡(Eˇ(k))\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)}):

Φ(k):(Ω0,∗​(X),∂¯I)→(Ω0,∗​(Xˇ,End⁡(Eˇ(k))),∂¯End⁡(Eˇ(k))),\Phi^{(k)}:(\Omega^{0,*}(X),\bar{\partial}_{I})\to(\Omega^{0,*}(\check{X},\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)})),\bar{\partial}_{\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)})}),

called the mirror transform. Below is a more precise version of our main theorem.

Theorem 1.3 (=Theorem 5.1).

For every k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}, we have

Φ(k)​(α⋆k−1β)=Φ(k)​(α)∘Φ(k)​(β)\Phi^{(k)}(\alpha\star_{k^{-1}}\beta)=\Phi^{(k)}(\alpha)\circ\Phi^{(k)}(\beta)

for any α,β∈Ω0,∗​(X)\alpha,\beta\in\Omega^{0,*}(X), and Φ(k)\Phi^{(k)} is bijective. In other words, the mirror transform

Φ(k):(Ω0,∗​(X),∂¯I)→(Ω0,∗​(Xˇ,End⁡(Eˇ(k))),∂¯End⁡(Eˇ(k)))\Phi^{(k)}:(\Omega^{0,*}(X),\bar{\partial}_{I})\to(\Omega^{0,*}(\check{X},\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)})),\bar{\partial}_{\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)})})

is an isomorphism of differential graded algebras for every k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}. In particular, we have an isomorphism

H​o​mA​(ℬcc(k),ℬcc(k))≅H​o​mB​(ℬˇcc(k),ℬˇcc(k)),Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)})\cong Hom_{B}(\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}),

of graded algebras for every k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}.

In this mirror isomorphism, the A-model action of H​o​mA​(ℬcc(k),ℬcc(k))Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}) on H​o​mA​(ℬ,ℬcc(k))Hom_{A}(\mathcal{B},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}) precisely corresponds to the B-model action of H∗​(Xˇ,End⁡(Eˇ))H^{*}(\check{X},\operatorname{End}(\check{E})) on E(Fx,ℒ)(k)E_{(F_{x},\mathcal{L})}^{(k)}. As far as we know, this is the first mirror theorem for coisotropic A-branes as well as for brane quantization. This also provides another instance where we see that SYZ transforms can provide information which is beyond the reach of homological mirror symmetry (cf. [14]).

1.5. Organization of the paper

In Section 2, we review the notion of coisotropic A-branes and in particular the semi-affine condition introduced in [17]. In Section 3, we construct a non-formal Moyal-type holomorphic star product on 𝒪X​[[ℏ]]\mathcal{O}_{X}[[\hbar]]. We also present three concrete examples (including a non-Kähler example). In Section 4, we make a slight modification of the SYZ transform constructed in [17] and use it to construct the family {ℬˇcc(k)}k∈ℤ>0\{\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}\}_{k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}} of mirror B-branes. Section 5 is the heart of the paper, where we define the mirror transform for morphism spaces via a twisted family Toeplitz construction. Finally we prove that the mirror transform induces a mirror isomorphism for every level k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}.

1.6. Setup and notations

Throughout this paper, let BB be a 2​n2n-dimensional connected integral affine manifold, and define X:=T∗​B/ΛX:=T^{*}B/\Lambda and Xˇ:=T​B/Λˇ\check{X}:=TB/\check{\Lambda}. Here, Λ⊂T∗​B\Lambda\subset T^{*}B and Λˇ⊂T​B\check{\Lambda}\subset TB denote the lattice bundle and the dual lattice bundle, respectively, induced by the integral affine structure on BB. Moreover, let ℬcc=(X,L,∇L)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}=(X,L,\nabla^{L}) be a rank-11 coisotropic A-brane on the symplectic manifold (X,ω)(X,\omega) satisfying the following standing assumption:

Assumption 1.4.

The coisotropic A-brane ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} is semi-affine (see Definition 2.4) and the curvature of ∇L\nabla^{L} is non-degenerate on every fiber of the SYZ fibration p:X→Bp:X\to B.

For each k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}, ℬcc(k)=(X,L⊗k,∇L⊗k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}=(X,L^{\otimes k},\nabla^{L^{\otimes k}}) is then a rank-11 coisotropic A-brane on the symplectic manifold (X,k​ω)(X,k\omega). We denote by ℬˇcc(k)\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)} be the mirror brane of ℬcc(k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}, which will be constructed in Section 4. Our standing assumption 1.4 implies that ℬˇcc(k)\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)} is a (possibly higher rank, especially for kk large) space-filling semi-affine B-brane on Xˇ(k)\check{X}^{(k)}, i.e. it is of the form

ℬˇcc(k)=(Xˇ(k),Eˇ(k),∇Eˇ(k)),\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}=(\check{X}^{(k)},\check{E}^{(k)},\nabla^{\check{E}^{(k)}}),

where (Eˇ(k),∇Eˇ(k))(\check{E}^{(k)},\nabla^{\check{E}^{(k)}}) is a Hermitian vector bundle on Xˇ(k)\check{X}^{(k)} with a unitary connection.

We furthermore adopt the following notations:

  • •

    Let 𝒞∞​(M)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(M) be the algebra of smooth ℂ\mathbb{C}-valued functions on a smooth manifold MM.

  • •

    For all open subset UU of BB, define the open subsets XU:=X|UX_{U}:=X|_{U} of XX and XˇU:=Xˇ|U\check{X}_{U}:=\check{X}|_{U} of Xˇ\check{X}. Similarly, define ΛU:=Λ|U\Lambda_{U}:=\Lambda|_{U} and ΛˇU:=Λˇ|U\check{\Lambda}_{U}:=\check{\Lambda}|_{U}.

  • •

    When x=(x1,…,x2​n)x=(x_{1},...,x_{2n}) are integral affine coordinates on BB, we denote by

    (x,y)=(x1,…,x2​n,y1,…,y2​n)and(x,yˇ)=(x1,…,x2​n,yˇ1,…,yˇ2​n)(x,y)=(x_{1},...,x_{2n},y^{1},...,y^{2n})\quad\text{and}\quad(x,\check{y})=(x_{1},...,x_{2n},\check{y}_{1},...,\check{y}_{2n})

    the induced real coordinates on T∗​BT^{*}B and on T​BTB respectively.

  • •

    Let ω\omega be the canonical symplectic form on XX given by ω=d​xi∧d​yi\omega=dx_{i}\wedge dy^{i}.

  • •

    A kk-tuple of complex valued functions on a manifold is identified with the corresponding column vector, and vice versa. If a=(a1,…,ak)a=(a_{1},...,a_{k}) and b=(b1,…,bk)b=(b_{1},...,b_{k}) are such kk-tuples, then we denote their dot product by a⋅ba\cdot b, i.e.

    a⋅b:=a1​b1+⋯+ak​bk.a\cdot b:=a_{1}b_{1}+\cdots+a_{k}b_{k}.

    If α=(α1,…,αk)\alpha=(\alpha_{1},...,\alpha_{k}) and β=(β1,…,βk)\beta=(\beta_{1},...,\beta_{k}) are kk-tuples of 11-forms, then we define

    α∧β:=α1∧β1+⋯+αk∧βk.\alpha\wedge\beta:=\alpha_{1}\wedge\beta_{1}+\cdots+\alpha_{k}\wedge\beta_{k}.
  • •

    For a k×kk\times k matrix AA, ATA^{T} denotes the transpose of AA and A−T:=(AT)−1A^{-T}:=(A^{T})^{-1}. For a kk-tuple aa, A​aAa denotes the matrix multiplication of AA and aa whenever it is well defined.

  • •

    If a=(a1,…,a2​n)a=(a_{1},...,a_{2n}), then we write

    𝒂1=(a1,…,an)and𝒂2=(an+1,…,a2​n).\boldsymbol{a}_{1}=(a_{1},...,a_{n})\quad\text{and}\quad\boldsymbol{a}_{2}=(a_{n+1},...,a_{2n}).

    The notations 𝒂1\boldsymbol{a}^{1} and 𝒂2\boldsymbol{a}^{2} are similarly defined for a=(a1,…,a2​n)a=(a^{1},...,a^{2n}).

  • •

    Denote by ℍn\mathbb{H}_{n} the Segal upper half space, i.e. the set of symmetric n×nn\times n matrices Ω\Omega over ℂ\mathbb{C} such that Im⁡Ω\operatorname{Im}\Omega is positive definite.

Acknowledgement

Leung was substantially supported by grants of the Hong Kong Research Grants Council (Project No. 14305923, 14302224, 14302225). The work of Q. Li was supported by grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. 12471061). The work of Y.-H. Suen was supported by the National Science and Technology Council (Project No. 113-2115-M-006-016-MY2) and partially supported by the Yushan Fellow Program by the Ministry of Education (MOE), Taiwan. (MOE-114-YSFMS-0005-001-P1). Y. Yau was supported by World Premier International Research Center Initiative (WPI), MEXT, Japan, and gratefully acknowledges the support of the National Center for Theoretical Sciences, where part of this work was carried out.

The authors thank Si Li for helpful discussions and interest in this work.

2. Preliminaries

In this section, we recall the notion of coisotropic A-branes and the semi-affine condition introduced by K.-L. Chan–Leung–Zhang [17] and give a rough idea on what should be the mirror B-brane. Such idea will be made rigorous in Section 4.

2.1. A- and B-branes

In this paper, we restrict our attention to the definition of rank-11 coisotropic A-branes. For a more general treatment involving higher-rank cases, we refer the reader to [17, 20, 23, 30].

Definition 2.1.

Let (X,ω)(X,\omega) be a symplectic manifold. A coisotropic A-brane is a coisotropic submanifold C⊂XC\subset X together with a U​(1)U(1)-line bundle (L,∇L)(L,\nabla^{L}) on CC such that

  • (a)

    The curvature −2​π​−1​F∇L-2\pi\sqrt{-1}F_{\nabla^{L}} vanishes on ker⁡(ω|C)\ker(\omega|_{C}). In particular, the 22-form F∇LF_{\nabla^{L}} descends to a map F∇L:T​C/ker⁡(ω|C)→T∗​CF_{\nabla^{L}}:TC/\ker(\omega|_{C})\to T^{*}C.

  • (b)

    The composition I:=ω−1​F∇L:T​C/ker⁡(ω|C)→T​C/ker⁡(ω|C)I:=\omega^{-1}F_{\nabla^{L}}:TC/\ker(\omega|_{C})\to TC/\ker(\omega|_{C}) defines a complex structure, i.e. I2=−i​dI^{2}=-id.

Remark 2.2.

The complex structure II on T​C/ker⁡(ω|C)TC/\ker(\omega|_{C}) associated with (C,L,∇L)(C,L,\nabla^{L}) is always integrable [25]. When C=L⊂XC=L\subset X is a Lagrangian, ker⁡(ω|L)=T​L\ker(\omega|_{L})=TL, so the brane condition boils down to the well-known condition for a Lagrangian brane, i.e. F∇L=0F_{\nabla^{L}}=0.

Specifically, by Condition (b) in Definition 2.1, the brane structure (L,∇L)(L,\nabla^{L}) of a space-filling coisotropic A-brane turns XX into a complex manifold with complex structure I:=ω−1​F∇LI:=\omega^{-1}F_{\nabla^{L}}. We use 𝒪X\mathcal{O}_{X} to denote the structure sheaf corresponding to this complex structure. The 2-form

ΩX:=F∇L+−1​ω\Omega_{X}:=F_{\nabla^{L}}+\sqrt{-1}\omega

is an II-holomorphic 2-form on XX, turning XX into a holomorphic symplectic manifold. Conversely, given a holomorphic symplectic manifold (X,ΩX)(X,\Omega_{X}) so that Im⁡(ΩX)\operatorname{Im}(\Omega_{X}) is non-degenerate and −2​π​−1​Re⁡(ΩX)-2\pi\sqrt{-1}\operatorname{Re}(\Omega_{X}) is the curvature of some U​(1)U(1)-line bundle (L,∇L)(L,\nabla^{L}) on XX, (X,L,∇L)(X,L,\nabla^{L}) defines a space-filling coisotropic A-brane, called the canonical coisotropic brane. We denote it by ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}.

Coisotropic A-branes show up at very specific points in the Kähler moduli. For example, if we scale ω\omega by an arbitrary constant kk, Condition (b) in Definition 2.1 will no longer be fulfilled by any U​(1)U(1)-line bundle because F∇LF_{\nabla^{L}} needs to be integral. However, when k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}, we do have the canonical coisotropic brane (X,L⊗k,∇L⊗k)(X,L^{\otimes k},\nabla^{L^{\otimes k}}) with respect to the scaled symplectic manifold (X,k​ω)(X,k\omega). We denote this brane by ℬcc(k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}.

Kapustin–Orlov [25] suggested that non-Lagrangian coisotropic A-branes should also be included as an object in the Fukaya category of (X,ω)(X,\omega). In particular, by mirror symmetry, any coisotropic A-brane should admit a mirror B-brane.

Definition 2.3.

Let Xˇ\check{X} be a complex manifold. A B-brane is a complex submanifold Cˇ⊂Xˇ\check{C}\subset\check{X} together with a unitary holomorphic vector bundle Eˇ\check{E} on Cˇ\check{C}.

2.2. Semi-affine branes

Starting from this section, we introduce the parameter k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}, and consider the A-model of (X,k​ω)(X,k\omega) and its mirror B-model.

Suppose (X,k​ω)(X,k\omega) admits a semi-flat (i.e. without singular fibers) Lagrangian torus fibration p:X→Bp:X\to B with section. It is well-known that (X,k​ω)(X,k\omega) can be identified with (T∗​B/Λ,k​ωs​t​d)(T^{*}B/\Lambda,k\omega_{std}), where

ωs​t​d=∑i=1nd​xi∧d​yi.\omega_{std}=\sum_{i=1}^{n}dx_{i}\wedge dy^{i}.

Let Xˇ:=T​B/Λˇ\check{X}:=TB/\check{\Lambda} and pˇ:Xˇ→B\check{p}:\check{X}\to B be the dual torus fibration. The total space Xˇ\check{X} admits a complex structure Jˇk\check{J}_{k}, which has local complex coordinates

zˇ(k)=(zˇ1(k),…,zˇ2​n(k)):=x+−1k​yˇ.\check{z}^{(k)}=(\check{z}_{1}^{(k)},...,\check{z}_{2n}^{(k)}):=x+\tfrac{\sqrt{-1}}{k}\check{y}.

This can be verified by observing how the pure spinors are transformed by the Poincaré bundle 𝒫\mathcal{P} on X×BXˇX\times_{B}\check{X}. The complex manifold Xˇ(k):=(Xˇ,Jˇk)\check{X}^{(k)}:=(\check{X},\check{J}_{k}) is called the SYZ mirror of (X,k​ω)(X,k\omega). Recall the following pullback diagram in the usual semi-flat SYZ setting:

The SYZ philosophy says that any symplecto-geometric object on XX is transformed to a complex-geometric object on Xˇ\check{X} via the “pullback-tensor-pushforward” construction. For instance, if L⊂XL\subset X is a Lagrangian section equipped with a flat U​(1)U(1)-bundle ℒ\mathcal{L}, then the SYZ transform of LL is the line bundle

Lˇ:=πˇ∗​(π∗​ℒ⊗𝒫).\check{L}:=\check{\pi}_{*}(\pi^{*}\mathcal{L}\otimes\mathcal{P}).

In other words, the fiber of Lˇ\check{L} at the point (x,yˇ)∈Xˇ(x,\check{y})\in\check{X} is given by

H0​(L∩Fx,ℒ⊗𝒫|(L∩Fx)×{yˇ})=ℒx⊗𝒫(px,yˇ),H^{0}(L\cap F_{x},\mathcal{L}\otimes\mathcal{P}|_{(L\cap F_{x})\times\{\check{y}\}})=\mathcal{L}_{x}\otimes\mathcal{P}_{(p_{x},\check{y})},

where L∩Fx={px}L\cap F_{x}=\{p_{x}\}. See [31, 15, 14] for more details and applications. K.-L. Chan–Leung–Zhang [17] applied the same idea to construct the mirror B-brane of a so-called semi-affine coisotropic A-brane.

Definition 2.4.

A (rank-11) coisotropic A-brane (C,L,∇L)(C,L,\nabla^{L}) said to be semi-affine if

  • (a)

    The restriction p|C:C→p​(C)p|_{C}:C\to p(C) is a torus bundle such that for any x∈Bx\in B, C∩FxC\cap F_{x} is an affine subtorus of the fiber Fx⊂XF_{x}\subset X over xx.

  • (b)

    The curvature of ∇L\nabla^{L} is a constant 22-form along each fiber, that is, if (yi)(y^{i}) are affine coordinates of C∩FxC\cap F_{x}, then F∇L|C∩Fx=∑i,jhi​j​d​yi∧d​yjF_{\nabla^{L}}|_{C\cap F_{x}}=\sum_{i,j}h_{ij}dy^{i}\wedge dy^{j} for some integers hi​j∈ℤh_{ij}\in\mathbb{Z}.

On the mirror side, the semi-affine condition becomes the following

Definition 2.5.

A B-brane (Cˇ,Eˇ,∇Eˇ)(\check{C},\check{E},\nabla^{\check{E}}) of Xˇ\check{X} is said to be semi-affine if

  • (a)

    The restriction pˇ|Cˇ:Cˇ→pˇ​(Cˇ)\check{p}|_{\check{C}}:\check{C}\to\check{p}(\check{C}) is a torus bundle such that for any x∈Bx\in B, Cˇ∩Fˇx\check{C}\cap\check{F}_{x} is an affine subtorus of the fiber Fˇx\check{F}_{x} of pˇ:Xˇ→B\check{p}:\check{X}\to B.

  • (b)

    The curvature of ∇Eˇ\nabla^{\check{E}} is a constant 2-form multiple of the identity map along each fiber.

K.-L. Chan–Leung–Zhang [17] established a correspondence

{Semi-affine A-branes}⟷{Semi-affine B-branes}\{\text{Semi-affine A-branes}\}\longleftrightarrow\{\text{Semi-affine B-branes}\}

via family Nahm transform, which involves the choice of a fiberwise spinor bundle. Roughly speaking, given a semi-affine coisotropic A-brane (C,L⊗k,∇L⊗k)(C,L^{\otimes k},\nabla^{L^{\otimes k}}), the fiber of the mirror B-brane Eˇ(k)\check{E}^{(k)} over the point (x,yˇ)∈Xˇ(x,\check{y})\in\check{X} is given by

ker⁡(D̸(x,yˇ):Γ​(C∩Fx,L|C∩Fx⊗k⊗𝒫|(C∩Fx)×{yˇ}⊗S̸x)→Γ​(C∩Fx,L|C∩Fx⊗k⊗𝒫|(C∩Fx)×{yˇ}⊗S̸x)),\ker(\not{D}_{(x,\check{y})}:\Gamma(C\cap F_{x},L|_{C\cap F_{x}}^{\otimes k}\otimes\mathcal{P}|_{(C\cap F_{x})\times\{\check{y}\}}\otimes\not{S}_{x})\to\Gamma(C\cap F_{x},L|_{C\cap F_{x}}^{\otimes k}\otimes\mathcal{P}|_{(C\cap F_{x})\times\{\check{y}\}}\otimes\not{S}_{x})),

where S̸x\not{S}_{x} is a spinor bundle on FxF_{x} and D̸(x,yˇ)\not{D}_{(x,\check{y})} is the Dirac operator.

In this paper, we focus primarily on space-filling branes. The spinor bundle will be replaced by the half-form bundle KΩ\sqrt{K_{\Omega}} with respect to a choice of fiberwise Kähler polarizations Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}. Once this datum is chosen, the line bundle L|FxL|_{F_{x}} becomes a pre-quantum line bundle on the Kähler manifold (Fx,F∇L|Fx,Ω)(F_{x},F_{\nabla^{L}}|_{F_{x}},\Omega). Accordingly, the Dirac operator is replaced by the fiberwise ∂¯\bar{\partial}-operator and the fiber of the mirror B-brane Eˇ(k)\check{E}^{(k)} of the canonical coisotropic brane ℬcc(k)=(X,L⊗k,∇L⊗k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}=(X,L^{\otimes k},\nabla^{L^{\otimes k}}) is given by

H0​(Fx,L|Fx⊗k⊗𝒫|Fx×{yˇ}⊗KΩ),H^{0}(F_{x},L|_{F_{x}}^{\otimes k}\otimes\mathcal{P}|_{F_{x}\times\{\check{y}\}}\otimes\sqrt{K_{\Omega}}),

where Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}. This is where geometric quantization comes in. See Section 4 for details.

2.3. Quotient bundles by fiberwise factors of automorphy

In the following (sub)sections we repeatedly use a standard construction to describe line bundles over XUX_{U} and XU×UXˇUX_{U}\times_{U}\check{X}_{U} for an open subset U⊂BU\subset B.

Consider m∈ℤ>0m\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0} and the trivial Hermitian line bundle (U×ℝm)×ℂ(U\times\mathbb{R}^{m})\times\mathbb{C} over U×ℝmU\times\mathbb{R}^{m}, equipped with a unitary connection ∇\nabla. Suppose we are given a fiberwise group action

∙:(U×ℤm)×(U×ℝm)×ℂ⟶(U×ℝm)×ℂ,\bullet:(U\times\mathbb{Z}^{m})\times(U\times\mathbb{R}^{m})\times\mathbb{C}\longrightarrow(U\times\mathbb{R}^{m})\times\mathbb{C},

covering the action of U×ℤmU\times\mathbb{Z}^{m} on U×ℝmU\times\mathbb{R}^{m} by translations, such that ∇\nabla is ∙\bullet-equivariant. We refer to such an action ∙\bullet as a fiberwise factor of automorphy. By descending ((U×ℝm)×ℂ,∇)((U\times\mathbb{R}^{m})\times\mathbb{C},\nabla) via ∙\bullet, we obtain a Hermitian line bundle with unitary connection over U×𝕋mU\times\mathbb{T}^{m}, where 𝕋m:=ℝm/ℤm\mathbb{T}^{m}:=\mathbb{R}^{m}/\mathbb{Z}^{m}. This is called the quotient bundle on U×𝕋mU\times\mathbb{T}^{m} associated with ∇\nabla and ∙\bullet.

As an important example, the Poincaré bundle (𝒫,∇𝒫)(\mathcal{P},\nabla^{\mathcal{P}}) on X×BXˇX\times_{B}\check{X} arises from this construction. Let U⊂BU\subset B be equipped with integral affine coordinates xx, so that XU×UXˇUX_{U}\times_{U}\check{X}_{U} is identified with U×ℝ4​nU\times\mathbb{R}^{4n} via coordinates (x,y,yˇ)(x,y,\check{y}). On U×ℝ4​nU\times\mathbb{R}^{4n} we consider the trivial Hermitian line bundle endowed with the unitary connection

d−2​π​−1​yˇ⋅d​y.d-2\pi\sqrt{-1}\check{y}\cdot dy.

Define a fiberwise factor of automorphy by: for (λ,λˇ)∈ℤ2​n×ℤ2​n(\lambda,\check{\lambda})\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n}\times\mathbb{Z}^{2n},

(λ,λˇ)∙(x,y,yˇ,v):=(x,y+λ,yˇ+λˇ,e2​π​−1​λˇ⋅y​v).(\lambda,\check{\lambda})\bullet(x,y,\check{y},v):=\left(x,y+\lambda,\check{y}+\check{\lambda},e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}\check{\lambda}\cdot y}v\right).

The associated quotient bundle on U×𝕋4​nU\times\mathbb{T}^{4n} defines the local model of (𝒫,∇𝒫)(\mathcal{P},\nabla^{\mathcal{P}}) on XU×UXˇUX_{U}\times_{U}\check{X}_{U}. These quotient bundles glue together to yield the global Poincaré bundle (𝒫,∇𝒫)(\mathcal{P},\nabla^{\mathcal{P}}).

3. Non-formal quantization on ℬcc(k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}

Equip XX with the holomorphic symplectic structure induced by ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} and let ΩX\Omega_{X} denote the corresponding holomorphic symplectic form, i.e. −2​π​−1⋅Re⁡ΩX-2\pi\sqrt{-1}\cdot\operatorname{Re}\Omega_{X} is the curvature of ∇L\nabla^{L} and Im⁡ΩX=ω\operatorname{Im}\Omega_{X}=\omega. Under our standing assumption 1.4, the main statement of this section is the existence of a canonical strict quantization of holomorphic functions on XX for each level kk. In particular, the asymptotic expansion of these deformed products yields a holomorphic deformation quantization of the holomorphic symplectic manifold (X,ΩX)(X,\Omega_{X}).

We briefly outline the section. Following [17], Subsection 3.1 constructs distinguished local models for the bundle (L,∇L)(L,\nabla^{L}), referred to as skew-Smith forms of ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}. These local models show that the fibers of pp, equipped with the restriction of Re⁡ΩX\operatorname{Re}\Omega_{X}, are mutually symplectomorphic. As a consequence, the induced symplectic structures on the fibers are classified by a diagonal integer matrix 𝑯\boldsymbol{H}, which we call the invariant factor matrix of ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}. In Subsection 3.2, 𝑯\boldsymbol{H} provides an explicit coordinate description of the holomorphic symplectic structure on XX. This yields an atlas of complex coordinates with affine transitions, from which we construct a Moyal-type quantization. Subsection 3.3 presents examples of SYZ fibrations p:X→Bp:X\to B (without singularities) admitting space-filling A-branes that satisfy the standing assumption 1.4. Finally, in Subsection 3.4, we give a proof of the existence of skew-Smith forms of ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}.

3.1. Skew-Smith forms, invariant factors, and complex coordinates

The semi-affine A-brane ℬcc=(X,L,∇L)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}=(X,L,\nabla^{L}) admits an explicit local coordinate description. By Proposition 27 in [17], every point in BB has a neighbourhood UU equipped with data

(3.1) (x,f,g~,H,χ),(x,f,\widetilde{g},H,\chi),

where x=(x1,…,x2​n)x=(x_{1},...,x_{2n}) are integral affine coordinates on UU, f,g~∈𝒞∞​(U,ℝ2​n)f,\widetilde{g}\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathbb{R}^{2n}), HH is a constant skew-symmetric 2​n×2​n2n\times 2n matrix over ℤ\mathbb{Z} and χ:ℤ2​n→U⁡(1)\chi:\mathbb{Z}^{2n}\to\operatorname{U}(1) is an HH-semi-character 111That is, χ​(λ+λ′)=(−1)λ⋅H​λ′​χ​(λ)​χ​(λ′)\chi(\lambda+\lambda^{\prime})=(-1)^{\lambda\cdot H\lambda^{\prime}}\chi(\lambda)\chi(\lambda^{\prime}) for all λ,λ′∈ℤ2​n\lambda,\lambda^{\prime}\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n}.. These data determine (L,∇L)|XU(L,\nabla^{L})|_{X_{U}} as the quotient bundle on U×𝕋2​nU\times\mathbb{T}^{2n} associated with

(3.2) d−2​π​−1​(12​f⋅d​x+g~⋅d​y+12​y⋅H​d​y)d-2\pi\sqrt{-1}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}f\cdot dx+\widetilde{g}\cdot dy+\tfrac{1}{2}y\cdot Hdy\right)

and the fiberwise factor of automorphy λ∙(x,y,v)=(x,y+λ,χ​(λ)​eπ​−1​λ⋅H​y​v)\lambda\bullet(x,y,v)=(x,y+\lambda,\chi(\lambda)e^{\pi\sqrt{-1}\lambda\cdot Hy}v).

In this subsection, we simplify this description and introduce local models for ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} that are better suited for the proof of our main results. These models are described in Proposition 3.1.

For an open subset U⊂BU\subset B equipped with integral affine coordinates xx and an ℝ2​n\mathbb{R}^{2n}-valued function g∈𝒞∞​(U,ℝ2​n)g\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathbb{R}^{2n}), denote by D​gDg the Jacobian matrix of gg with respect to xx.

Proposition 3.1.

Every point in BB admits a neighbourhood UU together with data

(x,g,gˇ,𝑯),(x,g,\check{g},\boldsymbol{H}),

where x=(x1,…,x2​n)x=(x_{1},...,x_{2n}) is an integral affine chart on UU, g∈𝒞∞​(U,ℝ2​n)g\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathbb{R}^{2n}) satisfies D​g=(D​g)TDg=(Dg)^{T}, gˇ∈ℝ2​n\check{g}\in\mathbb{R}^{2n} is constant, and 𝐇=diag⁡(h1,…,hn)\boldsymbol{H}=\operatorname{diag}(h_{1},...,h_{n}) is an n×nn\times n diagonal matrix with h1,…,hn∈ℤ>0h_{1},...,h_{n}\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0} satisfying h1​∣h2∣​⋯∣hnh_{1}\mid h_{2}\mid\cdots\mid h_{n}. With respect to these data, (L,∇L)|XU(L,\nabla^{L})|_{X_{U}} is isomorphic to the quotient bundle on U×𝕋2​nU\times\mathbb{T}^{2n} associated with the unitary connection

d−2​π​−1​(𝒙1⋅𝑯−1​d​𝒙2+(𝒚2+𝒈2)⋅𝑯​d​(𝒚1+𝒈1)),d-2\pi\sqrt{-1}(\boldsymbol{x}_{1}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}d\boldsymbol{x}_{2}+(\boldsymbol{y}^{2}+\boldsymbol{g}^{2})\cdot\boldsymbol{H}d(\boldsymbol{y}^{1}+\boldsymbol{g}^{1})),

and the fiberwise factor of automorphy

(3.3) λ∙(x,y,v)=(x,y+λ,e2​π​−1​(gˇ⋅λ+𝝀2⋅𝑯​(𝒚1+𝒈1))​v).\lambda\bullet(x,y,v)=(x,y+\lambda,e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}(\check{g}\cdot\lambda+\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}(\boldsymbol{y}^{1}+\boldsymbol{g}^{1}))}v).

The proof is largely computational, involving a sequence of coordinate changes and gauge transformations based on the coordinate description introduced at the beginning of this subsection. We postpone the details to Subsection 3.4.

As a consequence of Proposition 3.1, the matrix 𝑯\boldsymbol{H} is an invariant of the brane ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}.

Proposition 3.2.

Let U,U′⊂BU,U^{\prime}\subset B be open subsets with U∩U′≠∅U\cap U^{\prime}\neq\emptyset, equipped respectively with the data (x,g,gˇ,𝐇)(x,g,\check{g},\boldsymbol{H}) and (x′,g′,gˇ′,𝐇′)(x^{\prime},g^{\prime},\check{g}^{\prime},\boldsymbol{H}^{\prime}) as in Proposition 3.1. Then

𝑯=𝑯′.\boldsymbol{H}=\boldsymbol{H}^{\prime}.
Proof.

The charts xx and x′x^{\prime} are related by the coordinate change x=A​(x′+b)x=A(x^{\prime}+b) on U∩U′U\cap U^{\prime} for some A∈GL⁡(2​n,ℤ)A\in\operatorname{GL}(2n,\mathbb{Z}) and b∈ℝ2​nb\in\mathbb{R}^{2n}. The induced coordinate changes on XX are y=A−T​y′y=A^{-T}y^{\prime}. Let

H=(0−𝑯𝑯0)andH′=(0−𝑯′𝑯′0).H=\begin{pmatrix}0&-\boldsymbol{H}\\ \boldsymbol{H}&0\end{pmatrix}\quad\text{and}\quad H^{\prime}=\begin{pmatrix}0&-\boldsymbol{H}^{\prime}\\ \boldsymbol{H}^{\prime}&0\end{pmatrix}.

Comparing the coordinate expression for the restriction of the curvature of ∇L\nabla^{L} stated in Proposition 3.1 onto a fiber of XU∩U′→U∩U′X_{U\cap U^{\prime}}\to U\cap U^{\prime}:

−π​−1​d​y∧H​d​y=−π​−1​d​y′∧H′​d​y′,\displaystyle-\pi\sqrt{-1}dy\wedge Hdy=-\pi\sqrt{-1}dy^{\prime}\wedge H^{\prime}dy^{\prime},

we obtain H=A​H′​ATH=AH^{\prime}A^{T}. The uniqueness of the skew-Smith norm form yields 𝑯=𝑯′\boldsymbol{H}=\boldsymbol{H}^{\prime}. ∎

This motivates the following definitions.

Definition 3.3.

A skew-Smith form of ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} is a Hermitian line bundle with a unitary connection over U×𝕋nU\times\mathbb{T}^{n} of the form as in Proposition 3.1.

Definition 3.4.

The invariant factor matrix of ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} is the diagonal matrix 𝑯\boldsymbol{H} given as in Proposition 3.1. The invariant factors of ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} are the diagonal entries of 𝑯\boldsymbol{H}.

Here and in the sequel, we always denote by 𝑯\boldsymbol{H} the invariant factor matrix of ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}, and by h1,…,hnh_{1},...,h_{n} the invariant factors of ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} with h1​∣h2∣​⋯∣hnh_{1}\mid h_{2}\mid\cdots\mid h_{n}.

We conclude this subsection by providing a coordinate description of the complex structure II induced by ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}.

Proposition 3.5.

Let (x,g,gˇ,𝐇)(x,g,\check{g},\boldsymbol{H}) be data as in Proposition 3.1. Then the functions

(3.4) z:=(z1,…,z2​n)=(𝒙1−−1​𝑯​(𝒚2+𝒈2),𝒙2+−1​𝑯​(𝒚1+𝒈1))z:=(z^{1},...,z^{2n})=(\boldsymbol{x}_{1}-\sqrt{-1}\boldsymbol{H}(\boldsymbol{y}^{2}+\boldsymbol{g}^{2}),\boldsymbol{x}_{2}+\sqrt{-1}\boldsymbol{H}(\boldsymbol{y}^{1}+\boldsymbol{g}^{1}))

form complex coordinates 222Strictly speaking, zz defines complex coordinates on the covering space T∗​UT^{*}U of XUX_{U}, since the fiber coordinates yy are periodic. By abuse of notation, we still treat zz as coordinates on XUX_{U}. on XX with respect to the complex structure II. Moreover, the holomorphic symplectic form ΩX\Omega_{X} is locally given by

(3.5) ΩX=d​𝒛1∧𝑯−1​d​𝒛2.\Omega_{X}=d\boldsymbol{z}^{1}\wedge\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}d\boldsymbol{z}^{2}.
Proof.

Recall that (x,y)(x,y) are coordinates on XUX_{U}. Since 𝑯\boldsymbol{H} is non-degenerate, the functions (z1,…,z2​n)(z^{1},\dots,z^{2n}) define a system of complex coordinates on XUX_{U}, and hence determine a complex structure IUI_{U} on it. Using Proposition 3.1, we compute the curvature of ∇L\nabla^{L}. Together with the condition (D​g)T=D​g(Dg)^{T}=Dg, a direct calculation shows that ΩX\Omega_{X} can be expressed in the form (3.5). In particular, ΩX\Omega_{X} is of type (2,0)(2,0) with respect to IUI_{U}. The non-degeneracy of ΩX\Omega_{X} forces that IUI_{U} is the restriction II to the open subset XU⊂XX_{U}\subset X. ∎

Corollary 3.6.

Let U,U′⊂BU,U^{\prime}\subset B be open subsets with U∩U′≠∅U\cap U^{\prime}\neq\emptyset, equipped with the data (x,g,gˇ,𝐇)(x,g,\check{g},\boldsymbol{H}) and (x′,g′,gˇ′,𝐇′)(x^{\prime},g^{\prime},\check{g}^{\prime},\boldsymbol{H}^{\prime}) respectively, as in Proposition 3.1. Suppose A∈GL⁡(2​n,ℤ)A\in\operatorname{GL}(2n,\mathbb{Z}) and b∈ℝ2​nb\in\mathbb{R}^{2n} define the transition between integral affine charts on BB via

x=A​(x′+b).x=A(x^{\prime}+b).

Then there exists c∈ℝ2​nc\in\mathbb{R}^{2n} such that the induced change of the corresponding complex coordinates z,z′z,z^{\prime} as described in Proposition 3.5 is given by the affine transformation

z=A​(z′+b+−1​c).z=A(z^{\prime}+b+\sqrt{-1}c).
Proof.

Recall that H=A​H​ATH=AHA^{T}, where HH is given by 3.10. Comparing the mixed terms in the coordinate expression for the curvature of ∇L\nabla^{L} given in Proposition 3.1, we deduce that d​(g​(x))=d​(A−T​g′​(x′))d(g(x))=d(A^{-T}g^{\prime}(x^{\prime})). Using the relation y=A−T​y′y=A^{-T}y^{\prime}, together with the definitions of zz and z′z^{\prime} from Proposition 3.5, we then obtain the desired affine transformation. ∎

3.2. A non-formal quantization of the holomorphic symplectic manifold (X,ΩX)(X,\Omega_{X})

We begin by outlining the main constructions in this subsection. Since the coordinate changes in (x,y)(x,y) are affine and compatible with the matrix 𝑯\boldsymbol{H}, the following local expressions for smooth functions f,g∈𝒞∞​(X)f,g\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X) patches together to define a global Poisson bracket on XX:

{f,g}:=12​π​∑i=1nhi−1​(∂f∂yi​∂g∂yn+i−∂f∂yn+i​∂g∂yi).\{f,g\}:=\frac{1}{2\pi}\sum_{i=1}^{n}h_{i}^{-1}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial y^{i}}\frac{\partial g}{\partial y^{n+i}}-\frac{\partial f}{\partial y^{n+i}}\frac{\partial g}{\partial y^{i}}\right).

We may regard {⋅,⋅}\{\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\} as a smooth family of translation-invariant Poisson brackets on 2​n2n-dimensional symplectic tori, parametrized by the base manifold BB. Using this structure, we obtain a corresponding family of quantum tori [39], realized as a non-formal deformation ⋆ℏ\star_{\hbar} of the commutative product on 𝒞∞​(X)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X) for ℏ∈ℝ\hbar\in\mathbb{R}. A key feature of the deformation ⋆ℏ\star_{\hbar} is that, although it is nonlocal in the fiber directions of the SYZ fibration X→BX\to B, it remains local along the base: if either ff or gg vanishes on XUX_{U} for some open subset U⊂BU\subset B, then f⋆ℏgf\star_{\hbar}g also vanishes on XUX_{U}.

Next, observe that the holomorphic coordinate vector fields on XX are given by

(3.6) ∂∂zi=12​(∂∂xi−∑j=1n∂gn+j∂xi​∂∂yn+j+−1​hi−1​∂∂yn+i),\displaystyle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial z^{i}}=\tfrac{1}{2}\left(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{i}}-\textstyle\sum_{j=1}^{n}\tfrac{\partial g^{n+j}}{\partial x_{i}}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial y^{n+j}}+\sqrt{-1}h_{i}^{-1}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial y^{n+i}}\right),
(3.7) ∂∂zn+i=12​(∂∂xn+i−∑j=1n∂gi∂xn+i​∂∂yj−−1​hi−1​∂∂yi),\displaystyle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial z^{n+i}}=\tfrac{1}{2}\left(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{n+i}}-\textstyle\sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{\partial g^{i}}{\partial x_{n+i}}\frac{\partial}{\partial y^{j}}-\sqrt{-1}h_{i}^{-1}\frac{\partial}{\partial y^{i}}\right),

for 1≤i≤n1\leq i\leq n. Consequently, for local holomorphic functions f,gf,g on XX, the bracket becomes

{f,g}=14​π​∑i=1nhi​(∂f∂zn+i​∂g∂zi−∂f∂zi​∂g∂zn+i),\{f,g\}=\frac{1}{4\pi}\sum_{i=1}^{n}h_{i}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial z^{n+i}}\frac{\partial g}{\partial z^{i}}-\frac{\partial f}{\partial z^{i}}\frac{\partial g}{\partial z^{n+i}}\right),

which coincides with the holomorphic Poisson bracket induced by the holomorphic symplectic form ΩX=d​𝒛1∧𝑯−1​d​𝒛2\Omega_{X}=d\boldsymbol{z}^{1}\wedge\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}d\boldsymbol{z}^{2}. We further show that the product ⋆ℏ\star_{\hbar} naturally extends to the space Ω0,∗​(X)\Omega^{0,*}(X) of (0,∗)(0,*)-forms in such a way that the Dolbeault operator ∂¯\overline{\partial} continues to act as a graded derivation. In particular, the subspace 𝒪X​(X)\mathcal{O}_{X}(X) of holomorphic functions is closed under ⋆ℏ\star_{\hbar}. The resulting subalgebra 𝒜ℏ​(X):=(𝒪X​(X),⋆ℏ)\mathcal{A}_{\hbar}(X):=(\mathcal{O}_{X}(X),\star_{\hbar}) yields a non-formal quantization of the holomorphic symplectic manifold (X,ΩX)(X,\Omega_{X}).

We now turn to the details of the construction.

3.2.1. Family of quantum tori

Fix ℏ∈ℝ\hbar\in\mathbb{R}. We begin by working over an open subset U⊂BU\subset B for which ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} admits a skew-Smith form. Let (x,y)(x,y) (resp. zz) be the corresponding (resp. complex) coordinates on XUX_{U}. Let f,g∈𝒞∞​(XU)f,g\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}) be smooth functions, and consider their fiberwise Fourier expansions:

f​(x,y)=∑m∈ℤ2​nf^m​(x)​e2​π​−1​m⋅y,g​(z)=∑m∈ℤ2​ng^m​(x)​e2​π​−1​m⋅y.f(x,y)=\sum_{m\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n}}\widehat{f}_{m}(x)e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}m\cdot y},\quad g(z)=\sum_{m\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n}}\widehat{g}_{m}(x)e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}m\cdot y}.

Following the standard construction of quantum tori via twisted convolution products of Fourier modes, we define a binary operation

⋆ℏ:𝒞∞(XU)×𝒞∞(XU)→𝒞∞(XU),(f,g)↦f⋆ℏg,\star_{\hbar}:\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U})\times\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U})\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}),\quad(f,g)\mapsto f\star_{\hbar}g,

by

(3.8) (f⋆ℏg)​(x,y):=∑q∈ℤ2​n(∑m+m′=qe−ℏ​π​−1​m⋅H−1​m′​f^m​(x)​g^m′​(x))​e2​π​−1​q⋅y,(f\star_{\hbar}g)(x,y):=\sum_{q\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n}}\left(\sum_{m+m^{\prime}=q}e^{-\hbar\pi\sqrt{-1}m\cdot H^{-1}m^{\prime}}\widehat{f}_{m}(x)\widehat{g}_{m^{\prime}}(x)\right)e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}q\cdot y},

where the bicharacter (m,m′)↦m⋅H−1​m′(m,m^{\prime})\mapsto m\cdot H^{-1}m^{\prime} on ℤ2​n\mathbb{Z}^{2n} with HH given in (3.10) is obtained from the fiberwise Fourier transform of the Poisson bracket {⋅,⋅}\{\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\}.

Equip 𝒞∞​(XU)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}) with its compact-open 𝒞∞\mathcal{C}^{\infty}-topology. While Rieffel [40] utilized this construction to develop a framework for strict deformation quantization in the context of C∗C^{*}-algebras, our interests lie in the differential geometric properties of the smooth algebra. For our purposes, it is sufficient to view the one-parameter family {(𝒞∞​(XU),⋆ℏ)}ℏ∈ℝ\{(\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}),\star_{\hbar})\}_{\hbar\in\mathbb{R}} as a deformation quantization of the Fréchet Poisson algebra (𝒞∞​(XU),{⋅,⋅})(\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}),\{\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\}) in the sense of Omori–Maeda–Miyazaki–Yoshioka [34].

Indeed, by employing seminorm estimates on the rapidly decreasing fiberwise Fourier coefficients (cf. [39, 40]), one can show that for each ℏ∈ℝ\hbar\in\mathbb{R}, (𝒞∞​(XU),⋆ℏ)(\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}),\star_{\hbar}) is a Fréchet algebra where the product ⋆ℏ\star_{\hbar} is jointly continuous. Furthermore, for all f,g∈𝒞∞​(XU)f,g\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}), the following conditions are satisfied:

  1. (1)

    The map ℝ→𝒞∞​(XU)\mathbb{R}\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}) given by ℏ↦f⋆ℏg\hbar\mapsto f\star_{\hbar}g is continuous, and f⋆0g=f​gf\star_{0}g=fg.

  2. (2)

    As ℏ→0\hbar\to 0, 1ℏ​(f⋆ℏg−g⋆ℏf)→−1​{f,g}\tfrac{1}{\hbar}(f\star_{\hbar}g-g\star_{\hbar}f)\to\sqrt{-1}\{f,g\}.

Using the following basis of Ω0,∗​(XU)\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U}) over 𝒞∞​(XU)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}):

{d​zi1∧⋯∧d​zil:0≤l≤2​n,1≤i1<⋯<il≤2​n}\{dz^{i_{1}}\wedge\cdots\wedge dz^{i_{l}}:0\leq l\leq 2n,1\leq i_{1}<\cdots<i_{l}\leq 2n\}

We extend ⋆ℏ\star_{\hbar} to Ω0,∗​(X)\Omega^{0,*}(X) in a natural way: for all f,g∈𝒞∞​(XU)f,g\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}) and indices i1,…,ia,j1,…,jbi_{1},...,i_{a},j_{1},...,j_{b},

(f​d​zi1∧⋯∧d​zia)⋆ℏ(g​d​zj1∧⋯∧d​zjb):=(f⋆ℏg)​d​zi1∧⋯∧d​zia∧d​zj1∧⋯∧d​zjb.\left(fdz^{i_{1}}\wedge\cdots\wedge dz^{i_{a}}\right)\star_{\hbar}\left(gdz^{j_{1}}\wedge\cdots\wedge dz^{j_{b}}\right):=(f\star_{\hbar}g)dz^{i_{1}}\wedge\cdots\wedge dz^{i_{a}}\wedge dz^{j_{1}}\wedge\cdots\wedge dz^{j_{b}}.

Using the same basis, we extend the Poisson bracket {⋅,⋅}\{\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\} to Ω0,∗​(XU)\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U}) analogously.

These constructions are compatible with restrictions to smaller open subsets. More precisely, if U′⊂UU^{\prime}\subset U and α,β∈Ω0,∗​(XU)\alpha,\beta\in\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U}), then

(α⋆ℏβ)|XU′=(α|XU′)⋆ℏ(β|XU′)and{α,β}|XU′={α|XU′,β|XU′},(\alpha\star_{\hbar}\beta)|_{X_{U^{\prime}}}=(\alpha|_{X_{U^{\prime}}})\star_{\hbar}(\beta|_{X_{U^{\prime}}})\quad\text{and}\quad\{\alpha,\beta\}|_{X_{U^{\prime}}}=\{\alpha|_{X_{U^{\prime}}},\beta|_{X_{U^{\prime}}}\},

where the right-hand sides are defined using the restricted coordinates on XU′X_{U^{\prime}}.

We also verify that these constructions are independent of the choice of local coordinates. Suppose (x′,y′)(x^{\prime},y^{\prime}) is another coordinate system on XUX_{U} arising from a different skew-Smith form of ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}. Then the fiber coordinates transform as y=A−T​y′y=A^{-T}y^{\prime} for some constant matrix A∈GL⁡(2​n,ℤ)A\in\operatorname{GL}(2n,\mathbb{Z}) satisfying A​H​AT=HAHA^{T}=H. By Corollary 3.6, the corresponding (0,1)(0,1)-forms transform as d​z¯=A​d​z¯′d\overline{z}=Ad\overline{z}^{\prime}. It follows from these transformation rules that both ⋆ℏ\star_{\hbar} and {⋅,⋅}\{\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\} are invariant under such coordinate changes. Consequently, they are well defined on Ω0,∗​(XU)\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U}) for any open subset U⊂BU\subset B.

Theorem 3.7.

For each ℏ∈ℝ\hbar\in\mathbb{R}, the assignment

U→(Ω0,∗​(XU),⋆ℏ,∂¯)U\to(\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U}),\star_{\hbar},\overline{\partial})

defines a sheaf of differential graded algebras on BB. Moreover, for every open subset U⊂BU\subset B and all α,β∈Ω0,∗​(XU)\alpha,\beta\in\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U}), the following convergences hold in the compact-open 𝒞∞\mathcal{C}^{\infty}-topology:

  1. (1)

    α⋆ℏβ→α⋆0β=α∧β\alpha\star_{\hbar}\beta\to\alpha\star_{0}\beta=\alpha\wedge\beta as ℏ→0\hbar\to 0;

  2. (2)

    1ℏ​[α,β]⋆ℏ→−1​{α,β}\tfrac{1}{\hbar}[\alpha,\beta]_{\star_{\hbar}}\to\sqrt{-1}\{\alpha,\beta\} as ℏ→0\hbar\to 0, where [⋅,⋅]⋆ℏ[\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,]_{\star_{\hbar}} denotes the graded commutator of ⋆ℏ\star_{\hbar}.

An immediate consequence of the theorem is that, for any open subset U⊂BU\subset B, the space 𝒪X​(XU)\mathcal{O}_{X}(X_{U}) forms a ℂ\mathbb{C}-subalgebra of (𝒞∞​(XU),⋆ℏ)(\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}),\star_{\hbar}). We denote this subalgebra by 𝒜ℏ​(XU)\mathcal{A}_{\hbar}(X_{U}).

Remark 3.8.

Although 𝒜ℏ​(X)\mathcal{A}_{\hbar}(X) is non-commutative for sufficiently small ℏ\hbar, with semiclassical behaviour 1ℏ​[f,g]⋆→−1​{f,g}\frac{1}{\hbar}[f,g]_{\star}\to\sqrt{-1}\{f,g\} as ℏ→0\hbar\to 0, it need not be non-commutative for all values of ℏ\hbar. For instance, when ℏ=1\hbar=1 and 𝑯\boldsymbol{H} is the identity matrix, the phase factor e−π​−1​m⋅m′e^{-\pi\sqrt{-1}\,m\cdot m^{\prime}} in (3.8) is symmetric in mm and m′m^{\prime}, and hence the resulting product is commutative.

Proof of Theorem 3.7.

As explained above, the assignment U↦(Ω0,∗​(XU),⋆ℏ)U\mapsto(\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U}),\star_{\hbar}) defines a sheaf of ℕ\mathbb{N}-graded algebras on BB. It therefore suffices to argue locally on the base. Accordingly, fix an open subset U⊂BU\subset B equipped with coordinates arising from a skew-Smith form of ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}.

To prove that ∂¯\overline{\partial} is a graded derivation of (Ω0,∗​(XU),⋆ℏ)(\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U}),\star_{\hbar}), it suffices to verify the Leibniz rule on functions. More precisely, for all f,g∈𝒞∞​(XU)f,g\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}) and each coordinate z¯i\overline{z}^{i}, we must show

(3.9) ∂∂z¯i​(f⋆ℏg)=(∂f∂z¯i)⋆ℏg+f⋆ℏ(∂g∂z¯i),1≤i≤2​n.\frac{\partial}{\partial\overline{z}^{i}}(f\star_{\hbar}g)=\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial\overline{z}^{i}}\right)\star_{\hbar}g+f\star_{\hbar}\left(\frac{\partial g}{\partial\overline{z}^{i}}\right),\quad 1\leq i\leq 2n.

To this end, observe that the product ⋆ℏ\star_{\hbar} is invariant under translations along the fibers. Furthermore, by its locality in the base direction, ⋆ℏ\star_{\hbar} is 𝒞∞​(U)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U)-bilinear, where 𝒞∞​(U)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U) acts on 𝒞∞​(XU)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}) via pullback. It follows that the Leibniz rule for ⋆ℏ\star_{\hbar} holds for coordinate vector fields ∂∂xi\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x^{i}}, as well as for vector fields of the form f​∂∂yjf\tfrac{\partial}{\partial y^{j}}, where ff is the pullback of a smooth function on UU. Since each ∂∂z¯i\frac{\partial}{\partial\overline{z}^{i}} can be expressed as a linear combination of such vector fields, (3.9) follows.

For open sets U⊂BU\subset B of the above form, the family {(𝒞∞​(XU),⋆ℏ)}ℏ∈ℝ\{(\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}),\star_{\hbar})\}_{\hbar\in\mathbb{R}} defines a deformation quantization of the Fréchet Poisson algebra (𝒞∞​(XU),{⋅,⋅})(\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}),\{\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\}) in the sense of Definition 1.1 in [34]. In particular, for all f,g∈𝒞∞​(XU)f,g\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(X_{U}), we have

  • •

    f⋆ℏg→f⋆0g=f​gf\star_{\hbar}g\to f\star_{0}g=fg as ℏ→0\hbar\to 0;

  • •

    1ℏ​[f,g]⋆ℏ→−1​{f,g}\tfrac{1}{\hbar}[f,g]_{\star_{\hbar}}\to\sqrt{-1}\{f,g\} as ℏ→0\hbar\to 0.

By construction, the product ⋆ℏ\star_{\hbar} extends to Ω0,∗​(XU)\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U}) in a manner compatible with the wedge product and the grading. The convergence statements (1) and (2) therefore extend immediately from functions to arbitrary (0,∗)(0,*)-forms α,β∈Ω0,∗​(XU)\alpha,\beta\in\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U}). Finally, since these statements are formulated in the compact-open 𝒞∞\mathcal{C}^{\infty}-topology, they globalize to arbitrary open subsets of BB. ∎

Remark 3.9.

We give a string-theoretic insight of the above star product. The fibrewise non-degeneracy of the curvature of ∇L\nabla^{L}, as assumed in Assumption 1.4, provides an identification between the lattice bundles on A- and B-sides. Together with the fiberwise Fourier transform, we can identify holomorphic functions on XX as a subclass of rapidly decreasing smooth functions on the lattice bundle Λ\Lambda on the A-side, exchanging the multiplication and the convolution product. As Λ\Lambda is canonically diffeomorphic to the space of fiberwise geodesic loop space of XX with base point in the zero section, physically speaking, our construction of a deformed product of holomorphic functions on XX can be regarded as a deformation of the product on a based loop space obtained from concatenation of strings. See related ideas in [15, 16, 26].

3.3. Examples

We present three kinds of examples of coisotropic A-branes satisfying Assumption 1.4.

3.3.1. ℝ2×𝕋2\mathbb{R}^{2}\times\mathbb{T}^{2}

Let’s consider B=ℝ2B=\mathbb{R}^{2} with standard affine structure. In this case, X≅(ℂ×)2X\cong(\mathbb{C}^{\times})^{2}. Consider the U​(1)U(1)-line bundle (L,∇L)(L,\nabla^{L}) obtained by the connection

d−2​π​−1​(x1​d​x2+y2​d​y1)d-2\pi\sqrt{-1}(x_{1}dx^{2}+y^{2}dy^{1})

and the fiberwise factor of automorphy

λ∙(x,y,v)=(x,y+λ,e2​π​−1​λ1​y2​v),\lambda\bullet(x,y,v)=(x,y+\lambda,e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}\lambda_{1}y^{2}}v),

for λ=(λ1,λ2)\lambda=(\lambda_{1},\lambda_{2}). The curvature of (L,∇L)(L,\nabla^{L}) is given by

−2​π​−1​F∇L=2​π​−1​(d​x1∧d​x2−d​y1∧d​y2)-2\pi\sqrt{-1}F_{\nabla^{L}}=2\pi\sqrt{-1}(dx_{1}\wedge dx_{2}-dy^{1}\wedge dy^{2})

and I=ω−1​F∇LI=\omega^{-1}F_{\nabla^{L}} has complex coordinates

z1:=x1−−1​y2,z2:=x2+−1​y1.z^{1}:=x_{1}-\sqrt{-1}y^{2},z^{2}:=x_{2}+\sqrt{-1}y^{1}.

This gives a canonical coisotropic brane ℬcc=(X,L,∇L)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}=(X,L,\nabla^{L}) on XX satisfying Assumption 1.4. The invariant factor matrix of this brane is 𝑯=(1)\boldsymbol{H}=\begin{pmatrix}1\end{pmatrix}.

3.3.2. The 44-torus 𝕋4\mathbb{T}^{4} and the Kodaira–Thurston manifold

Consider B=𝕋2:=ℝ2/ℤ2B=\mathbb{T}^{2}:=\mathbb{R}^{2}/\mathbb{Z}^{2}. The standard periodic coordinates x~=(x~1,x~2)\widetilde{x}=(\widetilde{x}_{1},\widetilde{x}_{2}) on BB, together with an integral affine coordinate change x~′=x~+m\widetilde{x}^{\prime}=\widetilde{x}+m for each m∈ℤ2m\in\mathbb{Z}^{2}, define the standard integral affine structure on BB. Let a∈ℕa\in\mathbb{N}. We consider another integral affine structure on BB which is determined by the new coordinates

(x1,x2)=(x~1,x~2+a​(x~1)2)(x_{1},x_{2})=(\widetilde{x}_{1},\widetilde{x}_{2}+a(\widetilde{x}_{1})^{2})

and an integral affine coordinate change for each (m1,m2)∈ℤ2(m_{1},m_{2})\in\mathbb{Z}^{2}:

(x1′x2′)=(102​a​m11)​(x1x2)+(m1m2+a​(m1)2).\begin{pmatrix}x^{\prime}_{1}\\ x^{\prime}_{2}\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\ 2am_{1}&1\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}x_{1}\\ x_{2}\end{pmatrix}+\begin{pmatrix}m_{1}\\ m_{2}+a(m_{1})^{2}\end{pmatrix}.

The cotangent bundle T∗​XT^{*}X then admits a global frame

(α1,α2,α3,α4):=(d​x1,d​x2−2​a​x1​d​x1,d​y1+2​a​x1​d​y2,d​y2)(\alpha_{1},\alpha_{2},\alpha_{3},\alpha_{4}):=(dx_{1},dx_{2}-2ax_{1}dx_{1},dy^{1}+2ax_{1}dy^{2},dy^{2})

satisfying d​α1=d​α2=d​α4=0d\alpha_{1}=d\alpha_{2}=d\alpha_{4}=0 and d​α3=2​a​α1∧α4d\alpha_{3}=2a\alpha_{1}\wedge\alpha_{4}. When a=0a=0, the integral affine structure reduces to the standard one and X≅𝕋4X\cong\mathbb{T}^{4}; when a>0a>0, XX is diffeomorphic to the Kodaira–Thurston manifold [44] (which gives an important example of non-Kähler mirror symmetry; see e.g., [1, 28, 35]).

Consider the quotient bundle associated with the unitary connection

d−2​π​−1​(x1​d​x2+y2​d​y1)d-2\pi\sqrt{-1}(x_{1}dx_{2}+y^{2}dy^{1})

and the fiberwise factor of automorphy

λ∙(x,y,v)=(x,y+λ,e2​π​−1​λ2​y1​v).\lambda\bullet(x,y,v)=(x,y+\lambda,e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}\lambda^{2}y^{1}}v).

Note that

d−2​π​−1​(x1′​d​x2′+(y′)2​d​(y′)1)\displaystyle d-2\pi\sqrt{-1}(x^{\prime}_{1}dx^{\prime}_{2}+(y^{\prime})^{2}d(y^{\prime})^{1})
=\displaystyle= d−2​π​−1​(x1​d​x2+y2​d​y1+d​(a​m1​(x1)2+2​a​(m1)2​x1+m1​x2−a​m2​(y2)2))\displaystyle d-2\pi\sqrt{-1}(x_{1}dx_{2}+y^{2}dy^{1}+d(am_{1}(x_{1})^{2}+2a(m_{1})^{2}x_{1}+m_{1}x_{2}-am_{2}(y^{2})^{2}))

and

λ∙′(x,y,v)=(x,y+λ,e2​π​−1​λ2​(y1−2​a​m2​y2)).\displaystyle\lambda\bullet^{\prime}(x,y,v)=(x,y+\lambda,e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}\lambda^{2}(y^{1}-2am_{2}y^{2})}).

Then via the gauge transformation

exp⁡(2​π​−1​(a​m1​(x1)2+2​a​(m1)2​x1+m1​x2−a​m2​(y2)2)),\exp\left(2\pi\sqrt{-1}\left(am_{1}(x_{1})^{2}+2a(m_{1})^{2}x_{1}+m_{1}x_{2}-am_{2}(y^{2})^{2}\right)\right),

we can glue the local quotient bundles to a global Hermitian line bundle with unitary connection (L,∇L)(L,\nabla^{L}) over XX such that ℬcc=(X,L,∇L)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}=(X,L,\nabla^{L}) is a rank-11 coisotropic A-brane on (X,ω)(X,\omega) satisfying Assumption 1.4. The invariant factor matrix of ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} is 𝑯=(1)\boldsymbol{H}=\begin{pmatrix}1\end{pmatrix}.

3.3.3. Ooguri–Vafa metric, away from the singularity

We give an example that BB is the smooth locus of an SYZ fibration with an affine singularity (cf. [10, 11]). Consider B=ℂ×B=\mathbb{C}^{\times} covered by three contractible open subsets Uα,Uβ,UγU_{\alpha},U_{\beta},U_{\gamma}, where

Uα=\displaystyle U_{\alpha}= {r​e−1​θ:r>0,θ∈(0,3​π2)},\displaystyle\{re^{\sqrt{-1}\theta}:r>0,\theta\in(0,\tfrac{3\pi}{2})\},
Uβ=\displaystyle U_{\beta}= {r​e−1​θ:r>0,θ∈(π,2​π)},\displaystyle\{re^{\sqrt{-1}\theta}:r>0,\theta\in(\pi,2\pi)\},
Uγ=\displaystyle U_{\gamma}= {r​e−1​θ:r>0,θ∈(−π2,π)}.\displaystyle\{re^{\sqrt{-1}\theta}:r>0,\theta\in(-\tfrac{\pi}{2},\pi)\}.

For η∈{α,β,γ}\eta\in\{\alpha,\beta,\gamma\}, let (xη,1,xη,2)(x_{\eta,1},x_{\eta,2}) denote the restriction of the standard real coordinates on ℂ≅ℝ2\mathbb{C}\cong\mathbb{R}^{2} to UηU_{\eta}, and let (Lη,∇Lη)(L_{\eta},\nabla^{L_{\eta}}) denote the quotient bundle on Uη×𝕋2U_{\eta}\times\mathbb{T}^{2} associated with the unitary connection

d−2​π​−1​(12​xη,1​d​xη,2+2​yη2​d​yη1)d-2\pi\sqrt{-1}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}x_{\eta,1}dx_{\eta,2}+2y_{\eta}^{2}dy_{\eta}^{1}\right)

and the fiberwise factor of automorphy

λη∙η(xη,yη,v)=(xη,yη+λ,e4​π​−1​λη2​yη1​v).\lambda_{\eta}\bullet_{\eta}(x_{\eta},y_{\eta},v)=(x_{\eta},y_{\eta}+\lambda,e^{4\pi\sqrt{-1}\lambda_{\eta}^{2}y_{\eta}^{1}}v).

Equip BB with the integral affine structure given by the integral affine coordinate changes:

xα=xβon ​Uα​β;xβ=xγon ​Uβ​γ;(xγ,1,xγ,2)=(xα,1+xα,2,xα,2)on ​Uγ​α.x_{\alpha}=x_{\beta}\quad\text{on }U_{\alpha\beta};\quad x_{\beta}=x_{\gamma}\quad\text{on }U_{\beta\gamma};\quad(x_{\gamma,1},x_{\gamma,2})=(x_{\alpha,1}+x_{\alpha,2},x_{\alpha,2})\quad\text{on }U_{\gamma\alpha}.

Let ϕα​β:(Lβ,∇Lβ)|Uα​β→(Lα,∇Lα)|Uα​β\phi_{\alpha\beta}:(L_{\beta},\nabla^{L_{\beta}})|_{U_{\alpha\beta}}\to(L_{\alpha},\nabla^{L_{\alpha}})|_{U_{\alpha\beta}} and ϕβ​γ:(Lγ,∇Lγ)|Uβ​γ→(Lβ,∇Lβ)|Uβ​γ\phi_{\beta\gamma}:(L_{\gamma},\nabla^{L_{\gamma}})|_{U_{\beta\gamma}}\to(L_{\beta},\nabla^{L_{\beta}})|_{U_{\beta\gamma}} be the identity maps. Note that with respect to the coordinates (xα,yα)(x_{\alpha},y_{\alpha}), (Lγ,∇Lγ)|Uγ​α(L_{\gamma},\nabla^{L_{\gamma}})|_{U_{\gamma\alpha}} is the quotient bundle associated with the unitary connection

d−2​π​−1​(12​(xα,1+xα,2)​d​xα,2+2​(−yα1+yα2)​d​yα1)d-2\pi\sqrt{-1}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}(x_{\alpha,1}+x_{\alpha,2})dx_{\alpha,2}+2(-y_{\alpha}^{1}+y_{\alpha}^{2})dy_{\alpha}^{1}\right)

and the fiberwise factor of automorphy

λα∙γ(xα,yα,v)=(xα,yα+λα,e4​π​−1​(−λα1+λα2)​yα1​v).\lambda_{\alpha}\bullet_{\gamma}(x_{\alpha},y_{\alpha},v)=(x_{\alpha},y_{\alpha}+\lambda_{\alpha},e^{4\pi\sqrt{-1}(-\lambda_{\alpha}^{1}+\lambda_{\alpha}^{2})y_{\alpha}^{1}}v).

Let ϕγ​α:(Lα,∇Lα)|Uγ​α→(Lγ,∇Lγ)|Uγ​α\phi_{\gamma\alpha}:(L_{\alpha},\nabla^{L_{\alpha}})|_{U_{\gamma\alpha}}\to(L_{\gamma},\nabla^{L_{\gamma}})|_{U_{\gamma\alpha}} be the isomorphism of Hermitian line bundles with unitary connection induced by the gauge transformation exp⁡(2​π​−1​(14​(xα,2)2−(yα1)2))\exp\left(2\pi\sqrt{-1}\left(\tfrac{1}{4}(x_{\alpha,2})^{2}-(y_{\alpha}^{1})^{2}\right)\right). Then the data glue to a global Hermitian line bundle with unitary connection (L,∇L)(L,\nabla^{L}) over BB such that ℬcc=(X,L,∇L)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}=(X,L,\nabla^{L}) is a rank-11 coisotropic A-brane on (X,ω)(X,\omega) satisfying Assumption 1.4. The invariant factor matrix of this brane is 𝑯=(2)\boldsymbol{H}=\begin{pmatrix}2\end{pmatrix}.

3.4. The proof of the existence of skew-Smith forms

In this last subsection, we give a detailed proof of Proposition 3.1.

Proof of Proposition 3.1.

We start with the data (x,f,g~,H,χ)(x,f,\widetilde{g},H,\chi) in (3.1).

Step (1): By Assumption 1.4, HH is invertible. We claim that HH can be chosen such that

(3.10) H=(0−𝑯𝑯0),H=\begin{pmatrix}0&-\boldsymbol{H}\\ \boldsymbol{H}&0\end{pmatrix},

where 𝑯\boldsymbol{H} denotes the diagonal matrix diag⁡(h1,…,hn)\operatorname{diag}(h_{1},...,h_{n}) and h1,…,hn∈ℤ>0h_{1},...,h_{n}\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0} are the elementary divisors of HH with h1​∣h2∣​⋯∣hnh_{1}\mid h_{2}\mid\cdots\mid h_{n}. This is because there exists A∈GL⁡(2​n,ℤ)A\in\operatorname{GL}(2n,\mathbb{Z}) such that

H′:=A​H​AT=(0−𝑯𝑯0).H^{\prime}:=AHA^{T}=\begin{pmatrix}0&-\boldsymbol{H}\\ \boldsymbol{H}&0\end{pmatrix}.

Performing an integral affine coordinate change x′=A​xx^{\prime}=Ax (hence y′=A−T​yy^{\prime}=A^{-T}y) and defining f′​(x′)=A−T​f​(x)f^{\prime}(x^{\prime})=A^{-T}f(x), g~′​(x′)=A​g~​(x)\widetilde{g}^{\prime}(x^{\prime})=A\widetilde{g}(x) and χ′​(λ)=χ​(AT​λ)\chi^{\prime}(\lambda)=\chi(A^{T}\lambda), we obtain a new set of data (x′,f′,g~′,H′,χ′)(x^{\prime},f^{\prime},\tilde{g}^{\prime},H^{\prime},\chi^{\prime}) of the same kind. Hence, our claim holds.

Now, given HH being chosen of the form (3.10), there is a canonical semi-character with respect to HH, namely χ0​(λ)=(−1)𝝀2⋅𝑯​𝝀1\chi_{0}(\lambda)=(-1)^{\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{1}}. Hence, χ\chi must be of the form

(3.11) χ​(λ)=χ0​(λ)​e2​π​−1​gˇ⋅λ,for some ​gˇ∈ℝ2​n.\chi(\lambda)=\chi_{0}(\lambda)e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}\check{g}\cdot\lambda},\quad\text{for some }\check{g}\in\mathbb{R}^{2n}.

Step (2): Let g=H−T​g~g=H^{-T}\widetilde{g} so that (3.2) becomes

d−2​π​−1​(12​f⋅d​x+g⋅H​d​y+12​y⋅H​d​y).d-2\pi\sqrt{-1}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}f\cdot dx+g\cdot Hdy+\tfrac{1}{2}y\cdot Hdy\right).

Via a gauge transformation by e−π​−1​g⋅H​ye^{-\pi\sqrt{-1}g\cdot Hy}, (L,∇L)|XU(L,\nabla^{L})|_{X_{U}} is isomorphic to the quotient bundle on U×𝕋2​nU\times\mathbb{T}^{2n} associated with the unitary connection

d−π​−1​(f⋅d​x−g⋅H​d​g+(y+g)⋅H​d​(y+g))d-\pi\sqrt{-1}\left(f\cdot dx-g\cdot Hdg+(y+g)\cdot Hd(y+g)\right)

and the fiberwise factor of automorphy

(3.12) λ∙(x,y,v)=(x,y+λ,χ​(λ)​eπ​−1⋅λ⋅H​(y+g)​v).\lambda\bullet(x,y,v)=(x,y+\lambda,\chi(\lambda)e^{\pi\sqrt{-1}\cdot\lambda\cdot H(y+g)}v).

Step (3): Define F:=12​(D​f−(D​f)T)F:=\tfrac{1}{2}(Df-(Df)^{T}) and G:=D​gG:=Dg. We claim that H−1=−(F+G​H​G)H^{-1}=-(F+GHG) and GT=GG^{T}=G, leaving its proof to Lemma 3.10. Noting that FT=−FF^{T}=-F, this claim implies

d​(f⋅d​x−g⋅H​d​g)=−d​x⋅(F+G​H​G)​d​x=d​x⋅H−1​d​x.d\left(f\cdot dx-g\cdot Hdg\right)=-dx\cdot\left(F+GHG\right)dx=dx\cdot H^{-1}dx.

Shrinking UU if necessary, the 11-form f⋅d​x−g⋅H​d​g−x⋅H−1​d​xf\cdot dx-g\cdot Hdg-x\cdot H^{-1}dx is exact. Hence, via a suitable gauge transformation, (L,∇L)|XU(L,\nabla^{L})|_{X_{U}} is isomorphic to the quotient bundle on U×𝕋2​nU\times\mathbb{T}^{2n} associated with the fiberwise factor of automorphy (3.12) and the unitary connection

(3.13) d−π​−1​(x⋅H−1​d​x+(y+g)⋅H​d​(y+g)).d-\pi\sqrt{-1}(x\cdot H^{-1}dx+(y+g)\cdot Hd(y+g)).

Step (4): Via a final gauge transformation eπ​−1​(𝒙1⋅𝑯−1​𝒙2+(𝒚2+𝒈2)⋅𝑯​(𝒚1+𝒈1))e^{\pi\sqrt{-1}(\boldsymbol{x}_{1}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}\boldsymbol{x}_{2}+(\boldsymbol{y}^{2}+\boldsymbol{g}^{2})\cdot\boldsymbol{H}(\boldsymbol{y}^{1}+\boldsymbol{g}^{1}))}, (L,∇L)|XU(L,\nabla^{L})|_{X_{U}} is isomorphic to the desired quotient bundle on U×𝕋2​nU\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}. ∎

Lemma 3.10.

Let F,G,HF,G,H be as in Step (3) of the proof of Proposition 3.1. Then

H−1=−(F+G​H​G)andG=GT.H^{-1}=-(F+GHG)\quad\text{and}\quad G=G^{T}.
Proof.

The holomorphic symplectic form ΩX\Omega_{X} on XX can be expressed as

ΩX=−12​d​x∧F​d​x+d​x∧GT​H​d​y+12​d​y∧H​d​y+−1​d​x∧d​y.\Omega_{X}=-\tfrac{1}{2}dx\wedge Fdx+dx\wedge G^{T}Hdy+\tfrac{1}{2}dy\wedge Hdy+\sqrt{-1}dx\wedge dy.

It implies that, locally, the complex structure on XX induced by ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} is given by

(3.14) (0−I2​nI2​n0)​(−FGT​H−(GT​H)TH)=(−H​G−H−FGT​H)\begin{pmatrix}0&-I_{2n}\\ I_{2n}&0\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}-F&G^{T}H\\ -(G^{T}H)^{T}&H\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}-HG&-H\\ -F&G^{T}H\end{pmatrix}

as a matrix relative to the frame (∂∂x1,…,∂∂x2​n,∂∂y1,…,∂∂y2​n)(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}},...,\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2n}},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial y^{1}},...,\tfrac{\partial}{\partial y^{2n}}). Denote by ImI_{m} the m×mm\times m identity matrix for any m∈ℤ>0m\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}. As the square of (3.14) is equal to −I4​n-I_{4n}, we obtain

(H​G)2+H​F=−I2​n,G=GT,F​H​G=GT​H​FandF​H+(GT​H)2=−I2​n(HG)^{2}+HF=-I_{2n},\quad G=G^{T},\quad FHG=G^{T}HF\quad\text{and}\quad FH+(G^{T}H)^{2}=-I_{2n}

(indeed, the last two equations can be deduced from the first two). In particular, the first equation implies that H−1=−(F+G​H​G)H^{-1}=-(F+GHG). ∎

4. The mirror B-brane ℬˇcc\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}} via Fourier–Mukai type transforms

In [17], K.-L. Chan, Zhang and the second author of this paper generalized the real Fourier–Mukai transforms (see [5, 31]) of Lagrangian A-branes on XX to arbitrary semi-affine coisotropic A-branes on XX. Taking the canonical coisotropic A-brane ℬcc(k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)} of (X,k​ω)(X,k\omega) as an example, their idea of constructing the mirror B-brane ℬˇcc(k)\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)} associated with ℬcc(k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)} is to twist the tensor product bundle π∗​L⊗k⊗𝒫\pi^{*}L^{\otimes k}\otimes\mathcal{P} with a fiberwise spinor bundle and then take the kernel of the associated family Dirac operator.

In this paper, we establish a slight modification of this construction – instead of using a fiberwise spinor bundle and a family Dirac operator, we first construct a local mirror B-brane on XˇU\check{X}_{U} associated with ℬcc(k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)} for each sufficiently small open subset UU of BB by taking a family geometric quantization of π∗​L⊗k⊗𝒫\pi^{*}L^{\otimes k}\otimes\mathcal{P}, and then glue these local mirror B-branes to a global one by family Blattner–Kostant–Sternberg pairing maps.

Throughout this section, we fix a value k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}.

4.1. Local construction of the mirror B-brane: outline

Consider a fixed open subset U⊂BU\subset B for which (L,∇L)(L,\nabla^{L}) has a skew-Smith form determined by data x,g,gˇx,g,\check{g} described in Proposition 3.1.

4.1.1. An explicit local form of L⊗k⊗𝒫L^{\otimes k}\otimes\mathcal{P}

Identify UU with an open subset of ℝ2​n\mathbb{R}^{2n} via the integral affine chart xx. Via the coordinates (x,y,yˇ)(x,y,\check{y}), we obtain a local trivialization T∗​U⊕T​U≅U×ℝ4​nT^{*}U\oplus TU\cong U\times\mathbb{R}^{4n} which identifies the lattice bundle ΛU⊕ΛˇU\Lambda_{U}\oplus\check{\Lambda}_{U} with U×ℤ4​nU\times\mathbb{Z}^{4n} and hence descends to a diffeomorphism XU×UXˇU≅U×𝕋4​nX_{U}\times_{U}\check{X}_{U}\cong U\times\mathbb{T}^{4n}.
