To avoid confusion, we henceforth let 𝕋ˇ2​n\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n} be another copy of 𝕋2​n\mathbb{T}^{2n} and write

(4.1) XU×UXˇU≅U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n.X_{U}\times_{U}\check{X}_{U}\cong U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}.

On this manifold, it is convenient to introduce the new coordinates (x,u,uˇ)(x,u,\check{u}), where

u=(u1,…,u2​n):=y+g​(x)anduˇ=(uˇ1,…,uˇ2​n):=yˇ−k​gˇ,u=(u^{1},...,u^{2n}):=y+g(x)\quad\text{and}\quad\check{u}=(\check{u}_{1},...,\check{u}_{2n}):=\check{y}-k\check{g},

and take the quotient bundle (𝐋,∇𝐋)(\mathbf{L},\nabla^{\mathbf{L}}) associated with the unitary connection

(4.2) d−2​π​−1​(k​𝒙1⋅𝑯−1​d​𝒙2+k​𝒖2⋅𝑯​d​𝒖1+yˇ⋅d​y)d-2\pi\sqrt{-1}(k\boldsymbol{x}_{1}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}d\boldsymbol{x}_{2}+k\boldsymbol{u}^{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}d\boldsymbol{u}^{1}+\check{y}\cdot dy)

and the fiberwise factor of automorphy

(λ,λˇ)∙(x,y,yˇ,v)=(x,y+λ,yˇ+λˇ,e2​π​−1​(k​gˇ⋅λ+k​𝝀2⋅𝑯​𝒖1+λˇ⋅y)​v).(\lambda,\check{\lambda})\bullet(x,y,\check{y},v)=(x,y+\lambda,\check{y}+\check{\lambda},e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}(k\check{g}\cdot\lambda+k\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}\boldsymbol{u}^{1}+\check{\lambda}\cdot y)}v).

By Proposition 3.1 and the construction of the Poincaré bundle, there exists an isomorphism π∗​L⊗k⊗𝒫|XU×UXˇU≅𝐋\pi^{*}L^{\otimes k}\otimes\mathcal{P}|_{X_{U}\times_{U}\check{X}_{U}}\cong\mathbf{L} of Hermitian line bundles with unitary connection, covering the diffeomorphism (4.1), so that

Γ​(XU×UXˇU,π∗​L⊗k⊗𝒫)≅Γ​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋).\Gamma(X_{U}\times_{U}\check{X}_{U},\pi^{*}L^{\otimes k}\otimes\mathcal{P})\cong\Gamma(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}).

4.1.2. Prequantum line bundles on a symplectic torus parametrized by XˇU\check{X}_{U}

The manifold XˇU≅U×𝕋ˇ2​n\check{X}_{U}\cong U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n} parametrizes a class of prequantum line bundles of the symplectic torus (𝕋2​n,d​𝒚2∧𝑯​d​𝒚1)(\mathbb{T}^{2n},d\boldsymbol{y}^{2}\wedge\boldsymbol{H}d\boldsymbol{y}^{1}) as follows. Given a point in U×𝕋ˇ2​nU\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}, we identity the fiber of the canonical projection U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n→U×𝕋ˇ2​nU\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}\to U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n} over this point with 𝕋2​n\mathbb{T}^{2n}. Then the restriction of 𝐋\mathbf{L} onto this fiber is a prequantum line bundle of (𝕋2​n,d​𝒚2∧𝑯​d​𝒚1)(\mathbb{T}^{2n},d\boldsymbol{y}^{2}\wedge\boldsymbol{H}d\boldsymbol{y}^{1}).

One can obtain an explicit description of this prequantum line bundle via a fixed value of (x,yˇ)(x,\check{y}) representing the given point – such a value is unique up to transforms (x,yˇ)↦(x,yˇ+λˇ)(x,\check{y})\mapsto(x,\check{y}+\check{\lambda}) for λˇ∈ℤ2​n\check{\lambda}\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n}. The prequantum line bundle is indeed isomorphic to the quotient bundle 𝐋(x,yˇ)\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})} of the trivial Hermitian line bundle ℝ2​n×ℂ\mathbb{R}^{2n}\times\mathbb{C} equipped with the unitary connection

d−2​π​−1​(k​𝒖2⋅𝑯​d​𝒖1+yˇ⋅d​y)d-2\pi\sqrt{-1}\left(k\boldsymbol{u}^{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}d\boldsymbol{u}^{1}+\check{y}\cdot dy\right)

by the factor of automorphy

λ∙(y,v)=(y+λ,e2​π​−1​k​(gˇ⋅λ+𝝀2⋅𝑯​𝒖1)​v).\lambda\bullet(y,v)=\left(y+\lambda,e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}k\left(\check{g}\cdot\lambda+\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}\boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)}v\right).

4.1.3. Invariant Kähler polarizations and half-form bundles

Every element Ω=(Ωi​j)∈ℍn\Omega=(\Omega_{ij})\in\mathbb{H}_{n} in the Segal upper half space determines a 𝕋2​n\mathbb{T}^{2n}-invariant complex structure on 𝕋2​n\mathbb{T}^{2n} via the complex coordinates

(4.3) w=(w1,…,wn):=𝑯​𝒚1−Ω​𝒚2,w=(w^{1},...,w^{n}):=\boldsymbol{H}\boldsymbol{y}^{1}-\Omega\boldsymbol{y}^{2},

which is compatible with the symplectic form d​𝒚2∧𝑯​d​𝒚1=−12​d​w∧(Im⁡Ω)−1​d​w¯d\boldsymbol{y}^{2}\wedge\boldsymbol{H}d\boldsymbol{y}^{1}=\tfrac{\sqrt{-1}}{2}dw\wedge(\operatorname{Im}\Omega)^{-1}d\overline{w}. Hence the antiholomorphic tangent bundle T0,1​𝕋2​nT^{0,1}\mathbb{T}^{2n} defines a Kähler polarization. It admits the global frame

∂∂w¯:=(∂∂w¯1,…,∂∂w¯n)=12​(Im⁡Ω)−1​(∂∂𝒚2+Ω​𝑯−1​∂∂𝒚1).\frac{\partial}{\partial\overline{w}}:=\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\overline{w}^{1}},...,\frac{\partial}{\partial\overline{w}^{n}}\right)=\frac{1}{2}(\operatorname{Im}\Omega)^{-1}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\boldsymbol{y}^{2}}+\Omega\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}\frac{\partial}{\partial\boldsymbol{y}^{1}}\right).

We briefly review the construction of half-form bundles for such polarizations (cf. [6]). The canonical bundle K:=⋀nT∗(1,0)​𝕋2​nK:=\bigwedge^{n}T^{*(1,0)}\mathbb{T}^{2n} admits the holomorphic frame dn​wd^{n}w. For another element Ω′∈ℍn\Omega^{\prime}\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, let w′w^{\prime} and K′K^{\prime} denote the corresponding complex coordinates and canonical bundle. There is a natural sesquilinear pairing

hK,K′:Γ​(𝕋2​n,K)×Γ​(𝕋2​n,K′)→𝒞∞​(𝕋2​n)h^{K,K^{\prime}}:\Gamma(\mathbb{T}^{2n},K)\times\Gamma(\mathbb{T}^{2n},K^{\prime})\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(\mathbb{T}^{2n})

defined by

hK,K′​(η,η′)⋅det(−𝑯)⋅d​y1∧⋯∧d​y2​n=(−1)n2​η∧η¯′.h^{K,K^{\prime}}(\eta,\eta^{\prime})\cdot\det(-\boldsymbol{H})\cdot dy^{1}\wedge\cdots\wedge dy^{2n}=\left(\sqrt{-1}\right)^{n^{2}}\eta\wedge\overline{\eta}^{\prime}.

In particular, hK,K′​(dn​w,dn​w′)=det(1−1​(Ω−Ω′¯))h^{K,K^{\prime}}(d^{n}w,d^{n}w^{\prime})=\det\left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{-1}}(\Omega-\overline{\Omega^{\prime}})\right).

Indeed, one can choose a family of holomorphic line bundles over 𝕋2​n\mathbb{T}^{2n} parametrized by ℍn\mathbb{H}_{n} such that, for each Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, the corresponding bundle K\sqrt{K} is a square root of KK and admits a global holomorphic frame dn​w\sqrt{d^{n}w} satisfying dn​w=dn​w⊗dn​wd^{n}w=\sqrt{d^{n}w}\otimes\sqrt{d^{n}w}. For each pair Ω,Ω′∈ℍn\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, this determines a sesquilinear pairing

hK,K′:Γ​(𝕋2​n,K)×Γ​(𝕋2​n,K′)→𝒞∞​(𝕋2​n),h^{\sqrt{K},\sqrt{K^{\prime}}}:\Gamma(\mathbb{T}^{2n},\sqrt{K})\times\Gamma(\mathbb{T}^{2n},\sqrt{K^{\prime}})\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(\mathbb{T}^{2n}),

defined on generators by

hK,K′​(dn​w,dn​w′)=det(1−1​(Ω−Ω′¯)),h^{\sqrt{K},\sqrt{K^{\prime}}}\left(\sqrt{d^{n}w},\sqrt{d^{n}w^{\prime}}\right)=\sqrt{\det\left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{-1}}(\Omega-\overline{\Omega^{\prime}})\right)},

where the square root is taken in the principal branch.

For each Ω\Omega, the pairing hK:=hK,Kh^{K}:=h^{K,K} defines a Hermitian metric on KK for which dn​wd^{n}w is parallel with respect to the Chern connection ∇K\nabla^{K}. Similarly, hK:=hK,Kh^{\sqrt{K}}:=h^{\sqrt{K},\sqrt{K}} defines a Hermitian metric on K\sqrt{K} with Chern connection ∇K\nabla^{\sqrt{K}}, under which dn​w\sqrt{d^{n}w} is parallel. In this way, (K,hK,∇K)(\sqrt{K},h^{\sqrt{K}},\nabla^{\sqrt{K}}) is a square root of (K,hK,∇K)(K,h^{K},\nabla^{K}). We continue to denote by (K,hK,∇K)(\sqrt{K},h^{\sqrt{K}},\nabla^{\sqrt{K}}), as well as by dn​w\sqrt{d^{n}w}, their pullbacks along the projection

U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n→𝕋2​n.U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}\to\mathbb{T}^{2n}.

For any Ω,Ω′∈ℍn\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, the Hermitian metric on 𝐋\mathbf{L} together with hK,K′h^{\sqrt{K},\sqrt{K^{\prime}}} induces a fiberwise sesquilinear pairing

(4.4) ⟨⋅,⋅⟩:Γ​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K)×Γ​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K′)→𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n)\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle:\Gamma(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K})\times\Gamma(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K^{\prime}})\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n})

given by

⟨s⊗dn​w,s′⊗dn​w′⟩​(x,yˇ):=det(1−1​(Ω−Ω′¯))​∫[0,1]2​ns​(x,y,yˇ)​s′​(x,y,yˇ)¯​d2​n​y.\langle s\otimes\sqrt{d^{n}w},s^{\prime}\otimes\sqrt{d^{n}w^{\prime}}\rangle(x,\check{y}):=\sqrt{\det\left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{-1}}(\Omega-\overline{\Omega^{\prime}})\right)}\int_{[0,1]^{2n}}s(x,y,\check{y})\overline{s^{\prime}(x,y,\check{y})}d^{2n}y.

In particular, this defines a fiberwise inner product on Γ​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K)\Gamma(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K}).

4.1.4. Local mirror B-branes as bundles of quantum Hilbert spaces

For a given Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, the pullback of T0,1​𝕋2​nT^{0,1}\mathbb{T}^{2n} defines a CR structure on U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​nU\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}. The induced unitary connection on 𝐋⊗K\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K} is flat along this CR structure, allowing us to define

H0​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K)H^{0}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K})

as the space of smooth sections of 𝐋⊗K\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K} that are flat along this CR structure.

We then construct a complex vector bundle 𝐄ˇ\mathbf{\check{E}} over U×𝕋ˇ2​nU\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n} by specifying its fiber over any point in U×𝕋ˇ2​nU\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}, represented by (x,yˇ)∈U×ℝˇ2​n(x,\check{y})\in U\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{2n}, to be the space

H0​(𝕋2​n,𝐋(x,yˇ)⊗K)H^{0}(\mathbb{T}^{2n},\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})}\otimes\sqrt{K})

of holomorphic sections of 𝐋(x,yˇ)⊗K\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})}\otimes\sqrt{K} with respect to the complex structure associated with Ω\Omega. This construction yields a natural identification

Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)≅H0​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K).\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})\cong H^{0}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K}).

Specifically, this identification induces a continuous 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n})-linear embedding

Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)↪Γ​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K),\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})\hookrightarrow\Gamma(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K}),

where both of the spaces are equipped with the compact-open 𝒞∞\mathcal{C}^{\infty}-topology. In this context, 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}) acts on the right-hand space via pullback.

The fiberwise inner product (4.4) on Γ​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K)\Gamma(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K}) gives rise to a Hermitian metric on 𝐄ˇ\mathbf{\check{E}} and a family orthogonal projection

(4.5) Π:Γ​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K)→Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ),\Pi:\Gamma(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K})\to\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}),

which is 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n})-linear. Denote by ∇\nabla the tensor product of the unitary connections on 𝐋\mathbf{L} and K\sqrt{K}. For any complex vector field ξ\xi on U×𝕋ˇ2​nU\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}, define

(4.6) ∇ˇξ:=Π∘∇ξ^:Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)→Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ),\check{\nabla}_{\xi}:=\Pi\circ\nabla_{\widehat{\xi}}:\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})\to\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}),

where ξ^\widehat{\xi} denotes the canonical lift of ξ\xi with respect to the decomposition

T​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋2​nˇ)=T​(U×𝕋2​nˇ)⊞T​𝕋2​n.T(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}^{2n}})=T(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}^{2n}})\boxplus T\mathbb{T}^{2n}.

While our construction closely parallels that of [17], it yields the following result.

Theorem 4.1.

The triple (U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ,∇ˇ)(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}},\check{\nabla}) defines a semi-affine B-brane on the complex manifold U×𝕋ˇ2​nU\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}, endowed with the complex structure determined by the coordinates zˇ\check{z}. We call it a local mirror B-brane of ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}. Moreover, the curvature of ∇ˇ\check{\nabla} is locally given by

−2​π​−1⋅d​zˇ∧k2​(H−1+−1​D​g)​d​zˇ¯⊗Id𝐄ˇ,-2\pi\sqrt{-1}\cdot d\check{z}\wedge\tfrac{k}{2}\left(H^{-1}+\sqrt{-1}Dg\right)d\overline{\check{z}}\otimes\operatorname{Id}_{\mathbf{\check{E}}},

where HH is as in (3.10) and D​gDg is the Jacobian matrix of gg with respect to xx.

We will provide a computational proof of this theorem in the next subsection.

4.2. Local construction of the mirror B-brane: computational proof

We continue to adopt the notation introduced in the previous subsection. The key idea in the proof of Theorem 4.1 is to transform the space of sections Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}) into a more tractable space in which all relevant data of 𝐄ˇ\mathbf{\check{E}} become explicitly computable.

To this end, we first identify Γ​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋)\Gamma(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}) with the space of smooth ℂ\mathbb{C}-valued functions s∈𝒞∞​(U×ℝ4​n)s\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{R}^{4n}) satisfying the following quasi-periodicity condition: for all λ,λˇ∈ℤ2​n\lambda,\check{\lambda}\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n},

(4.7) s​(x,y+λ,yˇ+λˇ)=e2​π​−1​(k​gˇ⋅λ+k​𝝀2⋅𝑯​𝒖1+λˇ⋅y)​s​(x,y,yˇ).s(x,y+\lambda,\check{y}+\check{\lambda})=e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}(k\check{g}\cdot\lambda+k\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}\boldsymbol{u}^{1}+\check{\lambda}\cdot y)}s(x,y,\check{y}).

4.2.1. Family Weil–Brezin transform

We adapt a standard technique from geometric quantization on symplectic tori — namely, the Weil–Brezin transform [6] — to a family setting. This yields an isomorphism of topological vector spaces

Γ​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K)≅𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝn×ℝˇn)).\Gamma(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K})\;\cong\;\mathcal{C}^{\infty}\bigl(U,\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})\bigr).

Here, the space Γ​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K)\Gamma(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K}) is endowed with the compact-open 𝒞∞\mathcal{C}^{\infty}-topology. On the right-hand side, ℝˇn\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n} denotes an additional copy of ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n}, and 𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝn×ℝˇn))\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) consists of smooth ℂ\mathbb{C}-valued functions on U×ℝn×ℝˇnU\times\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n} that are rapidly decreasing in the ℝn×ℝˇn\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n} variables. This space is endowed with its natural Fréchet topology, defined by seminorms controlling uniform convergence on compact subsets of UU together with the Schwartz decay of all derivatives in the ℝn×ℝˇn\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n} directions (see Appendix A).

To facilitate the construction, we introduce a complete set of representatives for the quotient ℤn/(k​𝑯​ℤn)\mathbb{Z}^{n}/(k\boldsymbol{H}\mathbb{Z}^{n}):

ℤk​𝑯n:={0,…,k​h1−1}×⋯×{0,…,k​hn−1}.\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}:=\{0,\dots,kh_{1}-1\}\times\cdots\times\{0,\dots,kh_{n}-1\}.
Proposition 4.2.

Let Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}. Then there exists an isomorphism of topological vector spaces

(4.8) Γ​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K)→𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝn×ℝˇn)),s↦[s],\Gamma(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K})\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})),\quad s\mapsto[s],

uniquely characterized by the following condition. Writing s=s¯⊗dn​ws=\underline{s}\otimes\sqrt{d^{n}w}, we require that for all (x,y,yˇ)∈U×ℝ4​n(x,y,\check{y})\in U\times\mathbb{R}^{4n},

s¯​(x,y,yˇ)=\displaystyle\underline{s}(x,y,\check{y})= ∑l∈ℤk​𝑯n∑q,qˇ∈ℤn[s]​(x,𝒚2−q+qˇ,𝒖ˇ1−l−k​𝑯​qˇ)\displaystyle\sum_{l\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}}\sum_{q,\check{q}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}[s](x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2}-q+\check{q},\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}-l-k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q})
⋅exp⁡(2​π​−1​(k​gˇ⋅y+𝒖ˇ2⋅(𝒚2−q+qˇ)−𝒖ˇ1⋅𝒈1​(x)+(l+k​𝑯​q)⋅𝒖1)).\displaystyle\cdot\exp\left(2\pi\sqrt{-1}\left(k\check{g}\cdot y+\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{2}\cdot(\boldsymbol{y}^{2}-q+\check{q})-\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}\cdot\boldsymbol{g}^{1}(x)+(l+k\boldsymbol{H}q)\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)\right).

Moreover, this isomorphism is 𝒞∞​(U)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U)-linear.

Proof.

We begin by transforming ss into a function s~∈𝒞∞​(U×ℝ4​n)\widetilde{s}\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{R}^{4n}) via a coordinate shift and phase factor:

(4.9) s~​(x,y,yˇ):=s¯​(x,y,yˇ+k​gˇ)⋅exp⁡(2​π​−1​(𝒚ˇ1⋅𝒈1​(x)−𝒚ˇ2⋅𝒚2−k​gˇ⋅y)).\widetilde{s}(x,y,\check{y}):=\underline{s}(x,y,\check{y}+k\check{g})\cdot\exp\left(2\pi\sqrt{-1}(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}\cdot\boldsymbol{g}^{1}(x)-\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{y}^{2}-k\check{g}\cdot y)\right).

This transformation converts the quasi-periodicity condition into a real-polarized form suitable for the application of the (fiberwise) Weil–Brezin transform: for all λ,λˇ∈ℤ2​n\lambda,\check{\lambda}\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n},

s~​(x,𝒚1+𝝀1,𝒚2,𝒚ˇ1,𝒚ˇ2+𝝀ˇ2)=\displaystyle\widetilde{s}(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{1}+\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{1},\boldsymbol{y}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{2}+\boldsymbol{\check{\lambda}}_{2})= s~​(x,y,yˇ),\displaystyle\widetilde{s}(x,y,\check{y}),
s~​(x,𝒚1,𝒚2+𝝀2,𝒚ˇ1+𝝀ˇ1,𝒚ˇ2)=\displaystyle\widetilde{s}(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{1},\boldsymbol{y}^{2}+\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{\check{\lambda}}_{1},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{2})= exp⁡(2​π​−1​(𝝀ˇ1⋅𝒖1+𝝀2⋅(k​𝑯​𝒖1−𝒚ˇ2)))⋅s~​(x,y,yˇ).\displaystyle\exp\left(2\pi\sqrt{-1}(\boldsymbol{\check{\lambda}}_{1}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^{1}+\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{2}\cdot(k\boldsymbol{H}\boldsymbol{u}^{1}-\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{2}))\right)\cdot\widetilde{s}(x,y,\check{y}).

The map s↦s~s\mapsto\widetilde{s} is an isomorphism of topological vector spaces with respect to the chosen 𝒞∞\mathcal{C}^{\infty}-topologies. Observe that the map (𝒚1,𝒚ˇ2)↦(𝒚1,k​𝑯​𝒚1−𝒚ˇ2)(\boldsymbol{y}^{1},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{2})\mapsto(\boldsymbol{y}^{1},k\boldsymbol{H}\boldsymbol{y}^{1}-\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{2}) induces an automorphism of ℤ2​n\mathbb{Z}^{2n}. Using this observation and applying the fiberwise Weil–Brezin transform, we obtain a unique function [s]∈𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝn×ℝˇn))[s]\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) such that

s~​(x,y,yˇ)=∑𝝀2,𝝀ˇ1∈ℤn[s]​(x,𝒚2−𝝀2,𝒚ˇ1−𝝀ˇ1)⋅exp⁡(2​π​−1​(𝝀ˇ1⋅𝒖1+𝝀2⋅(k​𝑯​𝒖1−𝒚ˇ2))).\widetilde{s}(x,y,\check{y})=\sum_{\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{\lambda}}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}[s](x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2}-\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}-\boldsymbol{\check{\lambda}}_{1})\cdot\exp\left(2\pi\sqrt{-1}(\boldsymbol{\check{\lambda}}_{1}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^{1}+\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{2}\cdot(k\boldsymbol{H}\boldsymbol{u}^{1}-\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{2}))\right).

Decomposing 𝝀ˇ1=l+k​𝑯​qˇ\boldsymbol{\check{\lambda}}_{1}=l+k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q} with l∈ℤk​𝑯nl\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n} and qˇ∈ℤn\check{q}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}, and performing the change of variables (q,qˇ)=(𝝀2+qˇ,qˇ)(q,\check{q})=(\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{2}+\check{q},\check{q}), we obtain

s~​(x,y,yˇ)=∑l∈ℤk​𝑯n∑q,qˇ∈ℤn[s]​(x,𝒚2−q+qˇ,𝒚ˇ1−l−k​𝑯​qˇ)⋅e2​π​−1​(𝒚ˇ2⋅(−q+qˇ)+(l+k​𝑯​q)⋅𝒖1).\widetilde{s}(x,y,\check{y})=\sum_{l\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}}\sum_{q,\check{q}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}[s](x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2}-q+\check{q},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}-l-k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q})\cdot e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{2}\cdot(-q+\check{q})+(l+k\boldsymbol{H}q)\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^{1})}.

Finally, substituting back using (4.9) yields the stated formula for sΩs_{\Omega}.

Since both the transformation s↦s~s\mapsto\widetilde{s} and the fiberwise Weil–Brezin transform are isomorphisms of topological vector spaces, their composition defines the claimed isomorphism. Finally, the 𝒞∞​(U)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U)-linearity is evident. ∎

Based on the expression of s¯\underline{s} stated in Proposition 4.2, a direct computation yields the following formula of the fiberwise sesquilinear pairing (4.4), which we omit the proof.

Proposition 4.3.

Let Ω,Ω′∈ℍn\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}\in\mathbb{H}_{n}. Let s↦[s]Ωs\mapsto[s]_{\Omega} and s′↦[s′]Ω′s^{\prime}\mapsto[s^{\prime}]_{\Omega^{\prime}} denote the isomorphisms corresponding to Ω,Ω′\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}, respectively, as in Proposition 4.2. Then

⟨s,s′⟩​(x,yˇ)=\displaystyle\langle s,s^{\prime}\rangle(x,\check{y})= det(1−1​(Ω−Ω′¯))​∑mˇ∈ℤ2​ne2​π​−1​𝒎ˇ2⋅𝒖ˇ2\displaystyle\sqrt{\det\left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{-1}}(\Omega-\overline{\Omega^{\prime}})\right)}\sum_{\check{m}\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n}}e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}\boldsymbol{\check{m}}_{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{2}}
⋅∫ℝn[s]Ω(x,𝒚2+𝒎ˇ2,𝒖ˇ1−𝒎ˇ1−k𝑯𝒎ˇ2)[s′]Ω′​(x,𝒚2,𝒖ˇ1−𝒎ˇ1)¯dn𝒚2.\displaystyle\cdot\int_{\mathbb{R}^{n}}[s]_{\Omega}\left(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2}+\boldsymbol{\check{m}}_{2},\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}-\boldsymbol{\check{m}}_{1}-k\boldsymbol{H}\boldsymbol{\check{m}}_{2}\right)\overline{[s^{\prime}]_{\Omega^{\prime}}\left(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}-\boldsymbol{\check{m}}_{1}\right)}d^{n}\boldsymbol{y}^{2}.

4.2.2. An explicit description of local mirror B-branes

Since Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}) is constructed from a family of geometric quantizations in Kähler polarization (namely, the fibers of 𝐄ˇ\mathbf{\check{E}}), a natural first attempt to describe this space is to construct a smooth family of orthonormal bases for these quantizations, parametrized by U×𝕋ˇ2​nU\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}, thereby yielding a unitary frame of 𝐄ˇ\mathbf{\check{E}}. However, due to monodromies along the fiber 𝕋ˇ2​n\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}, such a globally smooth choice does not exist in general.

As we shall see, it is nevertheless convenient to introduce a distinguished family of orthonormal bases of the fibers of 𝐄ˇ\mathbf{\check{E}}, parametrized by U×ℝˇ2​nU\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{2n}, as follows.

Definition 4.4.

For each l∈ℤk​𝑯nl\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n} and (x,y,yˇ)∈U×ℝ4​n(x,y,\check{y})\in U\times\mathbb{R}^{4n}, define

σl​(x,y,yˇ):=∑q∈ℤnNΩ​(𝒖2−q,𝒖ˇ1−l)⋅e2​π​−1​(k​gˇ⋅y−𝒈1​(x)⋅𝒖ˇ1+𝒖ˇ2⋅(𝒚2−q)+(l+k​𝑯​q)⋅𝒖1)⊗dn​w,\sigma_{l}(x,y,\check{y}):=\sum_{q\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}N_{\Omega}(\boldsymbol{u}^{2}-q,\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}-l)\cdot e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}(k\check{g}\cdot y-\boldsymbol{g}^{1}(x)\cdot\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{2}\cdot(\boldsymbol{y}^{2}-q)+(l+k\boldsymbol{H}q)\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^{1})}\otimes\sqrt{d^{n}w},

where for all a,b∈ℂna,b\in\mathbb{C}^{n},

(4.10) NΩ​(a,b):=kn4​exp⁡(π​−1​(a+(k​𝑯)−1​b)⋅(k​Ω)​(a+(k​𝑯)−1​b)).N_{\Omega}(a,b):=k^{\frac{n}{4}}\exp\left(\pi\sqrt{-1}(a+(k\boldsymbol{H})^{-1}b)\cdot(k\Omega)(a+(k\boldsymbol{H})^{-1}b)\right).

For each fixed (x,yˇ)(x,\check{y}), σl​(x,⋅,yˇ)\sigma_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y}) defines a smooth section of 𝐋(x,yˇ)⊗K\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})}\otimes\sqrt{K}, which is essentially a classical theta function.

Proposition 4.5.

For each Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, the collection {σl​(x,⋅,yˇ)}l∈ℤk​𝐇n\{\sigma_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y})\}_{l\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}} forms an orthonormal basis of the Hilbert space H0​(𝕋2​n,𝐋(x,yˇ)⊗K)H^{0}(\mathbb{T}^{2n},\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})}\otimes\sqrt{K}). If Ω′∈ℍn\Omega^{\prime}\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, with corresponding orthonormal basis {σl′​(x,⋅,yˇ)}l∈ℤk​𝐇n\{\sigma^{\prime}_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y})\}_{l\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}}, then for all l,l′∈ℤk​𝐇nl,l^{\prime}\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n},

⟨σl​(x,⋅,yˇ),σl′′​(x,⋅,yˇ)⟩=δl​l′,\left\langle\sigma_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y}),\sigma^{\prime}_{l^{\prime}}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y})\right\rangle=\delta_{ll^{\prime}},

where

⟨⋅,⋅⟩:H0​(𝕋2​n,𝐋(x,yˇ)⊗K)×H0​(𝕋2​n,𝐋(x,yˇ)⊗K′)→ℂ\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle:H^{0}(\mathbb{T}^{2n},\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})}\otimes\sqrt{K})\times H^{0}(\mathbb{T}^{2n},\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})}\otimes\sqrt{K^{\prime}})\to\mathbb{C}

denotes the sesquilinear pairing induced by (4.4).

This result is essentially contained in [4, 6], where the authors treat the case of unimodular symplectic forms on 𝕋2​n\mathbb{T}^{2n}. Our setting is only slightly more general, allowing for non-unimodular integral symplectic forms and a more intricate parametrization of the prequantum line bundle 𝐋(x,yˇ)\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})}. The proof of the above proposition is nevertheless standard, and we therefore omit the details.

Using this family of orthonormal bases, together with the fiberwise Weil–Brezin transform established in Proposition 4.2, we obtain a characterization of the space Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}) as

𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn)),\displaystyle\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})),

the space of ℂ\mathbb{C}-valued smooth functions on U×ℝˇnU\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n} that are rapidly decreasing in the ℝˇn\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}-variables, endowed with its standard Fréchet topology (see Appendix A).

Proposition 4.6.

Let Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}. There exists an isomorphism of topological vector spaces

(4.11) Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)→𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn)),s↦⟨s⟩,\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})),\quad s\mapsto\langle s\rangle,

uniquely characterized by the following condition: for all (x,y,yˇ)∈U×ℝ4​n(x,y,\check{y})\in U\times\mathbb{R}^{4n},

(4.12) s​(x,y,yˇ)=∑l∈ℤk​𝑯nσl​(x,y,yˇ)​∑qˇ∈ℤn⟨s⟩​(x,𝒖ˇ1−l−k​𝑯​qˇ)⋅e2​π​−1​qˇ⋅𝒖ˇ2.s(x,y,\check{y})=\sum_{l\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}}\sigma_{l}(x,y,\check{y})\sum_{\check{q}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\langle s\rangle\left(x,\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}-l-k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}\right)\cdot e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}\check{q}\cdot\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{2}}.

Moreover, this isomorphism is 𝒞∞​(U)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U)-linear.

Remark 4.7.

Formula (4.12) shows that for any open subset V⊂ℝ2​nV\subset\mathbb{R}^{2n} contained in the interior of a fundamental domain of 𝕋ˇ2​n\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}, one can construct a unitary frame {sl}l∈ℤk​𝑯n\{s_{l}\}_{l\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}} of 𝐄ˇ\mathbf{\check{E}} over U×VU\times V by choosing ⟨sl⟩\langle s_{l}\rangle to be suitable bump functions in the 𝒚ˇ1\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}-direction. However, such a frame cannot be extended to U×𝕋ˇ2​nU\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n} while preserving smoothness of the associated functions ⟨sl⟩\langle s_{l}\rangle; this reflects the nontrivial topology of 𝐄ˇ\mathbf{\check{E}} along the 𝕋ˇ2​n\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}-direction.

Observe that the coordinate transformation (x,𝒚2,𝒚ˇ1)↦(x,𝒖2+(k​𝑯)−1​𝒚ˇ1,𝒚ˇ1)(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\mapsto(x,\boldsymbol{u}^{2}+(k\boldsymbol{H})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}) induces an automorphism of the topological vector space 𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝn×ℝˇn))\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})). Under this transformation, the Gaussian factor NΩ​(𝒖2,𝒚ˇ1)N_{\Omega}(\boldsymbol{u}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}) becomes independent of 𝒚ˇ1\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1} and is rapidly decreasing with respect to the variable 𝒖2+(k​𝑯)−1​𝒚ˇ1\boldsymbol{u}^{2}+(k\boldsymbol{H})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}. Consequently, for any smooth function ss on U×ℝˇnU\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n} that is rapidly decreasing in the variable 𝒚ˇ1\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}, its product with the Gaussian factor resides in the space 𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝn×ℝˇn))\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})). This allows us to define a continuous ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear embedding ι:𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))→𝒞∞​(U×ℝn×ℝˇn)\iota:\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}))\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}) as:

(ι​(s))​(x,𝒚2,𝒚ˇ1):=s​(x,𝒚ˇ1)⋅NΩ​(𝒖2,𝒚ˇ1).(\iota(s))(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}):=s(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\cdot N_{\Omega}(\boldsymbol{u}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}).
Lemma 4.8.

A function s∈𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝn×ℝˇn))s\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) lies in the image of ι\iota if and only if the expression s​(x,𝐲2,𝐲ˇ𝟏)⋅(NΩ​(𝐮2,𝐲ˇ1))−1s(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}_{1}})\cdot\left(N_{\Omega}(\boldsymbol{u}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\right)^{-1} is independent of the variable 𝐲2\boldsymbol{y}^{2}.

Proof.

The “only if” part follows directly from the definition of ι\iota. For the “if” part, suppose the given expression is independent of 𝒚2\boldsymbol{y}^{2} and denote it by f​(x,𝒚ˇ1)f(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}). In the transformed coordinates introduced above, the Gaussian factor NΩ​(𝒖2,𝒚ˇ1)N_{\Omega}(\boldsymbol{u}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}) depends solely on the coordinate 𝒖2+(k​𝑯)−1​𝒚ˇ1\boldsymbol{u}^{2}+(k\boldsymbol{H})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1} and is independent of 𝒚ˇ1\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}. Since ss is rapidly decreasing in all fiber variables, it follows that ff inherits the necessary rapid decay in the 𝒚ˇ1\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}-variable. Thus, f∈𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) and s=ι​(f)s=\iota(f), which completes the proof. ∎

Proof of Proposition 4.6.

For each s∈Γ​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K)s\in\Gamma(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K}), assign it to a smooth function ⟨s⟩\langle s\rangle on U×ℝn×ℝˇnU\times\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n} by

(4.13) ⟨s⟩​(x,𝒚2,𝒚ˇ1):=[s]​(x,𝒚2,𝒚ˇ1)​(NΩ​(𝒖2,𝒚ˇ1))−1.\langle s\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}):=[s](x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\left(N_{\Omega}(\boldsymbol{u}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\right)^{-1}.

We claim that ss lies in Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)=H0​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K)\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})=H^{0}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K}) if and only if ⟨s⟩\langle s\rangle is independent of the variable 𝒚2\boldsymbol{y}^{2}. Once this claim holds, by Lemma 4.8, the assignment s↦⟨s⟩s\mapsto\langle s\rangle defines a bijective ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear map Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)→𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})).

To establish that this is a topological isomorphism, we note that Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}) and 𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) embed into the Fréchet spaces Γ​(U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐋⊗K)\Gamma(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{L}\otimes\sqrt{K}) and 𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝn×ℝˇn))\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})), respectively, as closed subspaces. It follows from the construction (4.13) that the assignment s↦⟨s⟩s\mapsto\langle s\rangle is continuous with respect to the compact-open 𝒞∞\mathcal{C}^{\infty}-topology and the standard Fréchet topology on these spaces. By the Open Mapping Theorem for Fréchet spaces, this continuous linear bijection is necessarily a homeomorphism.

Now, we prove the claim. Recall that ∇\nabla denotes the tensor product of the unitary connections on 𝐋\mathbf{L} and K\sqrt{K}. Since the required CR structure on U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​nU\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n} is the pullback of T0,1​𝕋2​nT^{0,1}\mathbb{T}^{2n}, the section ss lies in Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}) if and only if

⟨∑j=1nhj−1​Ωj​i​∇∂yjs+∇∂yn+is⟩​(x,𝒚2,𝒚ˇ1)=0for all ​1≤i≤n.\left\langle\textstyle\sum_{j=1}^{n}h_{j}^{-1}\Omega_{ji}\nabla_{\partial_{y^{j}}}s+\nabla_{\partial_{y^{n+i}}}s\right\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=0\quad\text{for all }1\leq i\leq n.

Fix 1≤i≤n1\leq i\leq n. It follows from (4.2) that

∇∂yi=∂∂yi−2​π​−1​(yˇi+k​hi​un+i)and∇∂yn+i=∂∂yn+i−2​π​−1​yˇn+i.\nabla_{\partial_{y^{i}}}=\tfrac{\partial}{\partial y^{i}}-2\pi\sqrt{-1}(\check{y}_{i}+kh_{i}u^{n+i})\quad\text{and}\quad\nabla_{\partial_{y^{n+i}}}=\tfrac{\partial}{\partial y^{n+i}}-2\pi\sqrt{-1}\check{y}_{n+i}.

Direct computations using Proposition 4.2 and (4.13) yield

⟨∇∂yis⟩​(x,𝒚2,𝒚ˇ1)=\displaystyle\left\langle\nabla_{\partial_{y^{i}}}s\right\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})= −2​π​−1​(yˇi+k​hi​un+i)⋅⟨s⟩​(x,𝒚2,𝒚ˇ1),\displaystyle-2\pi\sqrt{-1}(\check{y}_{i}+kh_{i}u^{n+i})\cdot\langle s\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}),
⟨∇∂yn+is⟩​(x,𝒚2,𝒚ˇ1)=\displaystyle\left\langle\nabla_{\partial_{y^{n+i}}}s\right\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})= (∂∂yn+i+2​π​−1​∑j=1nΩi​j​(yˇjhj+k​un+j))​⟨s⟩​(x,𝒚2,𝒚ˇ1).\displaystyle\left(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial y^{n+i}}+2\pi\sqrt{-1}\textstyle\sum_{j=1}^{n}\Omega_{ij}\left(\tfrac{\check{y}_{j}}{h_{j}}+ku^{n+j}\right)\right)\langle s\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}).

Since Ω\Omega is symmetric, i.e. Ωi​j=Ωj​i\Omega_{ij}=\Omega_{ji}, the above equalities imply that s∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)s\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}) if and only if ∂⟨s⟩∂yn+i​(x,𝒚2,𝒚ˇ1)=0\tfrac{\partial\langle s\rangle}{\partial y^{n+i}}(x,\boldsymbol{y}^{2},\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=0 for all 1≤i≤n1\leq i\leq n, i.e. ⟨s⟩\langle s\rangle is independent of 𝒚2\boldsymbol{y}^{2}. Therefore, our claim holds. Moreover, the desired formula follows from Proposition 4.2, (4.13) and Definition 4.4. The 𝒞∞​(U)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U)-linearity is then immediate. ∎

For Ω,Ω′∈ℍn\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, let 𝐄ˇ\mathbf{\check{E}} and 𝐄ˇ′\mathbf{\check{E}}^{\prime} denote the corresponding bundles. The fiberwise pairing ⟨⋅,⋅⟩\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle in (4.4) restricts to a pairing

Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)×Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ′)→𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n).\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})\times\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}^{\prime})\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}).

Let s↦⟨s⟩Ωs\mapsto\langle s\rangle_{\Omega} and s′↦⟨s′⟩Ω′s^{\prime}\mapsto\langle s^{\prime}\rangle_{\Omega^{\prime}} denote the isomorphisms corresponding to Ω,Ω′\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}, respectively, as in Proposition 4.6. Combining Proposition 4.3 with standard Gaussian integral computations, one obtains, for s∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)s\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}) and s′∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ′)s^{\prime}\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}^{\prime}), the formula

(4.14) ⟨s,s′⟩​(x,yˇ)=∑mˇ∈ℤ2​ne2​π​−1​𝒎ˇ2⋅𝒖ˇ2⋅⟨s⟩Ω​(x,𝒖ˇ1−𝒎ˇ1−k​𝑯​𝒎ˇ2)​⟨s′⟩Ω′​(x,𝒖ˇ1−𝒎ˇ1)¯.\langle s,s^{\prime}\rangle(x,\check{y})=\sum_{\check{m}\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n}}e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}\boldsymbol{\check{m}}_{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{2}}\cdot\langle s\rangle_{\Omega}\left(x,\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}-\boldsymbol{\check{m}}_{1}-k\boldsymbol{H}\boldsymbol{\check{m}}_{2}\right)\overline{\langle s^{\prime}\rangle_{\Omega^{\prime}}\left(x,\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}-\boldsymbol{\check{m}}_{1}\right)}.

In particular, this defines a Hermitian metric on 𝐄ˇ\mathbf{\check{E}}.

4.2.3. Proof of Theorem 4.1

Our strategy for proving Theorem 4.1 is to compute the connection ∇ˇ\check{\nabla} explicitly. This requires a detailed understanding of the family orthogonal projection Π\Pi defined in (4.5). Since Π\Pi is 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n})-linear, it suffices to carry out the computation fiberwise using an orthonormal basis of each fiber of 𝐄ˇ\mathbf{\check{E}}.

More precisely, at each point (x,yˇ)(x,\check{y}), the operator Π\Pi restricts to the orthogonal projection

Γ​(𝕋2​n,𝐋(x,yˇ)⊗K)→H0​(𝕋2​n,𝐋(x,yˇ)⊗K)≅𝐄ˇ(x,yˇ),\Gamma(\mathbb{T}^{2n},\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})}\otimes\sqrt{K})\to H^{0}(\mathbb{T}^{2n},\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})}\otimes\sqrt{K})\cong\mathbf{\check{E}}_{(x,\check{y})},

In practice, for any section s∈Γ​(𝕋2​n,𝐋(x,yˇ)⊗K)s\in\Gamma(\mathbb{T}^{2n},\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})}\otimes\sqrt{K}), the projection Π​s\Pi s is given by

(4.15) Π​s=∑l∈ℤk​𝑯n⟨s,σl​(x,⋅,yˇ)⟩⋅σl​(x,⋅,yˇ),\Pi s=\sum_{l\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}}\langle s,\sigma_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y})\rangle\cdot\sigma_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y}),

where σl​(x,⋅,yˇ)\sigma_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y}) are defined in Definition 4.4, forming an orthonormal basis of 𝐄ˇ(x,yˇ)\mathbf{\check{E}}_{(x,\check{y})}.

Proof of Theorem 4.1.

The proof consists of explicit local computations of the connection ∇ˇ\check{\nabla}. We outline the main steps.

Step 1. Let s∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)s\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}). Using (4.2) together with Proposition 4.2, we compute the terms [∇Ys][\nabla_{Y}s] for vector fields of the form Y=∂∂xjY=\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{j}}, ∂∂yj\tfrac{\partial}{\partial y^{j}} or ∂∂yˇj\tfrac{\partial}{\partial\check{y}_{j}}.

Step 2. Using the results of Step 1 and (4.13), we compute ⟨∇Zjs⟩\langle\nabla_{Z_{j}}s\rangle and ⟨∇Z¯js⟩\langle\nabla_{\overline{Z}_{j}}s\rangle, where

Zj=∂∂zˇj=12​(∂∂xj−−1​k​∂∂yˇj)andZ¯j=∂∂zˇ¯j=12​(∂∂xj+−1​k​∂∂yˇj),Z_{j}=\frac{\partial}{\partial\check{z}_{j}}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{j}}-\sqrt{-1}k\frac{\partial}{\partial\check{y}_{j}}\right)\quad\text{and}\quad\overline{Z}_{j}=\frac{\partial}{\partial\overline{\check{z}}_{j}}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{j}}+\sqrt{-1}k\frac{\partial}{\partial\check{y}_{j}}\right),

for 1≤j≤2​n1\leq j\leq 2n. These expressions involve the terms ⟨∇∂yjs⟩\langle\nabla_{\partial_{y^{j}}}s\rangle.

Step 3. Combining Step 2 with the definition of ∇ˇ\check{\nabla} in (4.6), which involves the fiberwise orthogonal projection Π\Pi, we obtain for 1≤i≤n1\leq i\leq n:

⟨∇ˇZi​s⟩=(∂∂zˇi−k​π​gi)​⟨s⟩,\displaystyle\left\langle\check{\nabla}_{Z_{i}}s\right\rangle=\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\check{z}_{i}}-k\pi g^{i}\right)\langle s\rangle,\quad ⟨∇ˇZn+i​s⟩=(12​∂∂xn+i−π​−1​khi​zˇ¯i−k​π​gn+i)​⟨s⟩,\displaystyle\left\langle\check{\nabla}_{Z_{n+i}}s\right\rangle=\left(\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{n+i}}-\pi\sqrt{-1}\frac{k}{h_{i}}\overline{\check{z}}_{i}-k\pi g^{n+i}\right)\langle s\rangle,
⟨∇ˇZ¯i​s⟩=(∂∂zˇ¯i+k​π​gi)​⟨s⟩,\displaystyle\left\langle\check{\nabla}_{\overline{Z}_{i}}s\right\rangle=\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\overline{\check{z}}_{i}}+k\pi g^{i}\right)\langle s\rangle,\quad ⟨∇ˇZ¯n+i​s⟩=(12​∂∂xn+i−π​−1​khi​zˇi+k​π​gn+i)​⟨s⟩.\displaystyle\left\langle\check{\nabla}_{\overline{Z}_{n+i}}s\right\rangle=\left(\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{n+i}}-\pi\sqrt{-1}\frac{k}{h_{i}}\check{z}_{i}+k\pi g^{n+i}\right)\langle s\rangle.

A key point in this step is the identity

Π​(∇∂yjs)=0,1≤j≤2​n.\Pi(\nabla_{\partial_{y^{j}}}s)=0,\quad 1\leq j\leq 2n.

This identity follows from the explicit expressions for ⟨∇∂yjs⟩\langle\nabla_{\partial_{y^{j}}}s\rangle (see the proof of Proposition 4.6), together with (4.15) and standard Gaussian integral computations.

Conclusion. The above formulas, combined with (4.14), show that ∇ˇ\check{\nabla} is unitary. Moreover, by Proposition 3.1, we have ∂gj∂xi=∂gi∂xj\tfrac{\partial g^{j}}{\partial x_{i}}=\tfrac{\partial g^{i}}{\partial x_{j}} for all i,ji,j, which implies that the (2,0)(2,0)- and (0,2)(0,2)-components of the curvature vanish, and that the curvature has the stated form. Hence 𝐄ˇ\mathbf{\check{E}} is a Hermitian holomorphic vector bundle with Chern connection ∇ˇ\check{\nabla}. Therefore, (U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ,∇ˇ)(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}},\check{\nabla}) defines a B-brane. Since it is space-filling and its curvature is of the above form, it is semi-affine. This completes the proof. ∎

4.3. Global construction of the mirror B-brane

In this subsection, we show that the local mirror B-branes glue to form a global one.

We start with fixing an open cover {Uα}\{U_{\alpha}\} of the base manifold BB together with a collection of triples (xα,gα,gˇα)(x_{\alpha},g_{\alpha},\check{g}_{\alpha}) given as in Proposition 3.1 with respect to each UαU_{\alpha}. In particular, xαx_{\alpha} are integral affine coordinates on UαU_{\alpha}. When Uα​β:=Uα∩Uβ≠∅U_{\alpha\beta}:=U_{\alpha}\cap U_{\beta}\neq\emptyset, the coordinate change from xαx_{\alpha} to xβx_{\beta} induces a matrix Aα​β∈GL⁡(2​n,ℤ)A_{\alpha\beta}\in\operatorname{GL}(2n,\mathbb{Z}) with Aα​β=Aβ​α−1A_{\alpha\beta}=A_{\beta\alpha}^{-1} such that d​xβ=Aα​β​d​xαdx_{\beta}=A_{\alpha\beta}dx_{\alpha} (and thus yα=Aα​β−T​yβy_{\alpha}=A_{\alpha\beta}^{-T}y_{\beta}). Furthermore, the cocycle condition

Aγ​α​Aβ​γ​Aα​β=IdA_{\gamma\alpha}A_{\beta\gamma}A_{\alpha\beta}=\operatorname{Id}

holds on each Uα​β​γ:=Uα∩Uβ∩Uγ≠∅U_{\alpha\beta\gamma}:=U_{\alpha}\cap U_{\beta}\cap U_{\gamma}\neq\emptyset.

For each index α\alpha, let 𝐋α\mathbf{L}_{\alpha} be the local model of π∗​L⊗k⊗𝒫\pi^{*}L^{\otimes k}\otimes\mathcal{P} over XUα×UαXˇUαX_{U_{\alpha}}\times_{U_{\alpha}}\check{X}_{U_{\alpha}} given as in the last subsection. Transition maps between the skew-Smith forms of LL induce a collection {ϕα​β}\left\{\phi_{\alpha\beta}\right\} with (α,β)(\alpha,\beta) ranging over pairs of indices satisfying Uα​β≠∅U_{\alpha\beta}\neq\emptyset, where

ϕα​β:𝐋β|Uα​β×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n→𝐋α|Uα​β×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n\phi_{\alpha\beta}:\mathbf{L}_{\beta}|_{U_{\alpha\beta}\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}}\to\mathbf{L}_{\alpha}|_{U_{\alpha\beta}\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}}

is an isomorphism of Hermitian line bundles with unitary connection covering the coordinate change from (xβ,yβ,yˇβ)(x_{\beta},y_{\beta},\check{y}_{\beta}) to (xα,yα,yˇα)(x_{\alpha},y_{\alpha},\check{y}_{\alpha}), such that ϕα​β=ϕβ​α−1\phi_{\alpha\beta}=\phi_{\beta\alpha}^{-1} and that for each triple of indices (α,β,γ)(\alpha,\beta,\gamma) with Uα​β​γ≠∅U_{\alpha\beta\gamma}\neq\emptyset,

ϕα​β∘ϕβ​γ∘ϕγ​α=Id.\phi_{\alpha\beta}\circ\phi_{\beta\gamma}\circ\phi_{\gamma\alpha}=\operatorname{Id}.

We refine the open cover {Uα}\{U_{\alpha}\} of BB to a new cover {Uα;Ω}\{U_{\alpha;\Omega}\}, where Ω\Omega ranges over the Siegel upper half space ℍn\mathbb{H}_{n}, and we set Uα;Ω:=UαU_{\alpha;\Omega}:=U_{\alpha}. For each index α\alpha and each Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, let (𝐄ˇα,Ω,∇ˇα,Ω)\left(\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega},\check{\nabla}^{\alpha,\Omega}\right) denote the corresponding local mirror B-brane constructed in the previous section.

The construction of transition maps between these local mirror B-branes proceeds in two steps. In the first step, we fix an index α\alpha and consider two arbitrary elements Ω,Ω′∈ℍn\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}\in\mathbb{H}_{n}.

Definition 4.9.

Define the family Blattner–Kostant–Sternberg (BKS) pairing map

BKSΩ,Ω′α:(Γ​(Uα×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇα,Ω′),∇ˇα,Ω′)→(Γ​(Uα×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇα,Ω),∇ˇα,Ω)\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}^{\alpha}:\left(\Gamma\left(U_{\alpha}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega^{\prime}}\right),\check{\nabla}^{\alpha,\Omega^{\prime}}\right)\to\left(\Gamma\left(U_{\alpha}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega}\right),\check{\nabla}^{\alpha,\Omega}\right)

to be the 𝒞∞​(Uα×𝕋ˇ2​n)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U_{\alpha}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n})-linear map uniquely determined by the following property: for each (x,yˇ)∈Uα×ℝ2​n(x,\check{y})\in U_{\alpha}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n}, the induced ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear map on fibers:

(𝐄ˇα,Ω′)(x,yˇ)=H0​(𝕋2​n,𝐋(x,yˇ)⊗KΩ′)→H0​(𝕋2​n,𝐋(x,yˇ)⊗KΩ)=(𝐄ˇα,Ω)(x,yˇ),\left(\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega^{\prime}}\right)_{(x,\check{y})}=H^{0}(\mathbb{T}^{2n},\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})}\otimes\sqrt{K_{\Omega^{\prime}}})\to H^{0}(\mathbb{T}^{2n},\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})}\otimes\sqrt{K_{\Omega}})=\left(\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega}\right)_{(x,\check{y})},

is the Blattner–Kostant–Sternberg pairing map.

Similar to the family orthogonal projection (4.5), for any s∈(𝐄ˇα,Ω′)(x,yˇ)s\in\left(\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega^{\prime}}\right)_{(x,\check{y})},

BKSΩ,Ω′α⁡(s)=∑l∈ℤk​𝑯n⟨s,σα,Ω,l​(x,⋅,yˇ)⟩⋅σα,Ω,l​(x,⋅,yˇ),\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}^{\alpha}(s)=\sum_{l\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}}\langle s,\sigma_{\alpha,\Omega,l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y})\rangle\cdot\sigma_{\alpha,\Omega,l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y}),

where σα,Ω,l​(x,⋅,yˇ)\sigma_{\alpha,\Omega,l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y}) are defined in Definition 4.4, forming an orthonormal basis of (𝐄ˇα,Ω′)(x,yˇ)\left(\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega^{\prime}}\right)_{(x,\check{y})}, and ⟨⋅,⋅⟩\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle denotes the sesquilinear pairing of (𝐄ˇα,Ω′)(x,yˇ)\left(\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega^{\prime}}\right)_{(x,\check{y})} and (𝐄ˇα,Ω)(x,yˇ)\left(\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega}\right)_{(x,\check{y})}.

Proposition 4.10.

For all index α\alpha and elements Ω,Ω′∈ℍn\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, BKSΩ,Ω′α\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}^{\alpha} is an isomorphism of Hermitian vector bundles with unitary connection.

Proof.

By Proposition 4.5 (c.f. Subsection 4.1 of [6]), for all l∈ℤk​𝑯nl\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n} and all point (x,yˇ)(x,\check{y}),

BKSΩ,Ω′α⁡(σα,Ω′,l​(x,⋅,yˇ))=σα,Ω,l​(x,⋅,yˇ).\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}^{\alpha}(\sigma_{\alpha,\Omega^{\prime},l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y}))=\sigma_{\alpha,\Omega,l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y}).

It then follows from Proposition 4.6 that, for all s∈Γ​(Uα×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇα,Ω′)s\in\Gamma\left(U_{\alpha}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega^{\prime}}\right),

⟨BKSΩ,Ω′α⁡s⟩Ω=⟨s⟩Ω′.\left\langle\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}^{\alpha}s\right\rangle_{\Omega}=\langle s\rangle_{\Omega^{\prime}}.

Then applying (4.14) and Theorem 4.1 completes the proof. ∎

In the second step, we fix Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n} and consider two indices α,β\alpha,\beta such that Uα​β≠∅U_{\alpha\beta}\neq\emptyset. Let Aα​β⋅ΩA_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega be the action of Aα​βA_{\alpha\beta} on Ω\Omega given as in (B.4). The tensor product of ϕα​β\phi_{\alpha\beta} with the transform:

Kβ,Ω→Kα,Aα​β⋅Ω,dn​wβ,Ωdet(2​Im⁡Ω)4↦dn​wα,Aα​β⋅Ωdet(2​Im⁡(Aα​β⋅Ω))4,\sqrt{K_{\beta,\Omega}}\to\sqrt{K_{\alpha,A_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega}},\quad\frac{\sqrt{d^{n}w_{\beta,\Omega}}}{\sqrt[4]{\det\left(2\operatorname{Im}\Omega\right)}}\mapsto\frac{\sqrt{d^{n}w_{\alpha,A_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega}}}{\sqrt[4]{\det\left(2\operatorname{Im}(A_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega)\right)}},

restricts to an isomorphism of Hermitian vector bundles with unitary connections:

ϕα​β;Ω:(Γ​(Uα​β×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇβ,Ω),∇ˇβ,Ω)→(Γ​(Uα​β×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇα,Aα​β⋅Ω),∇ˇα,Aα​β⋅Ω)\phi_{\alpha\beta;\Omega}:\left(\Gamma\left(U_{\alpha\beta}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\beta,\Omega}\right),\check{\nabla}^{\beta,\Omega}\right)\to\left(\Gamma\left(U_{\alpha\beta}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,A_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega}\right),\check{\nabla}^{\alpha,A_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega}\right)

Now, we combine the two steps to construct a general transition map with the aid of the following proposition.

Proposition 4.11.

Suppose α,β\alpha,\beta are indices with Uα​β≠∅U_{\alpha\beta}\neq\emptyset and Ω,Ω′∈ℍn\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}\in\mathbb{H}_{n}. Then the following diagram commutes:

Proof.

By construction, the transition map ϕα​β;Ω′\phi_{\alpha\beta;\Omega^{\prime}} is induced from the isomorphism ϕα​β\phi_{\alpha\beta} of the underlying family of prequantum line bundles — covering the symplectomorphism of the torus determined by the coordinate change — together with the natural transformation of half-forms corresponding to Ω′↦Aα​β⋅Ω′\Omega^{\prime}\mapsto A_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega^{\prime}. The same holds for ϕα​β;Aα​β−1⋅Ω\phi_{\alpha\beta;A_{\alpha\beta}^{-1}\cdot\Omega}. In particular, these maps are unitary, preserve the connections, and respect the spaces of holomorphic sections.

On the other hand, the operators BKSΩ,Aα​β⋅Ω′α\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,A_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega^{\prime}}^{\alpha} and BKSAα​β−1⋅Ω,Ω′β\operatorname{BKS}_{A_{\alpha\beta}^{-1}\cdot\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}^{\beta} are defined fiberwise via the sesquilinear pairing induced by the Hermitian and holomorphic structures on the half-form–corrected prequantum line bundles. Consequently, each map is canonical and completely determined by these structures.

Combining these two facts, the diagram commutes: applying a BKS pairing map followed by the half-form–corrected prequantum line bundle isomorphism for the target complex structure gives the same result as first applying the isomorphism for the original complex structure and then the corresponding BKS pairing map. This holds because the isomorphisms intertwine the Hermitian structures, the connections, and the spaces of holomorphic sections. ∎

Definition 4.12.

For all indices α,β\alpha,\beta with Uα​β≠∅U_{\alpha\beta}\neq\emptyset and Ω,Ω′∈ℍn\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, define the isomorphism of Hermitian vector bundles with unitary connections

ϕˇα​β;Ω,Ω′:(Γ​(Uα​β×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇβ,Ω′),∇ˇβ,Ω′)→(Γ​(Uα​β×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇα,Ω),∇ˇα,Ω)\check{\phi}_{\alpha\beta;\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}:\left(\Gamma\left(U_{\alpha\beta}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\beta,\Omega^{\prime}}\right),\check{\nabla}^{\beta,\Omega^{\prime}}\right)\to\left(\Gamma\left(U_{\alpha\beta}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega}\right),\check{\nabla}^{\alpha,\Omega}\right)

by

ϕˇα​β;Ω,Ω′:=ϕα​β;Aα​β−1⋅Ω∘BKSAα​β−1⋅Ω,Ω′β=BKSΩ,Aα​β⋅Ω′α∘ϕα​β;Ω′.\check{\phi}_{\alpha\beta;\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}:=\phi_{\alpha\beta;A_{\alpha\beta}^{-1}\cdot\Omega}\circ\operatorname{BKS}_{A_{\alpha\beta}^{-1}\cdot\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}^{\beta}=\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,A_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega^{\prime}}^{\alpha}\circ\phi_{\alpha\beta;\Omega^{\prime}}.

In the statement of the following theorem, we explicitly include the superscript (k)(k) to highlight the dependence of all relevant objects on the level kk.

Theorem 4.13.

For each level k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}, the isomorphisms ϕˇα​β;Ω,Ω′(k)\check{\phi}_{\alpha\beta;\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}^{(k)} satisfy the cocycle condition; hence the local data (𝐄ˇα,Ω(k),∇ˇ(k),α,Ω)(\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega}^{(k)},\check{\nabla}^{(k),\alpha,\Omega}) glue to a semi-affine B-brane

ℬˇcc(k):=(Xˇ(k),Eˇ(k),∇Eˇ(k))\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}:=(\check{X}^{(k)},\check{E}^{(k)},\nabla^{\check{E}^{(k)}})

on Xˇ(k)\check{X}^{(k)}, which we call the mirror B-brane of ℬcc(k)\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}.

Proof.

Let α,β,γ\alpha,\beta,\gamma be indices with Uα​β​γ≠∅U_{\alpha\beta\gamma}\neq\emptyset and Ω,Ω′,Ω′′∈ℍn\Omega,\Omega^{\prime},\Omega^{\prime\prime}\in\mathbb{H}_{n}. Using the compatibility of the family BKS pairing maps with the transition maps (Proposition 4.11) together with the cocycle condition Aα​β​Aβ​γ​Aγ​α=IdA_{\alpha\beta}A_{\beta\gamma}A_{\gamma\alpha}=\operatorname{Id}, the triple composition

(4.16) ϕˇα​β;Ω,Ω′∘ϕˇβ​γ;Ω′,Ω′′∘ϕˇγ​α;Ω′′,Ω\check{\phi}_{\alpha\beta;\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}\circ\check{\phi}_{\beta\gamma;\Omega^{\prime},\Omega^{\prime\prime}}\circ\check{\phi}_{\gamma\alpha;\Omega^{\prime\prime},\Omega}

decomposes as the composition of the following two terms:

  1. (1)

    the triple composition of family BKS pairing maps

    BKSΩ,Aα​β⋅Ω′α∘BKSAα​β⋅Ω′,Aα​β​Aβ​γ⋅Ω′′α∘BKSAα​β​Aβ​γ⋅Ω′′,Ωα,\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,A_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega^{\prime}}^{\alpha}\circ\operatorname{BKS}_{A_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega^{\prime},A_{\alpha\beta}A_{\beta\gamma}\cdot\Omega^{\prime\prime}}^{\alpha}\circ\operatorname{BKS}_{A_{\alpha\beta}A_{\beta\gamma}\cdot\Omega^{\prime\prime},\Omega}^{\alpha},

    which is the identity by the proof of Proposition 4.10; and

  2. (2)

    the triple composition ϕα​β;Aβ​γ​Aγ​α⋅Ω∘ϕβ​γ;Aγ​α⋅Ω∘ϕγ​α;Ω\phi_{\alpha\beta;A_{\beta\gamma}A_{\gamma\alpha}\cdot\Omega}\circ\phi_{\beta\gamma;A_{\gamma\alpha}\cdot\Omega}\circ\phi_{\gamma\alpha;\Omega}, which is also the identity due to the cocycle condition ϕα​β∘ϕβ​γ∘ϕγ​α=Id\phi_{\alpha\beta}\circ\phi_{\beta\gamma}\circ\phi_{\gamma\alpha}=\operatorname{Id}.

Therefore, the composition (4.16) is the identity, as required. ∎

5. The mirror theorem

This section is devoted to the main objective of the paper: establishing a mirror statement asserting the existence of a graded algebra isomorphism

Recall from Theorem 4.13 that the mirror B-brane ℬˇcc(k)=(Xˇ(k),Eˇ(k),∇Eˇ(k))\check{\mathcal{B}}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}=(\check{X}^{(k)},\check{E}^{(k)},\nabla^{\check{E}^{(k)}}) is obtained by gluing local data defined on open subsets of the form U×𝕋2​n≅XU⊂XU\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\cong X_{U}\subset X, where U⊂BU\subset B is as in Proposition 3.1. For each such subset, one chooses a parameter Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, which determines a family of torus-invariant Kähler polarizations and thereby provides an explicit local model of the B-brane.

A natural first approach to constructing the desired algebra isomorphism is via a family of Toeplitz operators, which encode — at least asymptotically — the action of deformation quantization on geometric quantization in Kähler geometry [9, 19, 41].

However, the non-formal quantization constructed in Section 3, which realizes the A-model morphism space HomA⁡(ℬcc(k),ℬcc(k))\operatorname{Hom}_{A}(\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)},\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}^{(k)}), is of Moyal type. As shown in [4] (see also [32]), the Berezin–Toeplitz star product and the Moyal product on a symplectic torus are gauge equivalent, with the gauge transformation governed by the Dolbeault Laplacian. This observation motivates our actual construction: we obtain the desired isomorphism by introducing a suitable twist of the family of Toeplitz operators. Our main result is formulated as follows.

Theorem 5.1.

Let k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}. Then the mirror transform constructed via family twisted Toeplitz operators

ΦU(k):(Ω0,∗​(XU),⋆k−1,∂¯I)→(Ω0,∗​(XˇU(k),End⁡(Eˇ(k))),∘,∂¯End⁡(Eˇ(k)))\Phi_{U}^{(k)}:\left(\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U}),\star_{k^{-1}},\overline{\partial}_{I}\right)\to\left(\Omega^{0,*}(\check{X}_{U}^{(k)},\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)})),\circ,\overline{\partial}_{\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)})}\right)

defines an isomorphism of sheaves of differential graded algebras on BB. Consequently, this induces a graded algebra isomorphism

(H∗​(X,𝒪X),⋆k−1)≅(H∗​(Xˇ,End⁡(Eˇ(k))),∘).(H^{*}(X,\mathcal{O}_{X}),\star_{k^{-1}})\cong(H^{*}(\check{X},\operatorname{End}(\check{E}^{(k)})),\circ).

The construction proceeds via a local-to-global argument (local in the base direction). In Subsection 5.1, we construct the mirror transform Φ(k)\Phi^{(k)} locally. In Subsection 5.2, we glue these local constructions to obtain a global transform. Finally, in Subsection 5.3, we complete the proof of Theorem 5.1.

5.1. Local construction of the mirror transform

Throughout this subsection, we restrict attention to an open subset U⊂BU\subset B over which ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}} admits a skew-Smith form on XU≅U×𝕋2​nX_{U}\cong U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}, and we fix a choice Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}. It is instructive to first examine the construction of family Toeplitz operators.

5.1.1. Motivation: family Toeplitz operators

Definition 5.2.

Let f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}). The family Toeplitz operator associated with ff is defined by

Tf:=Π∘m(πU∗​f):Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)→Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ),T_{f}:=\Pi\circ\operatorname{m}_{(\pi_{U}^{*}f)}:\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})\to\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}),

where Π\Pi denotes the family orthogonal projection and m(πU∗​f)\operatorname{m}_{(\pi_{U}^{*}f)} is multiplication by the pullback of ff along the canonical projection πU:U×𝕋2​n×𝕋ˇ2​n→U×𝕋2​n\pi_{U}:U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}\to U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}.

By construction, for each f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}), the operator TfT_{f} defines a smooth section of End⁡(𝐄ˇ)\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}}) with the property that, for every (x,yˇ)(x,\check{y}), its action on the fiber 𝐄ˇ(x,yˇ)\mathbf{\check{E}}_{(x,\check{y})} coincides with the Toeplitz operator on H0​(𝕋2​n,𝐋(x,yˇ)⊗K)H^{0}(\mathbb{T}^{2n},\mathbf{L}_{(x,\check{y})}\otimes\sqrt{K}) associated with the restriction of ff to the fiber {x}×𝕋2​n\{x\}\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}. Consequently, we obtain a 𝒞∞​(U)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U)-linear map

T:𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)→Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ))T:\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n})\to\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}}))

given by f↦Tff\mapsto T_{f}.

Recall that each fiber x×𝕋2​n{x}\times\mathbb{T}^{2n} carries the symplectic form −12​d​w∧(Im⁡Ω)−1​d​w¯\tfrac{\sqrt{-1}}{2}dw\wedge(\operatorname{Im}\Omega)^{-1}d\overline{w}, where ww are the complex coordinates described in (4.3). To define a gauge transformation connecting the non-formal Moyal product ⋆k−1\star_{k^{-1}} to the fiberwise Berezin–Toeplitz product, we introduce the following second order differential operator on U×𝕋2​nU\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}:

(5.1) Δ=−12​π​∂∂w⋅(Im⁡Ω)​∂∂w¯,\Delta=-\frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{\partial}{\partial w}\cdot(\operatorname{Im}\Omega)\frac{\partial}{\partial\overline{w}},

viewed as a family of Dolbeault Laplacians up to normalization. For each k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}, the expected non-formal gauge transformation is e1k​Δe^{\frac{1}{k}\Delta}. However, this operator corresponds to a backward heat flow and is ill-posed as a map 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)→𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n})\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}).

Nonetheless, its composition with the family Toeplitz map, T∘e1k​ΔT\circ e^{\frac{1}{k}\Delta}, can be computed explicitly on individual Fourier modes.

Lemma 5.3.

Let m=(m1,…,m2​n)∈ℤ2​nm=(m_{1},...,m_{2n})\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n} and f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}) be defined by

f​(x,y)=exp⁡(2​π​−1​m⋅u).f(x,y)=\exp\left(2\pi\sqrt{-1}m\cdot u\right).

Let l,l~∈ℤk​𝐇nl,\widetilde{l}\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n} be such that q~:=(k​𝐇)−1​(l+𝐦1−l~)∈ℤn\widetilde{q}:=(k\boldsymbol{H})^{-1}(l+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}-\widetilde{l})\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}. Then for all (x,yˇ)(x,\check{y}),

Texp⁡(1k​Δ)​(f)​(σl​(x,⋅,yˇ))=exp⁡(2​π​−1k​(−𝒎2⋅𝑯−1​(𝒖ˇ1+12​𝒎1−l~)+k​q~⋅𝒖ˇ2))⋅σl~​(x,⋅,yˇ),T_{\exp\left(\frac{1}{k}\Delta\right)(f)}(\sigma_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y}))=\exp\left(\tfrac{2\pi\sqrt{-1}}{k}\left(-\boldsymbol{m}_{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}+\tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{m}_{1}-\widetilde{l})+k\widetilde{q}\cdot\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{2}\right)\right)\cdot\sigma_{\widetilde{l}}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y}),

where σl​(x,⋅,yˇ)\sigma_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y}) is given in Definition 4.4.

By definition of the Toeplitz operator,

TfΩ​(σl)=∑l~∈ℤk​𝑯n⟨fΩ​σl,σl~⟩​σl~,T_{f_{\Omega}}(\sigma_{l})=\sum_{\widetilde{l}\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}}\left\langle f_{\Omega}\sigma_{l},\sigma_{\widetilde{l}}\right\rangle\sigma_{\widetilde{l}},

where, for brevity, we write σl=σl(x,⋅,yˇ))\sigma_{l}=\sigma_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y})) and fΩ=exp⁡(1k​Δ)​(f)f_{\Omega}=\exp\left(\frac{1}{k}\Delta\right)(f). Hence, the statement reduces to the computation of the matrix coefficients ⟨fΩ​σl,σl~⟩\left\langle f_{\Omega}\sigma_{l},\sigma_{\widetilde{l}}\right\rangle, which are expressed as Gaussian integrals. These computations are analogous to those carried out in the proof of Theorem 5 in [4], although the present setting involves additional parameters and more cumbersome notation. We therefore omit the details.

We also provide an explicit formula for the action of a single Fourier mode on sections of 𝐄ˇ\mathbf{\check{E}} via the composition T∘e1k​ΔT\circ e^{\frac{1}{k}\Delta}.

Lemma 5.4.

Let m∈ℤ2​nm\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n} and define the function f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}) by

f​(x,y)=exp⁡(2​π​−1​m⋅u).f(x,y)=\exp\left(2\pi\sqrt{-1}m\cdot u\right).

Then for all s∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)s\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}),

(5.2) ⟨Texp⁡(1k​Δ)​(f)​s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1)=exp⁡(−2​π​−1k​𝒎2⋅𝑯−1​(𝒚ˇ1+12​𝒎1))⋅⟨s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1+𝒎1).\left\langle T_{\exp\left(\frac{1}{k}\Delta\right)(f)}s\right\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=\exp\left(\tfrac{-2\pi\sqrt{-1}}{k}\boldsymbol{m}_{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}\left(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\right)\right)\cdot\langle s\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}).
Proof.

Recall from Proposition 4.6 that

s​(x,y,yˇ)=∑l∈ℤk​𝑯nσl​(x,y,yˇ)​∑qˇ∈ℤn⟨s⟩​(x,𝒖ˇ1−l−k​𝑯​qˇ)⋅e2​π​−1​qˇ⋅𝒖ˇ2.s(x,y,\check{y})=\sum_{l\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}}\sigma_{l}(x,y,\check{y})\sum_{\check{q}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\langle s\rangle\left(x,\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}-l-k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}\right)\cdot e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}\check{q}\cdot\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{2}}.

For all l∈ℤk​𝑯nl\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}, let l~\widetilde{l} be the unique element in ℤk​𝑯n\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n} such that l+𝒎1−l~∈k​𝑯​ℤnl+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}-\widetilde{l}\in k\boldsymbol{H}\mathbb{Z}^{n}, and let q~​(l)=(k​𝑯)−1​(l+𝒎1−l~)∈ℤn\widetilde{q}(l)=(k\boldsymbol{H})^{-1}(l+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}-\widetilde{l})\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}. By Lemma 5.3,

(Texp⁡(1k​Δ)​(f)​s)​(x,y,yˇ)=\displaystyle\left(T_{\exp\left(\frac{1}{k}\Delta\right)(f)}s\right)(x,y,\check{y})= ∑l∈ℤk​𝑯nσl~​(x,y,yˇ)​∑qˇ∈ℤn⟨s⟩​(x,𝒖ˇ1−l−k​𝑯​qˇ)\displaystyle\sum_{l\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}}\sigma_{\widetilde{l}}(x,y,\check{y})\sum_{\check{q}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\langle s\rangle\left(x,\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}-l-k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}\right)
⋅e2​π​−1k​(−𝒎2⋅𝑯−1​(𝒖ˇ1+12​𝒎1−l~)+k​q~​(l)⋅𝒖ˇ2)+2​π​−1​qˇ⋅𝒖ˇ2.\displaystyle\cdot e^{\frac{2\pi\sqrt{-1}}{k}\left(-\boldsymbol{m}_{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}+\frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{m}_{1}-\widetilde{l})+k\widetilde{q}(l)\cdot\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{2}\right)+2\pi\sqrt{-1}\check{q}\cdot\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{2}}.

For the right hand side of the above equality, we first replace the index qˇ\check{q} by qˇ−q~​(l)\check{q}-\widetilde{q}(l), then apply the equality −l+k​𝑯​q~​(l)=𝒎1−l~-l+k\boldsymbol{H}\widetilde{q}(l)=\boldsymbol{m}_{1}-\widetilde{l}, and finally multiply the summand by the factor e−2​π​−1​𝒎2⋅(k​𝑯)−1​(−k​𝑯​qˇ)e^{-2\pi\sqrt{-1}\boldsymbol{m}_{2}\cdot(k\boldsymbol{H})^{-1}(-k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q})}, which is indeed equal to 11. As a result, it yields the term

∑l∈ℤk​𝑯nσl~​(x,y,yˇ)​∑qˇ∈ℤn⟨s⟩​(x,𝒖ˇ1+𝒎1−l~−k​𝑯​qˇ)​e2​π​−1k​(−𝒎2⋅𝑯−1​(𝒖ˇ1+12​𝒎1−l~−k​𝑯​qˇ))+2​π​−1​qˇ⋅𝒖ˇ2.\displaystyle\sum_{l\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}}\sigma_{\widetilde{l}}(x,y,\check{y})\sum_{\check{q}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\langle s\rangle\left(x,\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}-\widetilde{l}-k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}\right)e^{\frac{2\pi\sqrt{-1}}{k}\left(-\boldsymbol{m}_{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{1}+\frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{m}_{1}-\widetilde{l}-k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q})\right)+2\pi\sqrt{-1}\check{q}\cdot\boldsymbol{\check{u}}_{2}}.

Since l↦l~l\mapsto\widetilde{l} forms a bijection ℤk​𝑯n→ℤk​𝑯n\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}\to\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}, we can replace l~\widetilde{l} by ll in the summand of the above term, concluding the proof. ∎

In particular, for m∈ℤ2​nm\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n} and the function f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}) defined by

f​(z)=e2​π​(−𝒎2⋅𝑯−1​𝒛1+𝒎1⋅𝑯−1​𝒛2),f(z)=e^{2\pi(-\boldsymbol{m}_{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}\boldsymbol{z}^{1}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}\boldsymbol{z}^{2})},

we have for all s∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)s\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}),

⟨Texp⁡(1k​Δ)​(f)​s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1)=e2​π​(−𝒎2⋅𝑯−1​(𝒛ˇ1+−12​k​𝒎1)+𝒎1⋅𝑯−1​𝒙2)⋅⟨s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1+𝒎1).\left\langle T_{\exp\left(\frac{1}{k}\Delta\right)(f)}s\right\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=e^{2\pi\left(-\boldsymbol{m}_{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}\left(\boldsymbol{\check{z}}_{1}+\frac{\sqrt{-1}}{2k}\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\right)+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}\boldsymbol{x}_{2}\right)}\cdot\langle s\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}).

Our next goal is to show that the composition T∘e1k​ΔT\circ e^{\frac{1}{k}\Delta} extends to a map from 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}) to Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ))\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}})). We begin with some preparatory observations.

5.1.2. Operator-theoretic characterization of Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ))\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}}))

In Proposition 4.6, we established an isomorphism of topological vector spaces

Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)→𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn)),s↦⟨s⟩.\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})),\quad s\mapsto\langle s\rangle.

This induces an injective ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear map

(5.3) Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ))→Endℂ⁡(𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))),Ψ↦⟨Ψ⟩,\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}}))\to\operatorname{End}_{\mathbb{C}}(\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}))),\quad\Psi\mapsto\langle\Psi\rangle,

where ⟨Ψ⟩\langle\Psi\rangle is the unique ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear operator satisfying ⟨Ψ⟩​⟨s⟩=⟨Ψ​(s)⟩\langle\Psi\rangle\langle s\rangle=\langle\Psi(s)\rangle for all s∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)s\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}).

We now reformulate Lemma 5.4. For 𝒎1∈ℤn\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}, define the shift operator

(5.4) S𝒎1:𝒞∞​(U×ℝˇn)→𝒞∞​(U×ℝˇn),S𝒎1⁡(s)​(x,𝒚ˇ1)=s​(x,𝒚ˇ1+𝒎1).\operatorname{S}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}:\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}),\quad\operatorname{S}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(s)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=s(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}).

For a function f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}), consider its fiberwise Fourier expansion

(5.5) f​(x,y)=∑𝒎1∈ℤnf𝒎1​(x,𝒖2)⋅e2​π​−1​𝒎1⋅𝒖1,f(x,y)=\sum_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}f_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(x,\boldsymbol{u}^{2})\cdot e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^{1}},

where f𝒎1∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋n)f_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{n}). Define the rescaled torus 𝕋ˇk​𝑯n:=ℝˇn/(k​𝑯​ℤn)\check{\mathbb{T}}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}:=\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}/(k\boldsymbol{H}\mathbb{Z}^{n}), and associate to each f𝒎1f_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}} a function fˇ𝒎1∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇk​𝑯n)\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}) by

(5.6) fˇ𝒎1​(x,𝒚ˇ1):=f𝒎1​(x,−1k​𝑯−1​(𝒚ˇ1+12​𝒎1)).\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}):=f_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(x,-\tfrac{1}{k}\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{m}_{1})).

By Lemma 5.4, if only finitely many f𝒎1f_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}} are nonzero and each has finitely many nonzero fiberwise Fourier modes in the 𝒖2\boldsymbol{u}^{2}-variable, then

(5.7) ⟨Texp⁡(1k​Δ)​(f)⟩=∑𝒎1∈ℤnfˇ𝒎1​S𝒎1.\left\langle T_{\exp\left(\frac{1}{k}\Delta\right)(f)}\right\rangle=\sum_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}\operatorname{S}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}.

Here, by a slight abuse of notation, fˇ𝒎1\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}} denotes the operator of multiplication by this function, and S𝒎1\operatorname{S}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}} its restriction to 𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})).

Our strategy for constructing the local mirror transform is to show that, for a general function f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}), the right-hand side of (5.7) remains well-defined and lies in the image of Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ))\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}})) under the injective map (5.3). To this end, we first characterize this image. As before, we identify functions f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇn)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{n}) with periodic smooth ℂ\mathbb{C}-valued functions on U×ℝˇnU\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}.

Lemma 5.5.

Let Ξ:𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))→𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))\Xi:\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}))\to\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) be a ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear operator. Then there exists Ψ∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ))\Psi\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}})) such that Ξ=⟨Ψ⟩\Xi=\langle\Psi\rangle if and only if Ξ\Xi satisfies the following conditions:

  • •

    Ξ\Xi is continuous;

  • •

    for all f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇn)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{n}) and s∈𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))s\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})), Ξ​(f⋅s)=f⋅Ξ​(s)\Xi(f\cdot s)=f\cdot\Xi(s);

  • •

    for all qˇ∈ℤn\check{q}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}, Ξ∘Sk​𝑯​qˇ=Sk​𝑯​qˇ∘Ξ\Xi\circ\operatorname{S}_{k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}}=\operatorname{S}_{k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}}\circ\Xi, where Sk​𝑯​qˇ\operatorname{S}_{k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}} is the shift operator defined in (5.4).

Proof.

We first record a common observation. Using (4.12), commutation with multiplication by smooth ℂ\mathbb{C}-valued functions on U×𝕋ˇ2​nU\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n} independent of 𝒚ˇ2\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{2} together with commutation with multiplication by functions of the form e2​π​−1​𝒎ˇ2⋅𝒚ˇ2e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}\boldsymbol{\check{m}}^{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{2}} with 𝒎ˇ2∈ℤn\boldsymbol{\check{m}}^{2}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n} is equivalent to the module property with respect to 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇn)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{n}) and to commutation with the shift operators Sk​𝑯​qˇ\operatorname{S}_{k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}}.

Suppose Ψ∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ))\Psi\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}})). Via the isomorphism (4.11), the operator

Ψ:Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)→Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)\Psi:\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})\to\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})

induces a continuous operator Ξ:=⟨Ψ⟩\Xi:=\langle\Psi\rangle. Moreover, Ψ\Psi is 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n})-linear. it commutes with multiplication by all smooth functions, and in particular with those appearing in the above observation. The desired properties of Ξ\Xi therefore follow.

Conversely, suppose Ξ\Xi satisfies the stated conditions, and let Ψ\Psi be the corresponding operator under (4.11). By the assumptions on Ξ\Xi and the observation above, Ψ\Psi commutes with multiplication by the same class of functions. Since these functions generate a dense subalgebra of 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n}), continuity implies that Ψ\Psi commutes with multiplication by all smooth functions. Hence Ψ\Psi is 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n})-linear, so Ψ∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ))\Psi\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}})) and Ξ=⟨Ψ⟩\Xi=\langle\Psi\rangle. ∎

5.1.3. Family twisted Toeplitz operators

We equip both 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}) and Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}) with the compact-open 𝒞∞\mathcal{C}^{\infty}-topology. On 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}), this topology may be described in terms of the fiberwise Fourier transform. It is induced by the family of seminorms defined as follows: for every compact subset K⊂UK\subset U and multi-indices μ∈ℕ2​n\mu\in\mathbb{N}^{2n} and ν,η∈ℕn\nu,\eta\in\mathbb{N}^{n},

∥f∥K,μ,ν,η:=sup(x,𝒚ˇ1,𝒎1)∈K×ℝˇn×ℤn|(𝒎1)η⋅∂|μ|+|ν|fˇ𝒎1∂xμ​∂(𝒚ˇ1)ν​(x,𝒚ˇ1)|.\lVert f\rVert_{K,\mu,\nu,\eta}:=\sup_{(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1},\boldsymbol{m}_{1})\in K\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}\times\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\left\lvert(\boldsymbol{m}_{1})^{\eta}\cdot\frac{\partial^{\lvert\mu\rvert+\lvert\nu\rvert}\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}}{\partial x^{\mu}\partial(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})^{\nu}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\right\rvert.

On the other hand, recall that the standard Fréchet topology on 𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) is induced by the seminorms: for every compact subset K⊂UK\subset U and multi-indices μ∈ℕ2​n\mu\in\mathbb{N}^{2n} and ν,η∈ℕn\nu,\eta\in\mathbb{N}^{n},

∥s∥K,μ,ν,η:=sup(x,𝒚ˇ1)∈K×ℝˇn|(𝒚ˇ1)η⋅∂|μ|+|ν|s∂xμ​∂(𝒚ˇ1)ν​(x,𝒚ˇ1)|.\lVert s\rVert_{K,\mu,\nu,\eta}:=\sup_{(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\in K\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}}\left\lvert(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})^{\eta}\cdot\frac{\partial^{\lvert\mu\rvert+\lvert\nu\rvert}s}{\partial x^{\mu}\partial(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})^{\nu}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\right\rvert.
Proposition 5.6.

Let f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}) and s∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)s\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}). Then the series

(5.8) ∑m∈ℤ2​nfˇ𝒎1⋅S𝒎1⁡(⟨s⟩)\sum_{m\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n}}\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}\cdot\operatorname{S}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(\langle s\rangle)

converges absolutely in 𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})), where fˇ𝐦1\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}} are defined in (5.6). Consequently, there is a well defined 𝒞∞​(U)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U)-linear map

(5.9) Φ:𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)→Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ)),f↦Φf,\Phi:\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n})\to\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}})),\quad f\mapsto\Phi_{f},

where for all s∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)s\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}), ⟨Φf​(s)⟩\langle\Phi_{f}(s)\rangle is given by (5.8), and the map

(5.10) 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)×Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)→Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ),(f,s)↦Φf​s,\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n})\times\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})\to\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}),\quad(f,s)\mapsto\Phi_{f}s,

is continuous.

Definition 5.7.

Let f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}). The family twisted Toeplitz operator associated with ff is the 𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n})-linear map

Φf:Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)→Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)\Phi_{f}:\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})\to\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}})

defined in (5.9).

Proof of Proposition 5.6.

Fix a compact subset K⊂UK\subset U and multi-indices μ∈ℕ2​n\mu\in\mathbb{N}^{2n} and ν,η∈ℕn\nu,\eta\in\mathbb{N}^{n}. For each q∈ℤnq\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}, define a multi-index 𝟏q∈ℕn\boldsymbol{1}_{q}\in\mathbb{N}^{n} by 𝟏q​(i)=1\boldsymbol{1}_{q}(i)=1 for qi≠0q_{i}\neq 0; 𝟏q​(i)=0\boldsymbol{1}_{q}(i)=0 otherwise.

Let CfC_{f} be the maximum of all seminorms ∥f∥K,μ′,ν′,η′\lVert f\rVert_{K,\mu^{\prime},\nu^{\prime},\eta^{\prime}} with μ′≤μ\mu^{\prime}\leq\mu, ν′≤ν\nu^{\prime}\leq\nu and |η′|≤|η|+2​n\lvert\eta^{\prime}\rvert\leq\lvert\eta\rvert+2n. Let CsC_{s} be the maximum of all seminorms ∥⟨s⟩∥K,μ′,ν′,η′\lVert\langle s\rangle\rVert_{K,\mu^{\prime},\nu^{\prime},\eta^{\prime}} with μ′≤μ\mu^{\prime}\leq\mu, ν′≤ν\nu^{\prime}\leq\nu and η′≤η\eta^{\prime}\leq\eta. Then

sup(x,𝒚ˇ1)∈K×ℝn|(𝒎1)η′⋅∂|μ′|+|ν′|fˇ𝒎1∂xμ′​∂(𝒚ˇ1)ν′​(x,𝒚ˇ1)|≤1|(𝒎1)2​𝟏𝒎1|⋅Cf\sup_{(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\in K\times\mathbb{R}^{n}}\left\lvert(\boldsymbol{m}_{1})^{\eta^{\prime}}\cdot\frac{\partial^{\lvert\mu^{\prime}\rvert+\lvert\nu^{\prime}\rvert}\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}}{\partial x^{\mu^{\prime}}\partial(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})^{\nu^{\prime}}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\right\rvert\leq\frac{1}{\lvert(\boldsymbol{m}_{1})^{2\boldsymbol{1}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}}\rvert}\cdot C_{f}

for all 𝒎1∈ℤn\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n} and all multi-indices μ′∈ℕ2​n\mu^{\prime}\in\mathbb{N}^{2n} and ν′,η′∈ℕn\nu^{\prime},\eta^{\prime}\in\mathbb{N}^{n} with μ′≤μ\mu^{\prime}\leq\mu, ν′≤ν\nu^{\prime}\leq\nu and η′≤η\eta^{\prime}\leq\eta. Then by Leibniz rule and Multinomial Theorem, we can show that

∥fˇ𝒎1⋅S𝒎1⁡(⟨s⟩)∥K,μ,ν,η≤2|μ|+|ν|+|η||(𝒎1)2​𝟏𝒎1|⋅Cf⋅Cs.\lVert\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}\cdot\operatorname{S}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(\langle s\rangle)\rVert_{K,\mu,\nu,\eta}\leq\frac{2^{\lvert\mu\rvert+\lvert\nu\rvert+\lvert\eta\rvert}}{\lvert(\boldsymbol{m}_{1})^{2\boldsymbol{1}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}}\rvert}\cdot C_{f}\cdot C_{s}.

Then the fact that

∑𝒎1∈ℤn2|μ|+|ν|+|η||(𝒎1)2​𝟏𝒎1|<∞,\sum_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\frac{2^{\lvert\mu\rvert+\lvert\nu\rvert+\lvert\eta\rvert}}{\lvert(\boldsymbol{m}_{1})^{2\boldsymbol{1}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}}\rvert}<\infty,

implies that

∑𝒎1∈ℤn‖fˇ𝒎1⋅S𝒎1⁡(⟨s⟩)‖K,μ,ν,η<∞.\sum_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\left\lVert\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}\cdot\operatorname{S}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(\langle s\rangle)\right\rVert_{K,\mu,\nu,\eta}<\infty.

Thus, (5.8) converges absolutely (and hence unconditionally) in the Fréchet space 𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})). We can then well define an element Φf​(s)∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)\Phi_{f}(s)\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}) by setting ⟨Φf​(s)⟩\langle\Phi_{f}(s)\rangle equal to (5.8). The above estimate also proves the continuity of (5.10).

Now, fix f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}) again. We show that Φf\Phi_{f} defines a section of End⁡(𝐄ˇ)\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}}) by checking the criterion in Lemma 5.5. Clearly, the map ⟨Φf⟩:⟨s⟩↦⟨Φf​(s)⟩\langle\Phi_{f}\rangle:\langle s\rangle\mapsto\langle\Phi_{f}(s)\rangle is ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear. The continuity condition in Lemma 5.5 is already verified. Next, fix s∈𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))s\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})), g∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇn)g\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{n}) and qˇ∈ℤn\check{q}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}. We have

⟨Φf⟩​(g⋅s)=∑𝒎1∈ℤnfˇ𝒎1⋅S𝒎1⁡(g⋅s)=∑𝒎1∈ℤnfˇ𝒎1⋅S𝒎1⁡(g)⋅S𝒎1⁡(s)=g⋅⟨Φf⟩​(s),\langle\Phi_{f}\rangle(g\cdot s)=\sum_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}\cdot\operatorname{S}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(g\cdot s)=\sum_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}\cdot\operatorname{S}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(g)\cdot\operatorname{S}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(s)=g\cdot\langle\Phi_{f}\rangle(s),

since S𝒎1⁡(g)=g\operatorname{S}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(g)=g. On the other hand,

(⟨Φf⟩∘Sk​𝑯​qˇ)​(s)=\displaystyle(\langle\Phi_{f}\rangle\circ\operatorname{S}_{k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}})(s)= ∑𝒎1∈ℤnfˇ𝒎1⋅(S𝒎1∘Sk​𝑯​qˇ)​(s)\displaystyle\sum_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}\cdot(\operatorname{S}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}\circ\operatorname{S}_{k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}})(s)
=\displaystyle= ∑𝒎1∈ℤnSk​𝑯​qˇ⁡(fˇ𝒎1⋅S𝒎1⁡(s))=(Sk​𝑯​qˇ∘⟨Φf⟩)​(s),\displaystyle\sum_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\operatorname{S}_{k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}}(\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}\cdot\operatorname{S}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(s))=(\operatorname{S}_{k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}}\circ\langle\Phi_{f}\rangle)(s),

since Sk​𝑯​qˇ⁡(fˇ𝒎1)=fˇ𝒎1\operatorname{S}_{k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}}(\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}})=\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}. To conclude, by Lemma 5.5 and injectivity of the map Ψ→⟨Ψ⟩\Psi\to\langle\Psi\rangle , Φf∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ))\Phi_{f}\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}})). The proof is complete. ∎

5.2. Global construction of the mirror transform

Fix k∈ℤ>0k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0} and work in the setting of Subsection 4.3. Recall from Definition 5.7 that, for each index α\alpha and Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, we have constructed the family twisted Toeplitz operator

Φα,Ω:Ω0,∗​(Uα×𝕋2​n)→Ω0,∗​(Uα×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇα,Ω)).\Phi_{\alpha,\Omega}:\Omega^{0,*}(U_{\alpha}\times\mathbb{T}^{2n})\to\Omega^{0,*}\left(U_{\alpha}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega})\right).

On the other hand, we extend the family BKS pairing map BKSΩ,Ω′α\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}^{\alpha} (Definition 4.9) to a linear map

(5.11) Ω0,∗​(Uα×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇα,Ω′)→Ω0,∗​(Uα×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇα,Ω)\Omega^{0,*}\left(U_{\alpha}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega^{\prime}}\right)\to\Omega^{0,*}\left(U_{\alpha}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,\Omega}\right)

by its action on the (0,∗)(0,*)-forms of the form d​z¯αi1∧⋯∧d​z¯αild\overline{z}_{\alpha}^{i_{1}}\wedge\cdots\wedge d\overline{z}_{\alpha}^{i_{l}} as the identity map.

Proposition 5.8.

For all index α\alpha and Ω,Ω′∈ℍn\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, the following diagram commutes:

Proof.

Throughout the proof, we suppress the index α\alpha. By construction of the family twisted Toeplitz operators and the family BKS pairing maps, the statement is immediate on the differential form component. It therefore suffices to verify the claim on the function component.

By continuity and 𝒞∞​(U)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U)-linearity, it is enough to check that

(5.12) ΦΩ,f∘BKSΩ,Ω′=BKSΩ,Ω′∘ΦΩ′,f\Phi_{\Omega,f}\circ\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}=\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}\circ\Phi_{\Omega^{\prime},f}

for functions of the form
