f​(x,y)=e2​π​−1​m⋅uwith ​m∈ℤ2​n.f(x,y)=e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}m\cdot u}\quad\text{with }m\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n}.

It suffices to compare both sides on an orthonormal basis of each fiber. Fix (x,yˇ)(x,\check{y}) and consider the bases {σl​(x,⋅,yˇ)}l∈ℤk​𝑯n\{\sigma_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y})\}_{l\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}} and {σl′​(x,⋅,yˇ)}l∈ℤk​𝑯n\{\sigma^{\prime}_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y})\}_{l\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}} corresponding to Ω\Omega and Ω′\Omega^{\prime}, respectively, as given in Proposition 4.5. For simplicity, write σl=σl​(x,⋅,yˇ)\sigma_{l}=\sigma_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y}) and σl′=σl′​(x,⋅,yˇ)\sigma^{\prime}_{l}=\sigma^{\prime}_{l}(x,\,\cdot\,,\check{y}).

Fix l∈ℤk​𝑯nl\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}. Let l~∈ℤk​𝑯n\widetilde{l}\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n} be such that q~:=(k​𝑯)−1​(l+𝒎1−l~)∈ℤn\widetilde{q}:=(k\boldsymbol{H})^{-1}(l+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}-\widetilde{l})\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}. Let Δ\Delta and Δ′\Delta^{\prime} denote the differential operators defined in (5.1) corresponding to Ω\Omega and Ω′\Omega^{\prime}, respectively, and set

fΩ=exp⁡(1k​Δ)​fandfΩ′=exp⁡(1k​Δ′)​f.f_{\Omega}=\exp\left(\tfrac{1}{k}\Delta\right)f\quad\text{and}\quad f_{\Omega^{\prime}}=\exp\left(\tfrac{1}{k}\Delta^{\prime}\right)f.

By the definitions of ΦΩ,ΦΩ′\Phi_{\Omega},\Phi_{\Omega^{\prime}} and BKSΩ,Ω′\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}, together with Lemma 5.3, we compute

(ΦΩ,f∘BKSΩ,Ω′)​(σl′)=\displaystyle({\Phi_{\Omega,f}}\circ\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}})(\sigma^{\prime}_{l})= ΦΩ,f​(σl)=⟨fΩ​σl,σl~⟩​σl~,\displaystyle\Phi_{\Omega,f}(\sigma_{l})=\langle f_{\Omega}\sigma_{l},\sigma_{\widetilde{l}}\rangle\sigma_{\widetilde{l}},
(BKSΩ,Ω′∘ΦΩ′,f)​(σl′)=\displaystyle(\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}\circ\Phi_{\Omega^{\prime},f})(\sigma^{\prime}_{l})= BKSΩ,Ω′⁡(⟨fΩ′​σl′,σl~′⟩​σl~′)=⟨fΩ′​σl′,σl~′⟩​σl~.\displaystyle\operatorname{BKS}_{\Omega,\Omega^{\prime}}\left(\langle f_{\Omega^{\prime}}\sigma^{\prime}_{l},\sigma^{\prime}_{\widetilde{l}}\rangle\sigma^{\prime}_{\widetilde{l}}\right)=\langle f_{\Omega^{\prime}}\sigma^{\prime}_{l},\sigma^{\prime}_{\widetilde{l}}\rangle\sigma_{\widetilde{l}}.

The explicit formula in Lemma 5.3 shows that the coefficients ⟨fΩ​σl,σl~⟩\langle f_{\Omega}\sigma_{l},\sigma_{\widetilde{l}}\rangle and ⟨fΩ′​σl′,σl′′⟩\langle f_{\Omega^{\prime}}\sigma^{\prime}_{l},\sigma^{\prime}_{l^{\prime}}\rangle coincide. Hence the two compositions agree on a basis, and the diagram commutes. ∎

In what follows, for indices α,β\alpha,\beta with Uα​β≠∅U_{\alpha\beta}\neq\emptyset and Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, we denote by

Ad⁡(ϕα​β;Ω):Γ​(Uα​β×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇβ,Ω))→Γ​(Uα​β×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇα,Aα​β⋅Ω))\operatorname{Ad}(\phi_{\alpha\beta;\Omega}):\Gamma(U_{\alpha\beta}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\beta,\Omega}))\to\Gamma(U_{\alpha\beta}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,A_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega}))

the conjugation by ϕα​β;Ω\phi_{\alpha\beta;\Omega}. We extend this map to

Ad⁡(ϕα​β;Ω):Ω0,∗​(Uα​β×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇβ,Ω))→Ω0,∗​(Uα​β×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇα,Aα​β⋅Ω))\operatorname{Ad}(\phi_{\alpha\beta;\Omega}):\Omega^{0,*}(U_{\alpha\beta}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\beta,\Omega}))\to\Omega^{0,*}(U_{\alpha\beta}\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}}_{\alpha,A_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega}))

by combining conjugation on the endomorphism part with the tensorial transformation of

d​zˇ¯βi1∧⋯∧d​zˇ¯βild\overline{\check{z}}_{\beta}^{i_{1}}\wedge\cdots\wedge d\overline{\check{z}}_{\beta}^{i_{l}}

under the coordinate change (xβ,yβ)↦(xα,yα)(x_{\beta},y_{\beta})\mapsto(x_{\alpha},y_{\alpha}).

Proposition 5.9.

For all indices α,β\alpha,\beta with Uα​β≠∅U_{\alpha\beta}\neq\emptyset and Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, the following diagram commutes:

where the left vertical arrow is the transform under the coordinate change (xβ,yβ)↦(xα,yα)(x_{\beta},y_{\beta})\mapsto(x_{\alpha},y_{\alpha}).

Proof.

We first observe that the Dolbeault form components transform tensorially under the coordinate change associated with the constant transition matrix Aα​β∈GL⁡(2​n,ℤ)A_{\alpha\beta}\in\operatorname{GL}(2n,\mathbb{Z}):

d​z¯β=Aα​β​d​z¯αandd​zˇ¯β=Aα​β​d​zˇ¯α.d\overline{z}_{\beta}=A_{\alpha\beta}d\overline{z}_{\alpha}\quad\text{and}\quad d\overline{\check{z}}_{\beta}=A_{\alpha\beta}d\overline{\check{z}}_{\alpha}.

It follows that both vertical arrows act on the (0,∗)(0,*)-form part in the same way. Therefore, it suffices to verify the commutativity of the diagram on the level of functions.

Let f∈𝒞∞​(Uα​β×𝕋2​n)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U_{\alpha\beta}\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}), and denote by f′f^{\prime} its transform under the coordinate change. By 𝒞∞​(Uα​β)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U_{\alpha\beta})-linearity and continuity, we may assume that ff is a single Fourier mode:

f​(xβ,yβ)=e2​π​−1​mβ⋅uβ,mβ∈ℤ2​n.f(x_{\beta},y_{\beta})=e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}m_{\beta}\cdot u_{\beta}},\quad m_{\beta}\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n}.

Then f′f^{\prime} is again a single Fourier mode in the (xα,yα)(x_{\alpha},y_{\alpha})-coordinates. By construction of the family Toeplitz operators, together with the compatibility of the transition isomorphisms ϕα​β;Ω\phi_{\alpha\beta;\Omega} with the Hermitian structures, connections, and polarizations, we have

Tα,Ω′,f′∘ϕα​β;Ω=ϕα​β;Ω∘Tβ,Ω,f,T_{\alpha,\Omega^{\prime},f^{\prime}}\circ\phi_{\alpha\beta;\Omega}=\phi_{\alpha\beta;\Omega}\circ T_{\beta,\Omega,f},

where Ω′:=Aα​β⋅Ω\Omega^{\prime}:=A_{\alpha\beta}\cdot\Omega. Similar to the proof of Proposition 4.11, this follows from the naturality of the Toeplitz construction, since ϕα​β;Ω\phi_{\alpha\beta;\Omega} intertwines all geometric data entering the fiberwise definition.

Consider the operators Δ\Delta and Δ′\Delta^{\prime} defined in (5.1) corresponding to Ω\Omega and Ω′\Omega^{\prime}, respectively. The operator Δ\Delta transforms naturally under the coordinate change, so that Δ′\Delta^{\prime} is its pushforward. Consequently, f~′:=exp⁡(1k​Δ′)​f′\widetilde{f}^{\prime}:=\exp(\tfrac{1}{k}\Delta^{\prime})f^{\prime} is the transform of f~:=exp⁡(1k​Δ)​f\widetilde{f}:=\exp(\tfrac{1}{k}\Delta)f. In particular, both f~\widetilde{f} and f~′\widetilde{f}^{\prime} are scalar multiples of ff and f′f^{\prime} by the same nonzero constant. By definition,

Φβ,Ω,f=Tβ,Ω,f~andΦα,Ω′,f′=Tβ,Ω,f~′.\Phi_{\beta,\Omega,f}=T_{\beta,\Omega,\widetilde{f}}\quad\text{and}\quad\Phi_{\alpha,\Omega^{\prime},f^{\prime}}=T_{\beta,\Omega,\widetilde{f}^{\prime}}.

Combining this with the intertwining relation above, we obtain

Φα,Ω′,f′∘ϕα​β;Ω=ϕα​β;Ω∘Φβ,Ω,f.\Phi_{\alpha,\Omega^{\prime},f^{\prime}}\circ\phi_{\alpha\beta;\Omega}=\phi_{\alpha\beta;\Omega}\circ\Phi_{\beta,\Omega,f}.

Finally, extending by 𝒞∞​(Uα​β)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U_{\alpha\beta})-linearity and continuity to arbitrary functions ff, we conclude that the diagram commutes. ∎

Combining Propositions 5.8 and 5.9, we deduce that the maps Φα,Ω\Phi_{\alpha,\Omega} are compatible on overlaps and therefore glue to a family of maps

ΦU:Ω0,∗​(XU)→Ω0,∗​(XˇU,End⁡(Eˇ)),\Phi_{U}:\Omega^{0,*}(X_{U})\to\Omega^{0,*}(\check{X}_{U},\operatorname{End}(\check{E})),

for each open subset U⊂BU\subset B. Consequently, the collection {ΦU}\{\Phi_{U}\} defines a morphism of sheaves of 𝒞B∞\mathcal{C}_{B}^{\infty}-modules.

5.3. Proof of the mirror theorem

Finally, we prove our main result, Theorem 5.1. Since the statement is sheaf-theoretic over the base BB, it suffices to carry out the argument on an open subset U⊂BU\subset B of the type considered in Proposition 3.1. We also fix, once and for all, an auxiliary choice of Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}. The proof is organized into a sequence of lemmas.

Lemma 5.10.

The map Φ\Phi is a homomorphism of differential graded algebras:

Φ:(Ω0,∗​(U×𝕋2​n),⋆k−1,∂¯)→(Ω0,∗​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ)),∘,∂¯).\Phi:(\Omega^{0,*}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}),\star_{k^{-1}},\overline{\partial})\to(\Omega^{0,*}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}})),\circ,\overline{\partial}).
Proof.

By construction, Φ\Phi preserves degree. We first verify that it is an algebra homomorphism. It is immediate that Φ\Phi sends the constant one function to the identity map. Thus, it suffices to prove that

(5.13) Φf∘Φg=Φf⋆k−1g\Phi_{f}\circ\Phi_{g}=\Phi_{f\star_{k^{-1}}g}

for all f,g∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)f,g\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}). Indeed, once (5.13) holds for functions, the general case for forms α,β∈Ω0,∗​(U×𝕋2​n)\alpha,\beta\in\Omega^{0,*}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}) follows from the identity Φ​(d​z¯i)=d​zˇ¯i\Phi(d\overline{z}^{i})=d\overline{\check{z}}^{i}. By continuity and 𝒞∞​(U)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U)-(bi)linearity, it is enough to verify (5.13) on Fourier modes of the form

f​(x,y)=e2​π​−1​m⋅u,g​(x,y)=e2​π​−1​m′⋅u,f(x,y)=e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}m\cdot u},\quad g(x,y)=e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}m^{\prime}\cdot u},

with m,m′∈ℤ2​nm,m^{\prime}\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n}. In this case, (5.13) follows by a direct computation using (5.2) ((see also [4], Theorem 5).

It remains to to show that Φ\Phi intertwines the Dolbeault operators. For similar reasons, it suffices to check that for each 1≤i≤2​n1\leq i\leq 2n,

(5.14) Φ∂f∂zi=∇ˇ∂zˇi¯∘Φf−Φf∘∇ˇ∂zˇi¯\Phi_{\frac{\partial f}{\partial z^{i}}}=\check{\nabla}_{\partial_{\overline{\check{z}_{i}}}}\circ\Phi_{f}-\Phi_{f}\circ\check{\nabla}_{\partial_{\overline{\check{z}_{i}}}}

for functions of the form f​(x,y)=fm​(x)​e2​π​−1​m⋅uf(x,y)=f_{m}(x)e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}m\cdot u} with m∈ℤ2​nm\in\mathbb{Z}^{2n} and a function fm∈𝒞∞​(U)f_{m}\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U). Fix s∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)s\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}). By Lemma 5.4,

⟨Φf​s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1)=Fm​(x,𝒚ˇ1)⋅⟨s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1+𝒎1),\left\langle\Phi_{f}s\right\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=F_{m}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\cdot\langle s\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}),

where

Fm​(x,𝒚ˇ1):=fm​(x)​exp⁡(−2​π​−1k​𝒎2⋅𝑯−1​(𝒚ˇ1+12​𝒎1)).F_{m}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}):=f_{m}(x)\exp\left(-\tfrac{2\pi\sqrt{-1}}{k}\boldsymbol{m}_{2}\cdot\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}\left(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\right)\right).

Using the formulas established in the proof of Theorem 4.1, we compute for 1≤i≤n1\leq i\leq n:

⟨∇ˇ∂zˇi¯​Φf​s−Φf​∇ˇ∂zˇi¯​s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1)=∂Fm∂zˇi¯​(x,𝒚ˇ1)⋅⟨s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1+𝒎1),\left\langle\check{\nabla}_{\partial_{\overline{\check{z}_{i}}}}\Phi_{f}s-\Phi_{f}\check{\nabla}_{\partial_{\overline{\check{z}_{i}}}}s\right\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=\frac{\partial F_{m}}{\partial\overline{\check{z}_{i}}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\cdot\langle s\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}),

and

⟨∇ˇ∂zˇn+i¯​Φf​s−Φf​∇ˇ∂zˇn+i¯​s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1)\displaystyle\left\langle\check{\nabla}_{\partial_{\overline{\check{z}_{n+i}}}}\Phi_{f}s-\Phi_{f}\check{\nabla}_{\partial_{\overline{\check{z}_{n+i}}}}s\right\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})
=\displaystyle= (12​∂Fm∂xn+i​(x,𝒚ˇ1)−k​π​−1hi​(−−1​mik)⋅Fm​(x,𝒚ˇ1))⋅⟨s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1+𝒎1)\displaystyle\left(\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial F_{m}}{\partial x_{n+i}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})-\frac{k\pi\sqrt{-1}}{h_{i}}\left(-\sqrt{-1}\frac{m_{i}}{k}\right)\cdot F_{m}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\right)\cdot\langle s\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1})
=\displaystyle= (12​∂Fm∂xn+i​(x,𝒚ˇ1)−π​mihi⋅Fm​(x,𝒚ˇ1))⋅⟨s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1+𝒎1).\displaystyle\left(\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial F_{m}}{\partial x_{n+i}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})-\frac{\pi m_{i}}{h_{i}}\cdot F_{m}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\right)\cdot\langle s\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}).

A direct computation using (3.6) and (3.7) shows that for j=ij=i and j=n+ij=n+i,

⟨Φ∂z¯jf​s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1)=⟨∇ˇ∂zˇj¯​Φf​s−Φf​∇ˇ∂zˇj¯​s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1).\left\langle\Phi_{\partial_{\overline{z}^{j}}f}s\right\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=\left\langle\check{\nabla}_{\partial_{\overline{\check{z}_{j}}}}\Phi_{f}s-\Phi_{f}\check{\nabla}_{\partial_{\overline{\check{z}_{j}}}}s\right\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}).

Therefore, Φ\Phi is a morphism of differential graded algebras. ∎

Lemma 5.11.

The map Φ:Ω0,∗​(U×𝕋2​n)→Ω0,∗​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ))\Phi:\Omega^{0,*}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n})\to\Omega^{0,*}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}})) is injective.

Proof.

It suffices to prove injectivity on functions. Let f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}) and assume Φf=0\Phi_{f}=0. We show that f=0f=0. By (5.5), it is enough to prove that fˇ𝒎1=0\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}=0 for all 𝒎1∈ℤn\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}, where fˇ𝒎1\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}} is defined in (5.6).

Fix 𝒎1∈ℤn\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n} and (x,𝒚ˇ1)∈U×ℝˇn(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\in U\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}. Choose a section s∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,𝐄ˇ)s\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\mathbf{\check{E}}) such that its associated function ⟨s⟩∈𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))\langle s\rangle\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) satisfies ⟨s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1+𝒎1)=1\langle s\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1})=1 and ⟨s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1+qˇ)=0\langle s\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\check{q})=0 for all qˇ∈ℤn\check{q}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n} with qˇ≠𝒎1\check{q}\neq\boldsymbol{m}_{1}. Then Proposition 5.6 implies

fˇ𝒎1​(x,𝒚ˇ1)=⟨Φf​(s)⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1)=0.\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=\langle\Phi_{f}(s)\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=0.

Since (x,𝒚ˇ1)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}) and 𝒎1\boldsymbol{m}_{1} were arbitrary, (5.5) yields f=0f=0. ∎

Lemma 5.12.

The map Φ:Ω0,∗​(U×𝕋2​n)→Ω0,∗​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ))\Phi:\Omega^{0,*}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n})\to\Omega^{0,*}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}})) is surjective.

Proof.

It suffices to prove surjectivity on functions. Fix Ψ∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ))\Psi\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}})). For each 𝒎1∈ℤn\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}, define a function fˇ𝒎1:U×ℝˇn→ℂ\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}:U\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}\to\mathbb{C} by

(5.15) fˇ𝒎1​(x,𝒚ˇ1):=(⟨Ψ⟩​s)​(x,𝒚ˇ1),\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}):=(\langle\Psi\rangle s)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}),

where s∈𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))s\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) is any function satisfying ⟨s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1+𝒎1)=1\langle s\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1})=1 and ⟨s⟩​(x,𝒚ˇ1+qˇ)=0\langle s\rangle(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\check{q})=0 for all qˇ∈ℤn\check{q}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n} with qˇ≠𝒎1\check{q}\neq\boldsymbol{m}_{1}. By Lemma 5.13, fˇ𝒎1\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}} is well defined.

We first show that fˇ𝒎1\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}} is smooth. Fix (x,𝒚ˇ1)∈U×ℝˇn(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\in U\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}. Choose a function s∈𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))s\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) which equals 11 on a sufficiently small neighbourhood of (x,𝒚ˇ1+𝒎1)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}) and vanishes away from a slightly larger neighbourhood. Then on a sufficiently small neighbourhood of (x,𝒚ˇ1)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}),

fˇ𝒎1=⟨Ψ⟩​(s).\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}=\langle\Psi\rangle(s).

Since the right-hand side is smooth at (x,𝒚ˇ1)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}), so is fˇ𝒎1\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}.

Next, fˇ𝒎1\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}} is invariant under the lattice k​𝑯​ℤnk\boldsymbol{H}\mathbb{Z}^{n}. Indeed, fix (x,𝒚ˇ1)∈U×ℝˇn(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\in U\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n} and qˇ∈ℤn\check{q}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}. Choosing s∈𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))s\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) which equals 11 at (x,𝒚ˇ1+k​𝑯​qˇ+𝒎1)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}) and vanishes away from a sufficiently small neighbourhood of (x,𝒚ˇ1+k​𝑯​qˇ+𝒎1)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1}), we obtain

fˇ𝒎1​(x,𝒚ˇ1+k​𝑯​qˇ)=(Sk​𝑯​qˇ∘⟨Ψ⟩)​(s)​(x,𝒚ˇ1)=(⟨Ψ⟩∘Sk​𝑯​qˇ)​(s)​(x,𝒚ˇ1)=fˇ𝒎1​(x,𝒚ˇ1)\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q})=(\operatorname{S}_{k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}}\circ\langle\Psi\rangle)(s)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=(\langle\Psi\rangle\circ\operatorname{S}_{k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}})(s)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})

by Lemma 5.5, where Sk​𝑯​qˇ\operatorname{S}_{k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}} denotes the shift operator defined in (5.4). So fˇ𝒎1\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}} descends to a smooth function on U×𝕋ˇk​𝑯nU\times\check{\mathbb{T}}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}, denoted by the same symbol.

Moreover, Lemma 5.14, together with the finiteness of ℤk​𝑯n\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n} and the k​𝑯​ℤnk\boldsymbol{H}\mathbb{Z}^{n}-invariance of fˇ\check{f}, implies that for all compact subset K⊂UK\subset U and multi-indices μ∈ℕ2​n\mu\in\mathbb{N}^{2n} and ν,η∈ℕn\nu,\eta\in\mathbb{N}^{n}, ∥fˇ∥K,μ,ν,η<∞\lVert\check{f}\rVert_{K,\mu,\nu,\eta}<\infty. Consequently, the Fourier series

f​(x,y):=∑𝒎1∈ℤnfˇ𝒎1​(x,−k​𝑯​𝒖2−12​𝒎1)⋅e2​π​−1​𝒎1⋅𝒖1f(x,y):=\sum_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\check{f}_{\boldsymbol{m}_{1}}(x,-k\boldsymbol{H}\boldsymbol{u}^{2}-\tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{m}_{1})\cdot e^{2\pi\sqrt{-1}\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^{1}}

defines a smooth function f∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋2​n)f\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{T}^{2n}). By construction, the associated section Φf\Phi_{f} coincides with Ψ\Psi. Hence Φ\Phi is surjective. ∎

Lemma 5.13 (Lattice dependence).

Let Ψ∈Γ​(U×𝕋ˇ2​n,End⁡(𝐄ˇ))\Psi\in\Gamma(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{2n},\operatorname{End}(\mathbf{\check{E}})) and (x,𝐲ˇ1)∈U×ℝˇn(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\in U\times\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}. If s∈𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))s\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) satisfies s​(x,𝐲ˇ1+𝐦1)=0s(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+\boldsymbol{m}_{1})=0 for all 𝐦1∈ℤn\boldsymbol{m}_{1}\in\mathbb{Z}^{n}, then (⟨Ψ⟩​s)​(x,𝐲ˇ1)=0(\langle\Psi\rangle s)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=0.

Proof.

Approximate ss in 𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) by compactly supported functions with the same vanishing property. By continuity, it suffices to treat the compactly supported case.

In this case, write s=∑i=1rfi⋅sis=\sum_{i=1}^{r}f_{i}\cdot s_{i}, where each sis_{i} is compactly supported (and hence in 𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n}))) and each fi∈𝒞∞​(U×𝕋ˇn)f_{i}\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\check{\mathbb{T}}^{n}) vanishes at (x,𝒚ˇ1)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}). By Lemma 5.5,

(⟨Ψ⟩​s)​(x,𝒚ˇ1)=∑i=1rfi​(x,𝒚ˇ1)⋅(⟨Ψ⟩​si)​(x,𝒚ˇ1)=0.(\langle\Psi\rangle s)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=\sum_{i=1}^{r}f_{i}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})\cdot(\langle\Psi\rangle s_{i})(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1})=0.

∎

Lemma 5.14 (Fiberwise Schwartz estimate).

Let K⊂UK\subset U be a compact subset, and μ∈ℕ2​n\mu\in\mathbb{N}^{2n} and ν,η∈ℕn\nu,\eta\in\mathbb{N}^{n} be multi-indices. Let 𝐲ˇ1∈ℝˇn\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}\in\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n} and l∈ℤk​𝐇nl\in\mathbb{Z}_{k\boldsymbol{H}}^{n}. Then there exists ε>0\varepsilon>0 such that

(5.16) sup(x,𝒚ˇ′1,qˇ)∈K×B¯​(𝒚ˇ1,ε)×ℤn|(l+k​𝑯​qˇ)η⋅∂|μ|+|ν|fˇl+k​𝑯​qˇ∂xμ​∂(𝒚ˇ′1)ν​(x,𝒚ˇ′1)|<∞,\sup_{(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}^{\prime}}_{1},\check{q})\in K\times\overline{\operatorname{B}}(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1},\varepsilon)\times\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\left\lvert(l+k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q})^{\eta}\cdot\frac{\partial^{\lvert\mu\rvert+\lvert\nu\rvert}\check{f}_{l+k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}}}{\partial x^{\mu}\partial(\boldsymbol{\check{y}^{\prime}}_{1})^{\nu}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}^{\prime}}_{1})\right\rvert<\infty,

where B¯​(𝐲ˇ1,ε)\overline{\operatorname{B}}(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1},\varepsilon) denotes the closed ball in ℝˇn\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n} of radius ε\varepsilon centred at 𝐲ˇ1\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}.

Proof.

Fix ε=14\varepsilon=\tfrac{1}{4} and choose a fiberwise cutoff function s∈𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝˇn))s\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\check{\mathbb{R}}^{n})) such that

s≡1on ​U×B¯​(𝒚ˇ1+l,ε)andsupp⁡(s)⊂U×B¯​(𝒚ˇ1+l,2​ε).s\equiv 1\quad\text{on }U\times\overline{\operatorname{B}}(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+l,\varepsilon)\quad\text{and}\quad\operatorname{supp}(s)\subset U\times\overline{\operatorname{B}}(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1}+l,2\varepsilon).

Then for all (x,𝒚ˇ′1,qˇ)∈K×B¯​(𝒚ˇ1,ε)×ℤn(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}^{\prime}}_{1},\check{q})\in K\times\overline{\operatorname{B}}(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1},\varepsilon)\times\mathbb{Z}^{n},

(⟨Ψ⟩​s)​(x,𝒚ˇ′1−k​𝑯​qˇ)=fˇl+k​𝑯​qˇ​(x,𝒚ˇ′1−k​𝑯​qˇ)=fˇl+k​𝑯​qˇ​(x,𝒚ˇ′1).(\langle\Psi\rangle s)(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}^{\prime}}_{1}-k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q})=\check{f}_{l+k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}^{\prime}}_{1}-k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q})=\check{f}_{l+k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}^{\prime}}_{1}).

Hence the seminorm bound ∥⟨Ψ⟩​s∥K,μ,ν,η<∞\lVert\langle\Psi\rangle s\rVert_{K,\mu,\nu,\eta}<\infty for ⟨Ψ⟩​s\langle\Psi\rangle s implies

sup(x,𝒚ˇ′1,qˇ)∈K×B¯​(𝒚ˇ1,ε)×ℤn|(𝒚ˇ′1−k​𝑯​qˇ)η⋅∂|μ|+|ν|fˇl+k​𝑯​qˇ∂xμ​∂(𝒚ˇ′1)ν​(x,𝒚ˇ′1)|<∞.\sup_{(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}^{\prime}}_{1},\check{q})\in K\times\overline{\operatorname{B}}(\boldsymbol{\check{y}}_{1},\varepsilon)\times\mathbb{Z}^{n}}\left\lvert(\boldsymbol{\check{y}^{\prime}}_{1}-k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q})^{\eta}\cdot\frac{\partial^{\lvert\mu\rvert+\lvert\nu\rvert}\check{f}_{l+k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q}}}{\partial x^{\mu}\partial(\boldsymbol{\check{y}^{\prime}}_{1})^{\nu}}(x,\boldsymbol{\check{y}^{\prime}}_{1})\right\rvert<\infty.

Since 𝒚ˇ′1\boldsymbol{\check{y}^{\prime}}_{1} ranges in a bounded set, the weights (𝒚ˇ′1−k​𝑯​qˇ)η(\boldsymbol{\check{y}^{\prime}}_{1}-k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q})^{\eta} are comparable to (l+k​𝑯​qˇ)η(l+k\boldsymbol{H}\check{q})^{\eta} outside a finite subset of ℤn\mathbb{Z}^{n}. The desired estimate follows by combining this comparison with compactness. ∎

Proof of Theorem 5.1.

By construction, the collection {ΦU(k)}\{\Phi_{U}^{(k)}\} defines a morphism of sheaves of 𝒞B∞\mathcal{C}_{B}^{\infty}-modules. For any open subset U⊂BU\subset B, Lemma 5.10 ensures that ΦU(k)\Phi_{U}^{(k)} is a morphism of differential graded algebras. Since ΦU(k)\Phi_{U}^{(k)} is bijective (Lemmas 5.11 and 5.12), it constitutes an isomorphism of sheaves of DGAs. This isomorphism naturally induces the claimed isomorphism on the resulting cohomology groups, which completes the proof. ∎

Appendix A Function Spaces with Fiberwise Rapid Decay

Let (x1,…,xn)(x^{1},...,x^{n}) be the standard coordinates on ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n}, and let U⊂ℝnU\subset\mathbb{R}^{n} be an open subset. Let (y1,…,ym)(y^{1},...,y^{m}) denote the standard coordinate on ℝm\mathbb{R}^{m}, and write 𝒮​(ℝm)\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^{m}) for the Schwartz space on ℝm\mathbb{R}^{m}. We define

𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝm))\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^{m}))

to be the space of smooth ℂ\mathbb{C}-valued functions s∈𝒞∞​(U×ℝm)s\in\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U\times\mathbb{R}^{m}) satisfying the following conditions: for all compact subset K⊂UK\subset U and multi-indices μ∈ℕn\mu\in\mathbb{N}^{n} and ν,η∈ℕm\nu,\eta\in\mathbb{N}^{m},

∥s∥K,μ,ν,η:=sup(x,y)∈K×ℝm|yη⋅∂|μ|+|ν|s∂xμ​∂yν​(x,y)|<∞.\lVert s\rVert_{K,\mu,\nu,\eta}:=\sup_{(x,y)\in K\times\mathbb{R}^{m}}\left\lvert y^{\eta}\cdot\frac{\partial^{\lvert\mu\rvert+\lvert\nu\rvert}s}{\partial x^{\mu}\partial y^{\nu}}(x,y)\right\rvert<\infty.

We refer to 𝒞∞​(U,𝒮​(ℝm))\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U,\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^{m})) as the space of smooth ℂ\mathbb{C}-valued functions on U×ℝmU\times\mathbb{R}^{m} that are rapidly decreasing in the ℝm\mathbb{R}^{m}-variables. We equip this space with the locally convex topology generated by the seminorms ∥⋅∥K,μ,ν,η\lVert\,\cdot\,\rVert_{K,\mu,\nu,\eta}.

Remark A.1.

This space can be identified with the completed projective tensor product 𝒞∞​(U)​⊗^π​𝒮​(ℝm)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U)\widehat{\otimes}_{\pi}\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^{m}), where 𝒞∞​(U)\mathcal{C}^{\infty}(U) is equipped with the usual compact-open 𝒞∞\mathcal{C}^{\infty}-topology and 𝒮​(ℝm)\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^{m}) is equipped with its Schwartz topology.

Appendix B Twisted integral symplectic groups

Let n∈ℤn\in\mathbb{Z}, InI_{n} denote the n×nn\times n identity matrix and

(B.1) Jn:=(0−InIn0).J_{n}:=\begin{pmatrix}0&-I_{n}\\ I_{n}&0\end{pmatrix}.

Recall that the real symplectic group Sp⁡(2​n,ℝ)={A∈GL⁡(2​n,ℝ):A​Jn​AT=Jn}\operatorname{Sp}(2n,\mathbb{R})=\left\{A\in\operatorname{GL}(2n,\mathbb{R}):AJ_{n}A^{T}=J_{n}\right\} has a canonical action on the Segal upper half space ℍn\mathbb{H}_{n}, which is given by

(B.2) A⋅Ω=(𝑨11​Ω+𝑨12)​(𝑨21​Ω+𝑨22)−1,where ​A=(𝑨11𝑨12𝑨21𝑨22).A\cdot\Omega=(\boldsymbol{A}_{1}^{1}\Omega+\boldsymbol{A}_{1}^{2})(\boldsymbol{A}_{2}^{1}\Omega+\boldsymbol{A}_{2}^{2})^{-1},\quad\text{where }A=\begin{pmatrix}\boldsymbol{A}_{1}^{1}&\boldsymbol{A}_{1}^{2}\\ \boldsymbol{A}_{2}^{1}&\boldsymbol{A}_{2}^{2}\end{pmatrix}.

We are interested in a variant of the standard integral symplectic group Sp⁡(2​n,ℤ)<Sp⁡(2​n,ℝ)\operatorname{Sp}(2n,\mathbb{Z})<\operatorname{Sp}(2n,\mathbb{R}), determined by a finite sequence h1,…,hn∈ℤ>0h_{1},...,h_{n}\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0} with h1​∣h2∣​⋯∣hnh_{1}\mid h_{2}\mid\cdots\mid h_{n} (for instance, h1,…,hnh_{1},...,h_{n} are invariant factors of ℬcc\mathcal{B}_{\operatorname{cc}}), and in the induced action of it on ℍn\mathbb{H}_{n} through a particular embedding into Sp⁡(2​n,ℝ)\operatorname{Sp}(2n,\mathbb{R}). We first define 𝑯=diag⁡(h1,…,hn)\boldsymbol{H}=\operatorname{diag}(h_{1},...,h_{n}) and HH as in (3.10). The group of our interest is as follows:

Sp⁡(ℤ2​n,H):={A∈GL⁡(2​n,ℤ):A​H​AT=H}.\operatorname{Sp}(\mathbb{Z}^{2n},H):=\left\{A\in\operatorname{GL}(2n,\mathbb{Z}):AHA^{T}=H\right\}.

This group has an automorphism A↦A−TA\mapsto A^{-T}. There is then a group embedding

(B.3) Sp⁡(ℤ2​n,H)↪Sp⁡(2​n,ℝ),A↦(𝑯−100In)​A−T​(𝑯00In),\operatorname{Sp}(\mathbb{Z}^{2n},H)\hookrightarrow\operatorname{Sp}(2n,\mathbb{R}),\quad A\mapsto\begin{pmatrix}\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}&0\\ 0&I_{n}\end{pmatrix}A^{-T}\begin{pmatrix}\boldsymbol{H}&0\\ 0&I_{n}\end{pmatrix},

which, together with (B.2), induces an action

(B.4) Sp⁡(ℤ2​n,H)×ℍn→ℍn,(A,Ω)↦A⋅Ω.\operatorname{Sp}(\mathbb{Z}^{2n},H)\times\mathbb{H}_{n}\to\mathbb{H}_{n},\quad(A,\Omega)\mapsto A\cdot\Omega.

This action satisfies the following property. If

y=(y1,…,y2​n)∈ℝ2​n,A=(𝑨11𝑨12𝑨21𝑨22)∈Sp⁡(ℤ2​n,H)andy′=A−T​y,y=(y^{1},...,y^{2n})\in\mathbb{R}^{2n},\quad A=\begin{pmatrix}\boldsymbol{A}_{1}^{1}&\boldsymbol{A}_{1}^{2}\\ \boldsymbol{A}_{2}^{1}&\boldsymbol{A}_{2}^{2}\end{pmatrix}\in\operatorname{Sp}(\mathbb{Z}^{2n},H)\quad\text{and}\quad y^{\prime}=A^{-T}y,

then for all Ω∈ℍn\Omega\in\mathbb{H}_{n}, letting Ω′=A⋅Ω\Omega^{\prime}=A\cdot\Omega,

(B.5) (𝒚′)1−𝑯−1​Ω′​(𝒚′)2=(𝑨11−𝑯−1​Ω​𝑨12)−1​(𝒚1−𝑯−1​Ω​𝒚2).(\boldsymbol{y}^{\prime})^{1}-\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}\Omega^{\prime}(\boldsymbol{y}^{\prime})^{2}=(\boldsymbol{A}_{1}^{1}-\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}\Omega\boldsymbol{A}_{1}^{2})^{-1}(\boldsymbol{y}^{1}-\boldsymbol{H}^{-1}\Omega\boldsymbol{y}^{2}).

Experimental support, please view the build logs for errors. Generated by L A T E xml .

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.
